The defilement of the sanctuary is the most extreme form of covenant violation — attacking the holy space itself rather than merely violating covenant ethics in the secular sphere.
7.31: 'They have built the shrines of Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom to burn their sons and daughters in the fire — something I never commanded, and something that never even entered my mind' — the Topheth (tōpet, possibly meaning fireplace/hearth) was the cultic installation in the Valley of Ben Hinnom (Gehenna) south of Jerusalem where child sacrifice to Molech was practiced (2 Kings 23:10; Isa 30:33). The practice may have been Phoenician in origin, brought to Judah through the influence of Phoenician-Sidonian culture during Ahab's era (the marriage to Jezebel) and revived under Manasseh. The fire-sacrifice of children is labeled as something YHWH never commanded and never imagined — the strongest possible statement that the practice is not a legitimate expression of covenant worship.
7.32: 'Therefore, the days are coming ... when this place will no longer be called Topheth or the Valley of Ben Hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter' — the renaming is a judgment oracle: the place of intentional child-burning will become the place of mass unintentional death. 'For they will bury the dead in Topheth until there is no room left' — the irony inverts the geography of horror: the valley where Israel burned children will become the valley where Israel buries war dead. The Valley of Ben Hinnom (Gehenna) later became Jerusalem's refuse dump, burning perpetually, and the word Gehenna (from the Greek transliteration of gēʾ hinnōm) became the NT's standard term for the place of final judgment (Matt 5:22,29; 10:28; 18:9).
7.33: 'The corpses of this people will become food for the birds of the sky and the wild animals of the earth, and no one will drive them away' — the unburied body is the ultimate disgrace in the ANE: proper burial was the final act of family and community obligation, and denial of burial was a curse reserved for enemies (1 Kings 21:24; 2 Kings 9:10). The 'no one to drive them away' detail is the absence of all social function: there are no surviving family members to protect the bodies, no community to maintain the minimum of human dignity in death.
7.34: 'I will bring to an end the sounds of joy and gladness, the voices of the bridegroom and the bride, in the cities of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem — for the land will become a wasteland' — the stopping of wedding sounds is the quintessential image of social death in the ancient world: marriage, procreation, and social celebration are the most basic human activities that continue even in adverse conditions. When weddings stop, the community has ceased to function. Jeremiah uses this image at 16:9 and 25:10; Revelation 18:23 applies the same image to the fall of Babylon. The 'voice of the bridegroom and bride' appears positively in 33:11 as a sign of restoration.

8.1: 'At that time ... the bones of the kings of Judah, the bones of its officials, the bones of the priests, the bones of the prophets, and the bones of Jerusalem's inhabitants will be brought out of their graves' — the desecration of the royal tombs is one of the most extreme forms of posthumous judgment imaginable in the ANE. Kings were buried with honor in dynastic tombs (the tombs of David: 1 Kings 2:10); disruption of royal burials was practiced by invaders as a sign of complete domination. The specific categories — kings, officials, priests, prophets, inhabitants — correspond exactly to the leadership classes indicted in 2:8 and 7:26. The judgment is comprehensive across all social strata.
8.2: 'They will be exposed before the sun, the moon, and all the host of heaven — which this people loved, served, followed, consulted, and worshipped' — the five-verb indictment for astral worship is the most concentrated description of star-cult devotion in the OT. The five verbs (loved, served, followed, consulted, worshipped) describe different aspects of the relationship with astral deities: emotional attachment (ʾāhab), active service (ʿābad), lifestyle orientation (hālak ʾaḥărê), inquiry (dāraš), and prostration (šāḥāh). The ironic punishment is that the bones will be exposed before the very celestial objects the people worshipped — but now as refuse, not as devotees.
8.3: 'And all the survivors of this evil family who remain in all the places where I have driven them will prefer death to life' — the desire for death over life reverses the life-orientation of the covenant: YHWH offered 'life and prosperity' (Deut 30:15), and the covenant people are now choosing their opposites. The survivors in the diaspora will have witnessed such devastation that continued existence offers nothing — the annihilation of their entire social world (temple, land, community, institutions) makes death preferable to the meaningless survival of a covenant people without their covenant context.
8.4: 'When people fall, don't they get back up? When someone turns away, don't they come back?' — the wisdom sayings contrast natural human resilience with Israel's theological stubbornness. When a person stumbles (yippōl), they naturally rise (yāqûm); when they turn away (yāšûb), they naturally return (yāšûb). The wordplay is intentional: the 'returning' that characterizes normal human behavior is precisely what Israel refuses to do in its covenant context. Repentance (šûbāh) is treated as less demanding than the automatic physical response to falling — and yet Israel will not do it.
8.5: 'Then why has this people — Jerusalem — turned away in perpetual backsliding? They cling to deception and refuse to come back' — mĕšûbāh niṣṣaḥat (perpetual/eternal backsliding) is Jeremiah's diagnostic: the turning away has become a permanent orientation rather than a temporary failure. 'Clinging to deception' (heḥĕzîqû bammirmāh) uses the same verb ḥāzaq (to hold fast/cling) used for Israel's covenant loyalty to YHWH in positive contexts (Deut 10:20; Ruth 1:14) — the irony is that the clinging-loyalty that should be directed toward YHWH is now directed toward the deceptions that substitute for him.
8.6: 'No one repents of his wickedness and asks, What have I done?' — the absence of the diagnostic question 'What have I done?' is the sign of moral self-awareness destroyed. The horse-plunging-into-battle image is vivid: warhorses trained for cavalry combat charge without deliberation, driven by training and adrenaline. Israel's moral behavior has become similarly automatic and unreflective — each person charges ahead on their own course (šāqap bĕmĕrûṣātô) without pausing to assess direction or destination. The absence of moral deliberation is itself a form of moral pathology.
8.7: 'Even the stork in the sky knows when to migrate; the turtledove, the swallow, and the crane observe the season of their return — but my people do not know the requirements of the LORD' — the migratory bird comparison is a form of shame rhetoric: the birds follow their divinely-embedded instincts with precision; Israel ignores its divinely-given covenant. The four birds listed were familiar to the Levantine climate as seasonal migrants. Mišpāṭ Yhwh (the requirements/ordinances of the LORD) refers to the covenant norms that the Torah made explicit — Israel's failure is not lack of information but lack of attentiveness to what the birds instinctively know: the right season for the right response.
8.8: 'How can you claim, We are wise, and we have the law of the LORD? But in fact the lying pen of the scribes has turned it into a lie' — the 'lying pen of the scribes' (ʿēṭ šeqer sōpĕrîm) is a critique of the scribal interpretation tradition that was producing Torah commentary and legal rulings in Jeremiah's era. Whether this refers to literal forgery (scholars debate) or to interpretive distortion that created legal loopholes from Torah requirements, the indictment is that the written law (tôrat Yhwh) was being undermined by the very specialists who claimed to preserve and transmit it. The critique anticipates Jesus's conflicts with scribal tradition (Matt 15:6: 'you have made void the word of God by your tradition').
8.9: 'The wise will be disgraced; they will be terrified and caught. They have rejected the word of the LORD — so what wisdom is left in them?' — the ḥăkāmîm (wise men/sages) were a distinct professional class in ancient Israel, distinct from the priests and prophets (cf. Jer 18:18: 'the law shall not perish from the priest, nor counsel from the wise, nor the word from the prophet'). Wisdom in the OT begins with the fear of the LORD (Prov 1:7); those who reject the LORD's word have destroyed the foundation of the very wisdom they claim. The categorical question 'what wisdom is left in them?' rhetorically answers itself: none.
8.10: 'Therefore I will give their wives to other men and their fields to new owners, because from the least to the greatest all are consumed by greed; from prophet to priest, all deal dishonestly' — the loss of wives and fields represents the total dispossession of the household unit — the fundamental social structure of Israelite life. The universal greedy indictment (miqqāṭōn wĕʿad-gādôl kullō bōṣēaʿ bāṣaʿ) from least to greatest covers the entire social spectrum. The prophet-to-priest inclusion of religious leaders in the indictment mirrors the comprehensive leadership failure in 2:8 and 5:31.
8.11: 'They dress the wound of my people as though it were minor, saying, Peace, peace, when there is no peace' — the 'peace, peace' (šālôm šālôm) false promise is one of the most memorable phrases in Jeremiah, repeated at 6:14 and echoed throughout the book's conflict with false prophets. Šālôm as covenant completeness — the comprehensive well-being that YHWH promises to the obedient community — has been misappropriated as a political-religious slogan assuring Jerusalem that no judgment is coming. The wound metaphor (šeber, the fracture/break of my people) suggests a structural injury; the false prophets are applying ointment to what requires surgery.
8.12: 'Were they ashamed when they committed these detestable acts? No, they felt no shame at all; they did not even know how to blush' — the incapacity for shame (hikkālēm lōʾ yēdāʿû) is Jeremiah's diagnosis of the social-psychological consequence of long-established sin: the moral feedback mechanism (blushing as the involuntary sign of conscience) has been destroyed by habituation to wrong. The parallel at 6:15 connects this shame-incapacity to the confident leadership of prophets and priests who will fall in the time of punishment.
8.13: 'I will take away their harvest, declares the LORD: no grapes on the vine, no figs on the fig tree, even the leaves will wither' — the agricultural curse of stripped vines and barren fig trees reverses the covenant blessing symbols. The vine and fig tree together represent the standard ANE image of peaceful agricultural plenty (1 Kings 4:25; Mic 4:4; Zech 3:10); their barrenness is the visible sign of covenant-curse. 'What I gave them will be stripped away' (wāʾettēn lāhem yaʿabĕrûm, lit. 'those I gave to them will pass away') — the gifts of the land return to YHWH when the land-lease conditions are violated.
8.14: 'Why are we sitting here? Come, let us flee to the fortified cities and die there — for the LORD our God has condemned us and has given us poisoned water to drink, because we have sinned against the LORD' — the community voice of defeat anticipates the siege of Jerusalem. The 'sitting here' implies paralysis in the face of advancing Babylonian forces; the flight to fortified cities is the desperate last resort. 'Condemned us' (hĕdimmānû) and 'poisoned water' (mê rōʾš, water of gall/poison) are the language of the covenant curse enacted — the people recognize that their suffering is covenantally deserved, a recognition that does not produce repentance but only fatalistic acceptance.
8.15: 'We hoped for peace, but no good has come; we waited for a time of healing, but instead there is only terror' — the reversal of the false prophets' promise: the community hoped for what the false prophets promised and received the opposite. This is the experiential confirmation of Jeremiah's charge against the 'peace, peace' slogan — the people who trusted the false prophets discover, in the moment of judgment, that the promise was empty. Rĕpāʾût (healing) is the covenant-restoration term; the 'time of healing' that has not come will be promised genuinely in 30:17 and 33:6.
8.16: 'From Dan the snorting of their warhorses is heard; the whole land shakes at the sound of their stallions' neighing' — Dan was the northernmost Israelite city (modern Tel Dan), the first to be reached by an invasion from the north (the Babylonian route from Mesopotamia via the Fertile Crescent). The battle sounds starting from Dan function as an alarm that reaches the whole land progressively. The horse imagery for the Babylonian cavalry is vivid — war chariots and cavalry were the decisive military technology of the Neo-Babylonian army.
8.17: 'For I am about to send poisonous snakes among you — vipers that cannot be charmed — and they will bite you, declares the LORD' — the snake-that-cannot-be-charmed image builds on the 'poisoned water' (rōʾš = poison/gall) theme of v. 14. Snake-charming was a recognized practice in the ancient Near East (cf. Ps 58:4-5; Eccl 10:11); the vipers that resist charming are invaders whose advance cannot be neutralized by any means — neither diplomatic negotiation nor magical protection. The image connects to Numbers 21:6 (YHWH sending fiery serpents against Israel in the wilderness) — the same divine agent who once used serpents to discipline Israel uses them again.
8.18: 'My sorrow is beyond healing; my heart is faint within me' — the shift from divine oracle to prophetic lament is sudden and significant. Jeremiah speaks in his own voice about his personal grief over the coming judgment he has been proclaiming. The 'sorrow beyond healing' (yaʾgôn ʿālay libbî dāwwāy) reflects the prophet's unique position: he knows the judgment is justified and coming, but he is not emotionally detached from the people who will suffer it. Jeremiah is often described as 'the weeping prophet' because of passages like this, 9:1, and the Lamentations attributed to him.
8.19: 'Listen — the cry of my people from a distant land: Is the LORD not in Zion? Is her King no longer there?' — the distant-land cry is the voice of exile before it has happened: Jeremiah hears in prophetic vision the anguished questions of a dispersed people asking whether the exile has voided the Zion covenant. 'Is her King no longer there?' — ʾên malkāh bāh — directly challenges the Davidic kingship theology. YHWH's inserted counter-question ('Why have they provoked me with their carved idols and worthless foreign gods?') answers the cry with the reason for the divine absence: idolatry caused the very abandonment the people now lament.
8.20: 'The harvest is over, the summer has ended — and we are still not saved' — this is among the most poignant lines in Jeremiah: the agricultural seasons have passed, the opportunities for action have run out, and salvation has not come. In Palestinian agriculture, the grain harvest (spring) and the summer fruit harvest (August-September) together constituted the year's productive capacity; when both were over, the year was finished. The seasonal metaphor expresses a theological reality: the time for repentance and covenant restoration has closed, and the people are still in their unresolved crisis. Paul may have this image in mind with 'now is the acceptable time' (2 Cor 6:2).
8.21: 'I am broken over the brokenness of my people; I mourn, and horror has gripped me' — ʿal-šeber bat-ʿammî hušbārtî (I am broken over the breaking of my people's daughter) — Jeremiah participates in the very judgment he has announced. The word šeber (breaking, fracture) is used for the mortal wound that will bring Judah down; Jeremiah suffers that wound vicariously. This is not professional detachment but pastoral solidarity: the prophet's grief is proportionate to the scale of the disaster. Lamentations 2:11 uses similar language in Jeremiah's voice over the fall of Jerusalem.
8.22: 'Is there no balm in Gilead? Is there no physician there? Then why has the wound of my people not been healed?' — Gilead (Transjordan) was famous in antiquity for its balsam resin (ṣĕrî), used as a wound-dressing and healing agent (Gen 37:25: Joseph sold by the Midianites who were carrying Gileadite balm to Egypt). The three rhetorical questions are saturated with grief: the resources exist (balm, physician), yet the healing has not come. The answer — implied but not stated — is that Israel's wound is not physical but covenantal, and the covenantal physician is being refused. The image inspired the American spiritual 'There Is a Balm in Gilead,' which applies the image christologically: the healing that is not available through the law is available through the gospel.

9.1: 'Oh that my head were a spring of water and my eyes a fountain of tears, so that I could weep day and night for the slain of my dear people!' — the opening wish for supernatural capacity for grief establishes chapter 9 as the fullest expression of Jeremiah's prophetic sorrow. 'My dear people' (bat-ʿammî, daughter of my people) is an affectionate collective term for the community. The 'slain' (ḥalalê) are those killed in battle and by the siege — not yet historical events at the time of writing but prophetically certain. Jeremiah's tears are the human emotional counterpart to the divine grief that drives the judgment.
9.2: 'Oh that I had a lodging place for travelers in the wilderness, so that I could leave my people and go away from them!' — the wish for escape from his own people is the darkest expression of prophetic isolation. The contrast between vv. 1-2 is striking: in v. 1 Jeremiah wishes he could weep more for them; in v. 2 he wishes he could flee from them. Both impulses are authentic: deep grief and deep revulsion coexist in the prophet's experience of a people he loves but who are 'all adulterers' (kî kullām mĕʾănāpîm) and 'a gathering of traitors' (ʿăṣeret bōgĕdîm). The tension between prophetic compassion and prophetic honesty is never resolved in Jeremiah.
9.3: 'They have bent their tongue like a bow to shoot falsehood; they have grown powerful in the land but not by truth' — the tongue-as-bow metaphor pictures deliberate deception as an aggressive act: lies are arrows aimed at specific targets. The 'growing powerful not by truth' (lōʾ lĕʾĕmûnāh gāberû bāʾāreṣ) describes the social-political success that the faithless have achieved — their prosperity proves not that their behavior is acceptable but that the covenant consequences have been temporarily deferred. 'They do not know me, declares the LORD' — yādaʿ in the covenant-intimacy sense: their practical behavior demonstrates the absence of genuine relationship, regardless of their religious vocabulary.
9.4: 'Beware of your neighbors; put no trust in any relative — for every brother is a schemer, and every neighbor goes around spreading slander' — the 'every brother is a Jacob' idiom: kol-ʾāḥ ʿāqôb yĕʿāqēb (every brother is a deceiver/Jacob). The verb ʿāqab means to supplant/trip up from behind; it is the root of the name Jacob (yaʿăqōb), who supplanted Esau (Gen 25:26; 27:36: 'Is he not rightly named Jacob? He has supplanted me twice!'). Jeremiah's society has generalized the Jacob-trait: the competitive deception that characterized the patriarch's early life has become the universal social norm. The betrayal of family and neighbor destroyed the social trust that covenant community required.
9.5: 'Everyone deceives his neighbor, and no one tells the truth. They have trained their tongue to lie; they exhaust themselves committing sin' — limmĕdû lĕšônām dabbēr-šeqer (they have trained their tongue to speak falsehood) treats deception as an acquired skill: the ability to lie fluently requires practice and habituation. The exhaustion through sin (nileʾû ʿăwōtām) is the moral version of the physical exhaustion of pointless labor — sustained transgression that produces no genuine benefit and drains the transgressor. The spiritual-agricultural metaphor runs throughout the book: covenant violation is literally fruitless and tiring.
9.6: 'You live in the midst of deception; through their deceit they refuse to know me, declares the LORD' — the deception has become the social environment: there is no social space outside of it. The refusal to know YHWH is described as an active choice enabled by the deception — the lies people tell create an alternative reality that crowds out the true knowledge of YHWH. In the Johannine register, this is the dynamic of darkness that 'hates the light' (John 3:20); in Jeremiah's register, it is the social construction of a lie-world that makes covenant truth inaccessible.
9.7: 'Therefore this is what the LORD of hosts says: I will refine them and test them — for what else can I do with my sinful people?' — the divine query 'what else can I do?' (mâ-ʾeʿĕśeh) is one of the most remarkable expressions of divine perplexity in the OT. It does not indicate divine helplessness but divine faithfulness to a people who have exhausted all normal means of covenant restoration: warnings have been given, prophets have been sent, judgment has been threatened. The refining-and-testing metaphor (Prov 17:3; 27:21; Isa 48:10; Mal 3:3) describes the purifying judgment: the exile will burn away the dross, but the process is painful for the metal.
9.8: 'Their tongue is a lethal arrow that shoots out deceit. With their mouths they speak peace to their neighbors, but in their hearts they are planning an ambush' — the peace-from-the-mouth / ambush-in-the-heart contrast is the prophetic diagnosis of covenant social collapse: the language of covenant solidarity (šālôm) is maintained while the practice of betrayal continues. This is what makes Jeremiah's era particularly dangerous: it is not openly hostile society but covertly treacherous society. The false peace-speaking is worse than open enmity because it prevents the betrayed party from protecting themselves.
9.9: 'Should I not punish them for all this? declares the LORD. Should I not take vengeance on such a nation as this?' — the double rhetorical question expects 'yes' and functions as a legal declaration of just cause for judgment. The identical formulation appears at 5:9 and 5:29, creating a refrain that punctuates the judgment oracles with legal self-justification. YHWH is not acting arbitrarily or out of wounded pride but in response to specific, documented covenant violations — the judgment is proportionate and just.
9.10: 'I will take up weeping and wailing for the mountains and lamentation for the wilderness pastures, for they have been laid waste so that no one passes through' — the mourning over the mountains and pastures personifies the land as a victim of the judgment, not merely its location. In the ANE, the land was understood as a moral agent responsive to the behavior of its inhabitants (Lev 18:25,28: the land vomits out its sinful inhabitants; Deut 11:17: the land refuses to yield its produce when YHWH withholds rain). The silence of the vacated land — no cattle, no birds, no wildlife — is the ecological consequence of the covenantal devastation.
9.11: 'I will make Jerusalem a heap of rubble, a haunt of jackals; I will make the cities of Judah a wasteland, with no one living in them' — the jackal-haunt (mĕʿôn tannîm) is one of the standard prophetic images for desolated cities (Isa 13:22; 34:13; 35:7; Jer 10:22; 49:33; 51:37; Mal 1:3). Jackals are scavengers that inhabit ruins — their presence marks a human settlement as definitively abandoned. The image was fulfilled in the post-586 BCE devastation of Judah, which archaeological surveys confirm as a dramatic population collapse across multiple sites. The Lachish Letters, written just before the Babylonian final assault, reflect a Judah rapidly losing its cities.
9.12: 'Who is wise enough to understand this? Who has received the word from the LORD and can explain it? Why has the land been ruined and laid waste like a desert that no one crosses?' — the wisdom-challenge is addressed to the sage class (8:8-9): the wisest interpreter of events is the one who can explain why the land has been destroyed. The question challenges the covenant-logic skeptics who attributed Judah's difficulties to political misfortune or divine indifference rather than to covenant violation. The answer in vv. 13-14 is programmatic: abandonment of the Torah + Baal-following = destruction.
9.13: 'Because they have abandoned my law that I placed before them, and have not obeyed my voice or followed it' — the two-part answer: law abandonment + voice disobedience. 'My law that I placed before them' (tôrātî ʾăšer nātattî lipnêhem) refers specifically to the written Torah — the covenant document that Moses deposited beside the ark (Deut 31:26) and that Josiah's reform had rediscovered (2 Kings 22:8). The written law's public availability makes its abandonment inexcusable: the people had access to the covenant terms and chose to disregard them.
9.14: 'Instead they followed the stubbornness of their own hearts and went after the Baals, just as their ancestors had taught them' — šĕrîrût libbām (the stubbornness of their hearts) appears again as Jeremiah's signature diagnosis. The generational transmission of Baal worship — 'just as their ancestors had taught them' — points to the family-based religious formation described in 7:18 (children, fathers, and women all participating in the queen of heaven cult). The apostasy is not individual but systemic: it has been embedded in the intergenerational transmission that Torah commanded for covenant faithfulness (Deut 6:7) but that has been redirected to Baal instead.
9.15: 'I will feed this people with wormwood and give them poisoned water to drink' — wormwood (laʾănāh) was an extremely bitter plant (Artemisia absinthium, still used in absinthe); to eat it was proverbially associated with suffering and injustice (Prov 5:4; Amos 5:7; 6:12). Poisoned water (mê rōʾš) continues the bitter-water image from 8:14. In contrast to the 'spring of living water' that YHWH is (2:13), the covenant-cursed people receive its opposite — bitter, poisonous sustenance. Revelation 8:11 (the star Wormwood poisoning the waters) draws on this prophetic tradition.
9.16: 'I will scatter them among nations that neither they nor their ancestors have known; and I will pursue them with the sword until I have consumed them' — the scattering among unknown nations intensifies the covenant-curse of Deuteronomy 28:36,64 ('the LORD will drive you and the king you set over you to a nation unknown to you or your fathers'). The 'unknown nations' are specifically non-Israel's covenant context: no temple, no land, no ancestral heritage, no community of Torah-observance. The sword-pursuit until consumption describes the sustained campaign: the exile will not be a brief diplomatic deportation but a comprehensive destruction.
9.17: 'Consider this carefully; summon the professional mourning women to come; send for the most skilled among them to come' — professional mourners (hamĕqônenôt, the wailing women; hahăkāmôt, the skilled ones) were an institutionalized profession in the ancient Near East. In Israel, Egypt, and Mesopotamia, trained women were employed to amplify the communal grief at funerals through rhythmic wailing, composing and performing dirges (qînôt). Their professional skill was the ability to generate and sustain grief rituals that helped communities process collective loss. Jeremiah summons them because the coming loss will be too great for amateur lamentation.
9.18: 'Let them come quickly and raise a lament over us, so that our eyes may stream with tears and our eyelids overflow with weeping' — the description of extreme grief — tears streaming from the eyes and overflowing from the eyelids — is the physiological language of Lamentations 1:16 ('my eye, my eye overflows with water'). The weeping is communal ('over us') — the professional mourners lead the community's grief rather than performing it on behalf of passive bystanders. In the ANE mourning context, the external wailing of the professionals was understood to draw out and legitimize the internal grief of the bereaved community.
9.19: 'For a cry of wailing is heard from Zion: We are devastated! We are utterly disgraced — because we have had to leave the land, because our homes have been torn down' — the Zion lament anticipated in prophetic vision. The two causes of shame given — leaving the land (kî ʿāzabnû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ) and destroyed homes (kî hišlîkû miškĕnôtênû) — capture the two dimensions of the exile disaster: territorial displacement and household destruction. The land was the gift of the covenant; the home was the social unit of covenant practice. Both were lost simultaneously in the Babylonian deportation.
9.20: 'Now, women, hear the word of the LORD; receive the word from his mouth. Teach your daughters to mourn; teach one another this dirge' — the address to women connects the professional mourning of vv. 17-18 to the generational transmission of communal grief practices. Just as the queen of heaven worship was transmitted from mothers to daughters (7:18), the mourning for the covenant's collapse must also be transmitted in the female sphere. The dirge to be taught is not merely a cultural grief expression but a covenant testimony: this is what happens when YHWH's people abandon the covenant.
9.21: 'For death has climbed through our windows and entered our palaces, cutting down children in the streets and young men in the public squares' — the personification of death as an invader who enters through windows is found in Canaanite-Ugaritic mythology, where Mot (Death) is described as entering through windows. The specific targeting of children in the streets and young men in the squares (ʾăpiqîm, also translated as playgrounds/public squares) describes the urban massacre: the most visible and unprotected members of the community are killed first. The image appears in Lamentations 2:21 as fulfilled historical narrative.
9.22: 'Human corpses will fall like manure on the open field, like grain stalks left behind by the reaper, with no one to gather them up' — the two comparison images are both agricultural: manure (the waste product of agriculture, abundant but valueless) and abandoned grain stalks (the reaper's leftovers). Both images describe bodies treated as refuse rather than as persons deserving burial rites. The 'no one to gather them up' (wĕʾên mĕʾassēp) is the denial of the harvesting function — the bodies lie in the field unrecovered, as if the community itself has ceased to function as a community.
9.23: 'Let not the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their strength, nor the rich in their wealth' — the three boasting prohibitions cover the standard ANE triads of human achievement: wisdom (the scribal class), strength (the warrior class), and wealth (the merchant class). Each was a form of social power that provided security in the human sphere. In the context of Jeremiah's time, these were also the three grounds on which the Jerusalem establishment justified its confidence in survival: they had wise advisors, a military, and economic resources. The oracle strips each ground of confidence by subordinating it to covenant knowledge.
9.24: 'But let him who boasts boast in this: that he understands and knows me — that I am the LORD who exercises steadfast love, justice, and righteousness in the earth' — the three divine character qualities (ḥesed, mišpāṭ, ṣĕdāqāh) that YHWH declares he delights in are the exact covenant qualities that Israel's prophets consistently accuse the people of lacking. Ḥesed (steadfast love/covenant loyalty), mišpāṭ (justice), and ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) are the Hosea 6:6, Amos 5:24, and Micah 6:8 triad. Paul quotes the verse's first half in 1 Corinthians 1:31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17, applying it to the cross as the proper ground of Christian boasting.
9.25: 'The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will punish all who are only outwardly circumcised' — the 'circumcised in foreskin' (kol-mûl bĕʿorlāh) but uncircumcised in heart extends the judgment beyond Israel to the surrounding nations. The passage (vv. 25-26) is unusual: it places Judah in the same category as Egypt, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and the desert peoples who practice circumcision as a custom but not as a covenant sign. Paul's argument in Romans 2:25-29 and Galatians 5:6 builds directly on this prophetic tradition: physical circumcision without covenant faithfulness is equivalent to non-circumcision.
9.26: 'Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and all who live in the wilderness and clip the hair at their temples — for all these nations are physically uncircumcised, and all the house of Israel is uncircumcised in heart' — the nations listed (except possibly the desert peoples) practiced various forms of circumcision. Egypt circumcised as a coming-of-age rite; several Semitic peoples practiced it as a pre-marriage rite. The point is that circumcision as a physical practice was not distinctively Israelite — what was distinctive was its function as the covenant sign. Israel has reduced the covenant sign to the level of a pagan custom by maintaining the physical rite while ignoring the covenant commitment it represents. Deuteronomy 10:16 and 30:6 issued the same corrective.

10.1: Jeremiah 10 opens with an urgent summons: hear the word YHWH speaks to you, O house of Israel. The chapter pivots from oracles of judgment into something closer to a disputation — a sustained polemic against the idol-worship that defines Judah's apostasy. The entire chapter (vv1–16) constitutes one of the Old Testament's most elaborate idol-critiques, standing alongside Isaiah 44 and Psalm 115. The form is ironic: objects that require human labor to construct are mocked as gods.
10.2: Do not learn the way of the nations — the Hebrew derek haggoyim covers not simply ritual practice but the entire mindset and orientation of those who look to the sky for portents. Be not dismayed at the signs of heaven: the nations watch celestial omens — eclipses, comets, planetary conjunctions — for divine communication. Mesopotamian omen-texts (Enuma Anu Enlil) ran to thousands of entries cataloguing exactly such phenomena. Israel is forbidden this interpretive framework: YHWH speaks through his prophets, not through the zodiac.
10.3: The polemic begins with a manufacturing sequence. The customs of the peoples are worthless (hevel, vapor, vanity — the same word structuring Ecclesiastes): one cuts a tree from the forest, a craftsman shapes it with an axe. The sequence deliberately demystifies what the nations regard as sacred. The tree has no inherent divinity; it is raw material that becomes an idol only through human skill — which means it is subordinate to its maker.
10.4: They adorn it with silver and gold, they fasten it with hammer and nails so it will not totter. The satirical edge is sharp: a god that must be nailed down to keep from falling cannot protect anyone. The same irony appears in Isaiah 44:17 and Psalm 135:15–17. The idol is permanent fixture, not living presence. It cannot move, act, speak, or respond — which is to say, it cannot do the things a god is supposed to do.
10.5: Like a scarecrow in a cucumber field — the Hebrew tomer bamiqshah compares the idol to a carved post set in a garden to frighten birds. It has form but no life, presence but no power. They must be carried, for they cannot walk: the idol requires human transport. Ancient Near Eastern texts describe elaborate processions carrying divine statues, treating the act as an honor. Jeremiah inverts the meaning: the need to be carried is evidence of impotence. Do not fear them, for they cannot do evil, neither is it in them to do good — the idol is morally neutral because it is ontologically empty.
10.6: The idol-polemic breaks into doxology. There is none like you, YHWH; you are great and your name is great in might. After cataloguing what the idols cannot do, Jeremiah declares what YHWH can and is. The contrast is total: the nations' gods are humanly made and humanly maintained; YHWH is self-existent and sovereign. Might (gevurah) connotes warrior-power and creative power simultaneously — the same power that defeated Egypt also sustains creation.
10.7: Who would not fear you, O King of the nations? — YHWH's kingship extends beyond Israel to all peoples. This universalizing claim stands against the ancient conception of gods as territorial, bound to particular nations and lands. Among all the wise men of the nations and in all their kingdoms there is none like you: Jeremiah does not merely dismiss the nations' gods as inferior competitors; he asserts YHWH's incomparability across all domains — wisdom, power, kingship.
10.8: They are both stupid and foolish; the instruction of idols is but wood — the Hebrew is dense and somewhat difficult, but the thrust is clear: the theological worldview built around idol-worship is epistemologically bankrupt. The instruction (musar) one receives from carved wood is nothing. Wisdom traditions across the ancient world evaluated teachers and teaching; Jeremiah applies that evaluative lens and finds the idol-system produces only foolishness.
10.9: Beaten silver is brought from Tarshish, and gold from Uphaz — Tarshish likely refers to a distant western trade destination (possibly Sardinia or Spain); Uphaz is uncertain but denotes a distant source of fine gold. The point is that the finest imported materials go into idol construction. The work of the craftsman and of the hands of the goldsmith: human skill at its highest level produces these objects. Yet they remain merely blue and purple — fine cloth — wrapped around dead wood. The luxury of the materials cannot impart life.
10.10: But YHWH is the true God; he is the living God and the everlasting King. Against all the craftsmen's products stands Elohim emet — the God of truth, the genuine God, the real God. The contrast with the idols is absolute: they are hevel (vapor); he is emet (solid reality). At his wrath the earth quakes, and the nations cannot endure his indignation: YHWH's response to opposition is cosmic, not ceremonial. The theophanic imagery of earthquake recall Sinai (Exod 19:18) and Psalm 18 — this is the God who acts in history and in nature.
10.11: Verse 11 is unique in all of Jeremiah: it is written in Aramaic, not Hebrew. The verse reads: Elahayya di-shamayya vearqa la avadu yeavadun min-area umin-tachat shemayya elleh — The gods who did not make the heavens and the earth shall perish from the earth and from under the heavens. Scholarly consensus holds this is a scribal gloss — a polemical response inserted to be quoted directly at Babylonian captors who might pressure exilic Jews to honor their gods. The Aramaic is the lingua franca of the Neo-Babylonian and Persian empires. The verse functions as a set formula: when a Babylonian demands you bow to Marduk, quote this.
10.12: The creation hymn (vv12–13) is almost verbatim reproduced in Jeremiah 51:15–19, suggesting it was a liturgical piece in circulation. It is he who made the earth by his power, who established the world by his wisdom — creation by power (koach) and wisdom (chokhmah) rather than by combat (contra the Babylonian Enuma Elish where Marduk defeats Tiamat). The wisdom tradition and prophetic tradition converge: the same God who speaks through prophets is the God who ordered the cosmos rationally.
10.13: When he utters his voice, there is a tumult of waters in the heavens, and he makes the mist rise from the ends of the earth. He makes lightning for the rain, and he brings forth the wind from his storehouses. The storm theophany of vv12–13 positions YHWH as storm-sovereign — a role contested by Baal in Canaanite religion, where the storm-god was the primary fertility deity. The meteorological imagery is not metaphorical decoration; it is a direct claim that Baal worship is unnecessary and wrong. YHWH controls the rain cycles that determine agricultural survival.
10.14: Every man is stupid and without knowledge; every goldsmith is put to shame by his idols — the idol-maker is convicted by his own product. The Hebrew nivhal (shamed, confounded) carries the force of being exposed and humiliated. In a shame-honor culture, this is devastating: the craftsman hoped the idol would bring him honor, but it becomes evidence of his foolishness. For his molten image is a lie, and there is no breath in them: the idol is sheqer (falsehood, deceit) — it promises what it cannot deliver.
10.15: They are worthless, a work of delusion — hevel again (vapor) and ma'aseh ta'tu'im (work of mockery or delusion). At the time of their punishment they shall perish: the idols are not merely ineffective but actively condemned. The idol-systems will not survive YHWH's judgment because they are ontologically hollow — they have no existence independent of human effort, and that effort is under judgment.
10.16: Not like these is he who is the portion of Jacob — the theological contrast completes the polemic. YHWH is described as Jacob's cheleq (portion, inheritance) — an intimate covenantal term normally used of land-shares in Canaan. YHWH himself is Israel's inheritance, not just a national deity who delivers territory. For he is the one who formed all things, and Israel is the tribe of his inheritance; YHWH of hosts is his name. The divine title YHWH Tseva'ot (YHWH of armies/hosts) closes the hymn — the creator is also the warrior-king who commands cosmic armies.
10.17: The scene shifts from cosmic polemic to national catastrophe. Gather up your bundle from the ground, O you who dwell under siege. The second-person singular shifts to address Jerusalem (feminine). The instruction to gather one's bundle for exile is not an escape route but a command to prepare for deportation. The imagery is that of a besieged city about to fall — the inhabitants should collect what they can carry before being driven out.
10.18: For thus says YHWH: Behold, I am slinging out the inhabitants of the land at this time — the Hebrew qola' uses sling-stone imagery: YHWH will hurl the people like stones from a sling. The violence and the precision of the image communicate both judgment and intentionality. And I will bring distress on them, that they may feel it: the purpose of judgment is not obliteration but recognition — the people must experience the consequences of their apostasy.
10.19: Jeremiah's lament voice enters: Woe is me because of my hurt! My wound is grievous. The prophet identifies with the nation's suffering even as he announces it. But I said, Truly this is an affliction, and I must bear it. The Hebrew is difficult — it may be Jeremiah speaking, or Jerusalem speaking, or both (the ambiguity is likely intentional). The lament tradition in Jeremiah blurs the line between prophet and city, between Jeremiah's personal suffering and Jerusalem's coming destruction.
10.20: My tent is destroyed, and all my cords are broken; my children have gone from me, and they are no more — the tent-imagery evokes nomadic life but also the tabernacle and the dwelling of God among his people. The dismantled tent is a picture of covenant rupture. There is no one to spread my tent again and to set up my curtains: no restoration is in view here, only the completeness of the devastation. The loss of children images the exile of Judah's population.
10.21: For the shepherds are stupid and do not inquire of YHWH; therefore they have not prospered, and all their flock is scattered. The ro'im (shepherds) are the political and religious leaders of Judah — the kings, priests, and prophets who should have guided the people toward YHWH but instead pursued their own interests and foreign alliances. The failure to darash (inquire, seek) YHWH is the root diagnosis: they simply did not consult the covenant God before making decisions. The result is the scattered flock — a scattered people heading into exile.
10.22: Hark, a report! Behold, it comes! — a great commotion out of the north country to make the cities of Judah a desolation, a lair of jackals. The north-foe motif recurs throughout Jeremiah 4–10. The north country is the geographic and theological direction from which Babylon will descend. A lair of jackals: the jackal becomes a recurring image for desolation in Jeremiah (9:11; 49:33; 51:37) — cities emptied of human life become habitat for wild animals, the reversal of civilization.
10.23: I know, O YHWH, that the way of man is not in himself, that it is not in man who walks to direct his steps. One of Jeremiah's great theological statements on human epistemological and volitional limits. The derek (way) requires divine guidance — not because humans lack intelligence but because human autonomy severed from YHWH produces systematic moral failure. Proverbs 16:9 parallels: the heart of a man plans his way, but YHWH establishes his steps. Jeremiah is not denying human agency but locating it within divine sovereignty.
10.24: Correct me, O YHWH, but in justice; not in your anger, lest you bring me to nothing. Jeremiah's prayer models covenant petition: he does not ask for immunity from discipline but for proportionate rather than consuming judgment. The distinction between mishpat (justice/measured correction) and af (wrath/anger) is significant — anger unchecked destroys; justice corrects. The prayer functions as intercession for the remnant: reduce the punishment to what can be survived.
10.25: Pour out your wrath on the nations that know you not, and on the peoples that call not on your name — Jeremiah redirects judgment toward those who have oppressed Israel: for they have devoured Jacob; they have devoured him and consumed him and have laid waste his habitation. This verse anticipates the oracles against the nations that will occupy chapters 46–51. The theological logic is not ethnic nationalism but covenantal accountability: the nations who served as YHWH's instrument of judgment on Israel will themselves face judgment for their cruelty and idolatry. The prayer ends on a note of appeal to YHWH's role as universal judge.

11.1: Chapter 11 opens a new unit: the word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH. The covenant-lawsuit form that follows is one of the most developed in the prophets. Jeremiah is commissioned to proclaim the covenant — specifically, the Deuteronomic covenant that Josiah had reinstituted following the discovery of the law-scroll in 621 BC (2 Kgs 22–23). The chapter thus presupposes a post-Josiah setting, likely early in Jehoiakim's reign when the reforms had already begun to unravel.
11.2: Hear the words of this covenant — the word berit (covenant) will appear seven times in vv1–11, structuring the passage as a covenant-proclamation. Jeremiah is not composing new theology; he is re-announcing the Mosaic covenant in a context where it has been systematically violated. The repetition of berit functions both as reminder and as indictment — every mention is an implicit accusation.
11.3: And say to them, Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Cursed be the man who does not hear the words of this covenant. The curse formula inverts the blessing of Deuteronomy 28: where Deuteronomy opened with barak (blessed), Jeremiah's covenant proclamation opens with arur (cursed). The curse is not new law but the already-announced consequence of covenant violation. Jeremiah is announcing that the cursed state has arrived.
11.4: …which I commanded your fathers when I brought them out of the land of Egypt, from the iron furnace — Egypt as iron furnace (kur habarzel) is a Deuteronomic image (Deut 4:20; 1 Kgs 8:51) depicting the severity of Israelite slavery. The covenant was forged in the context of liberation: YHWH freed a people from the worst form of human bondage and then entered covenant with them. Listen to my voice and do all that I command you. So shall you be my people, and I will be your God — the covenant formula in its simplest bilateral form.
11.5: That I may confirm the oath that I swore to your fathers, to give them a land flowing with milk and honey — the patriarchal promise (Gen 12:1–3, 15:18–21, 22:15–18) stands behind the Sinai covenant. The Exodus-covenant is the mechanism by which the patriarchal land-promise is fulfilled. Jeremiah is tracing the full theological line from Abraham to Sinai to Canaan, demonstrating that covenant violation now threatens the entire promise-chain. As at this day: the land had been given, was being occupied — covenant violation now threatens that possession.
11.6: Then YHWH said to me, Proclaim all these words in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem — Jeremiah is sent as a circuit-preacher of the covenant, traveling to every city and the capital. This is consistent with his role throughout the book as covenant-prosecutor. The instruction to proclaim in the streets of Jerusalem suggests the message is not restricted to Temple precincts; it is public, confrontational, unavoidable.
11.7: For I solemnly warned your fathers when I brought them up out of the land of Egypt, warning them persistently, even to this day — the repeated sending of prophets is a characteristic Deuteronomistic theme (cf. 2 Kgs 17:13; Jer 7:25; 25:4; 26:5). YHWH's patience is not passivity — he has been warning, sending messengers, calling for return. The failure is entirely on the human side. Saying, Obey my voice: the covenant demand is simple; the failure to meet it is a choice, not an accident.
11.8: Yet they did not obey or incline their ear, but everyone walked in the stubbornness of his evil heart. The sherirut lev hara' (stubbornness of the evil heart) is a recurring Jeremianic diagnosis (7:24; 13:10; 16:12; 18:12; 23:17). The stubbornness is not merely behavioral but anthropological — the heart itself has been corrupted toward self-will. Therefore I brought upon them all the words of this covenant, which I commanded them to do, but they did not: the curses of Deuteronomy 28 are now being implemented.
11.9: Again YHWH said to me: A conspiracy exists among the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem. The term qesher (conspiracy, binding together) is normally used of political plots (2 Kgs 12:20; 14:19). Here it describes the collective apostasy of Judah and Jerusalem as a deliberate organized rebellion — not accidental drift but coordinated rejection of the covenant. The language heightens the culpability: this is not ignorance but intentional betrayal.
11.10: They have turned back to the iniquities of their forefathers, who refused to hear my words; they have gone after other gods to serve them. The generational structure of apostasy: the present generation has not invented a new sin but returned to the ancestral pattern. The house of Israel and the house of Judah have broken my covenant that I made with their fathers: both kingdoms are indicted. This matters in Jeremiah's context because the northern kingdom had already fallen to Assyria in 722 BC — Judah has not learned from that catastrophic judgment.
11.11: Therefore, thus says YHWH: Behold, I am bringing disaster upon them that they cannot escape — ra'ah (disaster, evil) is a covenant-judgment term. The declaration that they cannot escape answers the false security embedded in the Zion-theology (the assumption that YHWH will always protect Jerusalem). Though they cry to me, I will not listen to them: covenant prayer requires covenant fidelity. YHWH's non-response to prayer is not capriciousness but covenant logic — they have broken the relationship that would make prayer meaningful.
11.12: Then the cities of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem will go and cry to the gods to whom they make offerings — the bitter irony: when YHWH stops listening, they will turn to the idols they have preferred. But they cannot save them in the time of their trouble: the crisis will expose the idols' impotence. The people will discover that the alternative gods they pursued cannot perform the one function most needed — deliverance. This is the idol-critique of chapter 10 now applied to historical judgment.
11.13: For your gods have become as many as your cities, O Judah, and as many as the streets of Jerusalem are the altars you have set up to shame, altars to make offerings to Baal. The proliferation of altars mirrors the proliferation of apostasy: every city has its own local shrine, every street its own altar. The phrase altars to shame (mizbachot laboshet) is a deliberate substitution — boshet (shame) replaces the deity's name, a polemical editorial convention used elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Ish-bosheth = Ish-Baal, Mephibosheth = Merib-Baal). Baal worship is literally named as shame.
11.14: As for you, do not pray for this people, or lift up a cry or prayer on their behalf, for I will not listen when they call to me in the time of their trouble. The prohibition of intercession (cf. 7:16; 14:11; 15:1) is one of the most theologically striking elements in Jeremiah. YHWH is withdrawing the intercessory access that Moses, Samuel, and the prophets had exercised on Israel's behalf (cf. 15:1: even if Moses and Samuel stood before me). The prohibition signals that the covenant has been violated beyond the point where intercession can operate — not because prayer is powerless, but because the relational conditions for prayer have been destroyed.
11.15: What right has my beloved in my house, when she has done many vile deeds? — the marriage imagery of the covenant surfaces: Israel is YHWH's beloved (yedidah), but she has defiled the house (the Temple) with her vile deeds. Can the vows and sacrificial flesh avert your doom?: the question is sarcastic — the cultic system cannot function as a substitute for covenant fidelity. The Temple sacrifices Judah continues to offer cannot ward off the judgment they have earned by their apostasy.
11.16: YHWH once called you 'a green olive tree, beautiful with good fruit' — the olive tree is one of the most valuable and long-lived of Palestinian cultivars, associated with prosperity, peace, and permanence (Ps 52:8). The past-tense once called is devastating: the beautiful description is historical, not current. With the roar of a great tempest he will set fire to it, and its branches will be consumed: the same tree YHWH called beautiful will be incinerated. The covenant people who were cultivated will be destroyed by the very God who tended them.
11.17: YHWH of hosts, who planted you, has decreed disaster against you, because of the evil that the house of Israel and the house of Judah have done, provoking me to anger by making offerings to Baal. YHWH as the planter and YHWH as the destroyer of the same tree frames the entire covenant relationship: he is not destroying something foreign to himself but his own planting. The logic of covenant judgment requires that the one who planted must also carry out the consequences of the covenant curse. Baal-worship is explicitly named as the trigger.
11.18: YHWH made it known to me and I knew; then you showed me their deeds — the narrative shifts into biographical material: Jeremiah describes his discovery that the people of Anathoth (his hometown) were plotting against his life. The transition from covenant-proclamation to personal persecution is not incidental — Jeremiah's bodily endangerment enacts what he prophetically announces: the rejection of the covenant word will cost the messenger his life, just as it will cost the nation its existence.
11.19: But I was like a gentle lamb led to the slaughter. I did not know it was against me they devised schemes, saying, 'Let us destroy the tree with its fruit; let us cut him off from the land of the living'. The gentle lamb led to slaughter becomes one of the OT's richest images, explicitly echoed in Isaiah 53:7 (he was led like a lamb to the slaughter) and applied to Jesus in John 1:29 and Acts 8:32. Jeremiah's innocent suffering at the hands of those who reject YHWH's word becomes a template for the Suffering Servant. The conspirators' phrase — cut him off from the land of the living — is the language of judicial murder.
11.20: But, O YHWH of hosts, who judges righteously, who tests the heart and the mind, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you have I committed my cause. Jeremiah's response to the conspiracy is not retaliation but covenant appeal. He commits his case (riv, his legal dispute) to YHWH as judge. The prayer that YHWH will vindicate him is not personal vengeance but covenant justice — the covenant God must act against those who violate his word and persecute his messenger. Tests the heart and mind (lev vekilyot, heart and kidneys) — YHWH sees motivations inaccessible to human courts.
11.21: Therefore thus says YHWH concerning the men of Anathoth who seek your life, and say, 'Do not prophesy in the name of YHWH, or you will die by our hand' — the threat is explicit and direct: cease prophesying or be killed. Anathoth was a Levitical city in Benjamin; the priestly community there likely resented Jeremiah's attacks on the Temple establishment and saw his prophecies as dangerous. Do not prophesy in the name of YHWH: the command to silence is itself a covenant violation — suppressing YHWH's word is the deeper crime underlying the murder plot.
11.22: Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I will punish them. The young men shall die by the sword, their sons and daughters shall die by famine. YHWH's response to the conspiracy against his prophet is covenant judgment applied specifically to the conspirators: the sword and famine — two of the standard covenant-curse triad (sword, famine, plague; cf. Jer 14:12; 21:9; 24:10). The specificity of the punishment — young men by sword, sons and daughters by famine — enacts the totality of household destruction.
11.23: And none of them shall be left. For I will bring disaster upon the men of Anathoth, the year of their punishment. The phrase shanah pequdatam (year of their punishment/visitation) is a Jeremianic technical term for the time of divine reckoning (cf. 8:12; 23:12; 48:44). The word paqad (visit, attend to, reckon with) can be positive (YHWH attending to his people in mercy) or negative (YHWH reckoning with their crimes). Here it is the latter: a fixed appointment with judgment from which there is no escape. The chapter ends with the persecutors fully condemned.

12.1: Righteous are you, O YHWH, when I complain to you; yet I would plead my case before you. Why does the way of the wicked prosper? Why do all who are treacherous thrive? Chapter 12 opens with one of the Old Testament's sharpest theodicy complaints. Jeremiah acknowledges YHWH's righteousness even while pressing the question of why the wicked prosper — a question that runs through Habakkuk, Job, and multiple Psalms (especially Ps 73). The complaint is not skepticism but covenant argument: if YHWH is just, why does injustice appear to succeed? The riv (legal dispute) language continues from 11:20.
12.2: You plant them, and they take root; they grow and produce fruit — the agricultural imagery inverts the olive-tree of 11:16. The wicked flourish like well-tended plants; they are not experiencing covenant curses but covenant blessings — at least on the surface. You are near in their mouth and far from their heart: the wicked have mastered the religious performance — they speak the right words — but their interior orientation is entirely away from YHWH. This is exactly the condition Jesus will describe in Matthew 15:8 (quoting Isaiah 29:13): their lips honor me, but their hearts are far from me.
12.3: But you, O YHWH, know me; you see me, and test my heart toward you — Jeremiah offers himself as the contrast: YHWH sees his actual interior orientation, not just his external words. Pull them out like sheep for slaughter, and set them apart for the day of slaughter. The prayer for divine action against the wicked is not personal vindictiveness but covenant appeal: Jeremiah is asking YHWH to act according to his own declared character as judge. The day of slaughter is an eschatological reckoning — a specific appointed time of account.
12.4: How long will the land mourn and the grass of every field wither? For the evil of those who dwell in it the beasts and the birds are swept away — the suffering of the natural world is connected to human covenant violation. The land mourning (abal) is not merely metaphorical; in biblical ecology, creation groans under the weight of human sin (cf. Rom 8:19–22). Drought, crop failure, and animal death are signs of covenant curse embedded in creation itself (Deut 28:23–24; Amos 4:6–10). Because they said, 'He will not see our latter end': the wicked believe YHWH is unaware or indifferent — the root theological error.
12.5: If you have raced with men on foot, and they have wearied you, how will you compete with horses? — YHWH's response to Jeremiah's complaint is not consolation but challenge. The rhetorical escalation is pointed: if the ordinary threats from Anathoth have already exhausted Jeremiah, how will he survive what is coming — the full Babylonian invasion, the destruction of Jerusalem, the even more violent persecution that awaits him? The answer is that Jeremiah cannot survive in his own strength; he must learn to depend entirely on YHWH. And if in a safe land you are so trusting, what will you do in the thicket of the Jordan? — the Jordan's thickets were dense, dangerous, lion-inhabited terrain (cf. 49:19; 50:44) — wilderness at its most threatening.
12.6: For even your brothers and the house of your father, even they have dealt treacherously with you; they are in full cry after you; do not believe them, though they speak friendly words to you. YHWH warns Jeremiah that even his own family has betrayed him — the Anathoth conspiracy was not just religious opposition but familial. This intensifies the Jeremiah-as-Suffering-Servant parallel: the one who speaks YHWH's word is betrayed by those closest to him. The specific warning not to trust friendly words is pastoral instruction for navigating a context where allegiances have been corrupted.
12.7: I have forsaken my house; I have abandoned my heritage; I have given the beloved of my soul into the hands of her enemies. YHWH himself speaks a lament — the same imagery Jeremiah used (11:15: my beloved in my house) now voiced by the divine speaker. The covenant reversal is total: my house, my heritage, the beloved of my soul are handed over to enemies. This is the language of a husband abandoning a wife — covenantal divorce enacted by exile. The theological claim is astonishing: YHWH is not unmoved by Jerusalem's destruction; he experiences it as loss.
12.8: My heritage has become to me like a lion in the forest; she has lifted up her voice against me; therefore I hate her. The beloved wife has become a predatory beast. The verb sane'ti (I hate her) is the legal language of divorce in Deuteronomy 24:3 — the husband who hates his wife sends her away. Applied to the covenant, it describes the covenantal repudiation of a people who have turned against their God. This is the theological inversion of Exodus 19: the nation YHWH brought to himself to be his treasured possession has become his enemy.
12.9: Is my heritage to me like a hyena's lair? Are the birds of prey against her all around? Go, assemble all the wild beasts; bring them to devour. The imagery becomes increasingly savage: Judah as a carcass surrounded by scavengers. The animals assembled to devour her represent the surrounding nations — Babylon and its allies — called by YHWH to execute judgment. The divine summons to the beasts inverts the Edenic order where humans had dominion over animals; in judgment, the animals are given dominion over the sinful human community.
12.10: Many shepherds have destroyed my vineyard; they have trampled down my portion; they have made my pleasant portion a desolate wilderness. The many shepherds may refer to the succession of bad Judahite kings (cf. Jer 23:1–4) or to the conquering nations. Either way, the vineyard image (cf. Isa 5:1–7 — YHWH's vineyard as Israel) is demolished: the carefully tended planting that was Israel has been ruined by those who should have protected it. The triple repetition (destroyed, trampled, made desolate) emphasizes the completeness of the devastation.
12.11: They have made it a desolation; desolate, it mourns to me. The whole land is made desolate, but no man lays it to heart. The land mourning (avelah) recurs from v4 — the earth itself grieves its desolation. No man lays it to heart: the tragedy is compounded by universal indifference. No one in leadership is paying attention to what is happening, drawing the covenantal connection between the land's suffering and the people's sin. The prophets weep; the officials ignore them.
12.12: Upon all the bare heights in the desert destroyers have come, for the sword of YHWH devours from one end of the land to the other; no flesh has peace. The bare heights (shefayim) are the exposed ridge-lines of Judah — strategic positions from which armies invade. The sword of YHWH is not metaphorical but the covenant instrument of judgment announced in Deuteronomy 32:25 and Leviticus 26:25. No flesh has peace (ein shalom lekal basar) — the false-peace prophets of 6:14 and 8:11 are now exposed: there is no shalom anywhere in the land.
12.13: They have sown wheat and have reaped thorns; they have tired themselves out but profit nothing. They shall be ashamed of their harvests because of the fierce anger of YHWH. Agricultural failure as covenant curse enacts Deuteronomy 28:38–40: you will plant much but harvest little. The labor invested in agriculture yields nothing useful — an image of wasted lives under judgment. The shame of the harvest is public, communal shame in a culture where agricultural success was a mark of divine favor.
12.14: Thus says YHWH concerning all my evil neighbors who touch the heritage that I have given my people Israel to inherit: Behold, I will pluck them up from their land. The scope of judgment expands beyond Judah to encompass the surrounding nations who have participated in its destruction. Evil neighbors likely refers to Moab, Ammon, Edom, and the Aramean states who will be judged in chapters 46–51. YHWH's judgment is universal: those who despoil his inheritance will themselves be despoiled. This protects the integrity of YHWH's covenantal ownership of the land even in judgment.
12.15: And after I have plucked them up, I will again have compassion on them, and I will bring them again each to his heritage and each to his land. A remarkable promise of restoration for the surrounding nations — not just Israel. After judgment, YHWH will again have compassion on those nations. This universalizing of restoration anticipates the New Covenant sections of Jeremiah 31–33 and the NT vision of the nations being included in the people of God (Rom 15:7–12; Eph 2:11–22).
12.16: And it shall come to pass, if they will diligently learn the ways of my people, to swear by my name, 'As YHWH lives,' even as they taught my people to swear by Baal, then they shall be built up in the midst of my people. The restoration of the nations is conditional on covenant alignment: learning Israel's ways and swearing by YHWH's name. The nations who taught Israel Baal-worship can be rehabilitated — if they reverse their instruction and align with the covenant. The phrase built up in the midst of my people is striking: the nations are not merely forgiven but incorporated into the covenant community.
12.17: But if any nation will not listen, then I will utterly pluck up and destroy that nation, declares YHWH. The offer of restoration comes with covenant accountability. Nations that refuse to align with YHWH face the same judgment as Israel. This bilateral structure mirrors the Sinai covenant itself: blessing for obedience, curse for disobedience. The passage ends by placing all nations within a single covenantal framework — under YHWH's judgment and open to YHWH's mercy, not as Gentile outsiders but as peoples accountable to the same sovereign.

13.1: Chapter 13 introduces the first of Jeremiah's great sign-acts — symbolic actions the prophet performs at YHWH's command that embody the message in physical form. Thus YHWH said to me: Go and buy a linen loincloth and put it around your waist, and do not dip it in water. The linen belt (ezor pishtim) is a garment of intimate contact — worn against the skin, symbolizing intimate relationship. Linen was the priestly garment material (Lev 6:10; 16:4), connecting the image to sacred function. The prohibition against washing it is deliberate: this belt has never been ritually cleansed.
13.2: So I bought a loincloth according to the word of YHWH, and put it around my waist. Jeremiah's obedience is immediate and unquestioned — a pattern throughout the book's sign-act narratives (cf. ch. 19, 27–28, 32). The prophet performs the commanded action before understanding its interpretation, modeling the covenant response of hearing and doing. The physical action makes the message visible, embodied, and memorable in a predominantly oral culture.
13.3: And the word of YHWH came to me a second time, saying — the two-stage structure of the sign-act (do this → now do that) mirrors the narrative theology of the act. The first command establishes the object; the second command initiates the process of its ruin. The two-stage form is itself instructive: YHWH did not begin with destruction but with intimacy. The ruin of the relationship is a second movement, not the first.
13.4: Take the loincloth that you have bought, which is around your waist, and arise, go to the Euphrates and hide it there in a cleft of the rock. The Euphrates (Perat) is Babylon's river — the seat of the coming exile and the location of the nation that will destroy Judah. The act of hiding the belt at the Euphrates is a prophetic anticipation of what will happen to Judah: it will be carried off to Babylonian territory, hidden from view, left to deteriorate.
13.5: So I went and hid it by the Euphrates, as YHWH commanded me. Some commentators argue that the Euphrates reference is literal (requiring two long journeys of ~700 miles each) while others suggest it refers to Parah, a village near Anathoth (Josh 18:23), a much shorter journey. The symbolic weight of the Euphrates as the Babylonian exile destination likely drives the text regardless of the physical geography. What matters theologically is the direction of the concealment: toward Babylon, away from home.
13.6: And after many days YHWH said to me, Arise, go to the Euphrates, and take from there the loincloth that I commanded you to hide there. After many days — the time gap is significant. The deterioration is not instantaneous; it takes time. The exile will not happen overnight but through a process of increasingly severe judgments. The return to retrieve the ruined belt dramatizes the moment of exposure: what has been happening in secret will be revealed.
13.7: Then I went to the Euphrates, and dug, and I took the loincloth from the place where I had hidden it. And behold, the loincloth was spoiled; it was good for nothing. The belt retrieved from the earth and moisture is rotted, useless, destroyed. Nishchat (spoiled, ruined, corrupted) — the same verb used in ch. 18 for the clay vessel spoiled in the potter's hands. What was beautiful and functional is now valueless. This is the condition to which Judah's pride has led.
13.8: Then the word of YHWH came to me — the interpretive oracle follows the sign-act. This two-part structure (act then word) is characteristic of prophetic sign-acts throughout the Hebrew Bible. The act creates a question in the audience; the word answers it. The method is pedagogically powerful: the physical image remains in memory even when the verbal explanation fades.
13.9: Thus says YHWH: Even so will I spoil the pride of Judah and the great pride of Jerusalem. The ga'on (pride) that will be spoiled is not merely arrogance but the cultural, religious, and national self-confidence of Judah — the belief that YHWH's dwelling in Jerusalem made the city inviolable, that Temple sacrifice secured divine favor, that being Abraham's descendants guaranteed protection. This is the pride that Jeremiah has been attacking throughout the book. The loincloth's ruin is the pride's ruin.
13.10: This evil people, who refuse to hear my words, who stubbornly follow their own heart and have gone after other gods to serve them and worship them, shall be like this loincloth, which is good for nothing. The interpretation is explicit: the rotted belt equals the people who have abandoned the covenant. Stubbornly follow their own heart (sherirut libam) is the diagnostic phrase from 11:8. The people have chosen autonomy over covenant; the result is the same as the unburied belt — ruin in a foreign land.
13.11: For as the loincloth clings to the waist of a man, so I made the whole house of Israel and the whole house of Judah cling to me, declares YHWH, that they might be for me a people, a name, a praise, and a glory, but they would not listen. The positive purpose of the covenant relationship is stated with striking intimacy: Israel was meant to cling (davaq) to YHWH as a garment clings to the body — inseparable, intimate, bearing the shape of the one to whom it is attached. The covenant design was that Israel would be YHWH's glory before the nations. The tragedy is total: the design was perfect; the failure was the people's refusal to listen.
13.12: You shall speak to them this word: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel, 'Every jar is filled with wine'. A second sign — now involving ceramic jars and wine. The proverb every jar is filled with wine was apparently a familiar saying. The audience would likely respond with the obvious: yes, of course jars are made to hold wine. Jeremiah inverts their assumption: he is not speaking of literal wine-filling but of judgment filling.
13.13: And they will say to you, 'Do we not indeed know that every jar is filled with wine?' Then you shall say to them, 'Thus says YHWH: Behold, I will fill with drunkenness all the inhabitants of this land: the kings who sit on David's throne, the priests, the prophets, and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem'. The wine is not blessing but intoxication leading to staggering and falling — a classic biblical image of divine judgment (cf. Ps 60:3; Isa 51:17–23; Jer 25:15–29). Every level of Judahite society is included: kings, priests, prophets, ordinary inhabitants. The judgment is universal within the covenant community.
13.14: And I will dash them one against another, fathers and sons together, declares YHWH. I will not pity or spare or have compassion, that I should not destroy them. The violence of the image — family members dashed against one another — pictures the social collapse of invasion and siege. Civil order dissolves; families turn on each other. The triple negation of divine compassion (I will not pity, I will not spare, I will not have compassion) intensifies the finality: this is not a warning but an announcement. The time for compassion has passed.
13.15: Hear and give ear; be not proud, for YHWH has spoken. A direct appeal interrupts the judgment oracles. The exhortation to hear (shim'u veshe'ezinu) against pride (ga'avah) links back to the loincloth sign: the pride that will be ruined is first of all a refusal to listen. Hearing and humility are synonymous here; pride is the deafness that prevents the word of YHWH from penetrating the heart.
13.16: Give glory to YHWH your God before he brings darkness, before your feet stumble on the twilight mountains, and while you look for light he turns it into gloom and makes it deep darkness. Give glory is the call to honor YHWH rather than idols — the same competitive dynamic throughout the polemic of chs. 10–13. The twilight mountains (harei neshef) image the moment before night fully falls — the last moment of possible return. The coming darkness is the Babylonian invasion and exile. There is still a window of repentance, but it is closing.
13.17: But if you will not listen, my soul will weep in secret for your pride; my eyes will weep bitterly and run down with tears, because YHWH's flock has been taken captive. The prophet weeps in anticipation of the exile he cannot prevent. The weeping in secret (bimasterin tivkeh nafshi) suggests Jeremiah's tears are not performative grief for public effect but genuine anguish hidden from view. YHWH's flock taken captive: the shepherd-flock imagery from 10:21 returns — the scattered sheep are the exiled people, and the prophet weeps for them.
13.18: Say to the king and the queen mother: 'Take a lowly seat, for your beautiful crown has come down from your head'. The king addressed is likely Jehoiachin, who was taken to Babylon in 597 BC after a brief three-month reign (2 Kgs 24:8–17); the queen mother is Nehushta. The removal of the crown dramatizes the end of Davidic sovereignty — not permanently (the Davidic covenant remains in Jer 33:14–26) but in immediate historical terms. Humility (shafal) is the only posture available to those stripped of power.
13.19: The cities of the Negeb are shut up, with none to open them; all Judah is taken into exile, wholly taken into exile. The Negeb is the southern desert territory of Judah — the last geographic buffer before complete territorial loss. Once the Negeb cities are sealed (by siege or conquest), there is no exit. The repetition wholly taken into exile (galat shalom giltat) uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis — the totality of the deportation is stressed. No one escapes; every stratum of society is displaced.
13.20: Lift up your eyes and see those who come from the north. Where is the flock that was given you, your beautiful flock? — Jerusalem is addressed (feminine). The north enemy is the Babylonian army, visible on the horizon. The question about the beautiful flock is both geographical (where are your people?) and covenantal (where is the community entrusted to your shepherding?). The leaders of Jerusalem received a people to tend; they have lost them through unfaithfulness.
13.21: What will you say when they set as head over you those whom you yourself have taught to be friends to you? Will not pangs take hold of you like those of a woman in labor? — the allies Judah cultivated through diplomacy and treaty — Egypt, Assyria at various points — will be the instruments of her destruction. The ones taught as friends become masters. The labor pangs image for unexpected, overwhelming distress is recurring throughout the prophets (Isa 13:8; 26:17; Mic 4:9–10; 1 Thess 5:3).
13.22: And if you say in your heart, 'Why have these things come upon me?' it is for the greatness of your iniquity that your skirts are lifted up and you suffer violence. The question why has this come upon me? is the wrong question — a question of the willfully ignorant. The answer is direct: covenantal iniquity. The imagery of skirts lifted up is the language of violent exposure and sexual humiliation — frequently used in prophetic literature to describe military conquest and the public shame of a captured city-woman (cf. Nah 3:5; Ezek 16:37).
13.23: Can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard his spots? Then also you can do good who are accustomed to do evil. One of Scripture's most powerful statements on the depth of moral corruption. The rhetorical questions expect the answer no: skin and spots are unchangeable natural facts.
