The prophet is not denying the possibility of divine transformation (cf. Jer 31:33 — I will put my law within them; Ezek 36:26 — a new heart) but the possibility of self-generated moral reformation. Human beings accustomed to evil (limudei hara') cannot reform themselves from within. This anthropological realism drives the necessity of the New Covenant.
13.24: I will scatter you like chaff driven by the wind from the desert. The wind-scattering of chaff is a classic biblical image for judgment (Ps 1:4; Isa 17:13; 29:5; 41:16) — the lightweight, worthless remains of threshing carried off in all directions by the threshing-floor wind. Israel's scattering in exile is not random but purposive: the judgment of the covenant God who is also the lord of wind and weather.
13.25: This is your lot, the portion I have measured out to you, declares YHWH, because you have forgotten me and trusted in lies. The exile is described as a goral (lot, portion) — the same word used for land-allotments in Joshua, now applied to judgment. What was given as blessing (land as inheritance) becomes the template for what is taken away as curse. The theological root of the disaster is stated simply: you have forgotten me (vatishkechi oti) and trusted in lies (vativtechi beshaqer) — the two defections that summarize the entire book's indictment.
13.26: I myself will lift up your skirts over your face, and your shame will be seen. The divine action of public exposure (see v22) is now attributed directly to YHWH — not merely permitted but enacted. The shame of conquest is absorbed into the covenant-judgment framework: YHWH is not a passive observer of Babylon's violence against Jerusalem but the active agent behind it. This theological claim — that YHWH uses pagan nations as instruments of his judgment — is the most difficult and central claim of the entire prophetic tradition.
13.27: I have seen your abominations, your adulteries and neighings, your lewd whorings, on the hills in the field. Woe to you, O Jerusalem! How long will it be before you are made clean? The chapter closes with a woe-lament (oi lakh, woe to you) — the form of mourning over those already dead or doomed. The accumulated sexual imagery (adultery, neighing, whorings) depicts Israel's covenant infidelity as comprehensive, habitual, and public. The closing question — how long will it be before you are made clean? — leaves the chapter on a note of suspended anguish, neither promise nor condemnation but the grief of a God who has been betrayed by the people he chose and cannot simply unmake his love for them.

14.1: Chapter 14 introduces one of Jeremiah's most distinctive literary forms: the liturgy of drought, a communal lament structured around the people's cry, YHWH's response, and a second round of intercession. The superscription identifies the occasion: the word of YHWH that came to Jeremiah concerning the drought (devarim asher hayu el-yirmeyahu al-divrei habatzorot — literally, the matters of the droughts, plural). Drought in the ancient Near East was not merely meteorological disaster; it was a theological signal. Deuteronomy 28:22–24 listed drought and scorching heat as covenant curses for disobedience. The land's failure to rain is the land's testimony against the people.
14.2: Judah mourns and her gates languish; her people lament on the ground, and the cry of Jerusalem goes up. The corporate nature of the disaster is established immediately: Judah (the nation), her gates (the economic and legal centers where commerce and judgment occurred), her people, Jerusalem — the entire social structure is afflicted. The gates languishing (amlelu) is a vivid image: the places of civic life have fallen silent, their normal activity suspended by crisis. The cry going up (tza'aqat yerushalayim) echoes Exodus 2:23 — the enslaved Israelites' cry that reached YHWH. The question implicit in this echo is whether YHWH will respond again.
14.3: Their nobles send their servants for water; they come to the cisterns; they find no water; they return with their vessels empty. The social hierarchy is leveled by drought: even the adirim (nobles, great ones) cannot obtain water through their superior social position. Their servants return with empty jars. They are ashamed and confounded and cover their heads: the gesture of covering the head is a sign of mourning and shame (2 Sam 15:30; Est 6:12). The public humiliation of the powerful underscores the completeness of the disaster — drought does not respect social rank.
14.4: Because of the ground that is dismayed, since there is no rain on the land, the farmers are ashamed; they cover their heads. The shame extends down the social ladder from nobles to farmers. The ground itself is chatat (dismayed, shattered) — the same word used of human emotional devastation applied to the parched soil. Biblical ecology makes the land's suffering a direct reflection of the covenant community's condition. The farmers who work the ground share in its humiliation: without rain their labor is worthless.
14.5: Even the doe in the field forsakes her newborn fawn because there is no grass. The breakdown of instinctive maternal behavior is one of the most powerful images in the passage. The doe is not abandoning her young from cruelty but from necessity — there is nothing to sustain her milk production. When even animals cannot maintain the most basic instinct of parental care, the ecological crisis has reached its extreme. The image anticipates Lamentations 4:3–4, which uses the same motif for the siege of Jerusalem.
14.6: The wild donkeys stand on the bare heights; they pant for air like jackals; their eyes fail because there is no vegetation. Wild donkeys inhabited the desert fringes; during normal years they survived on sparse scrubland vegetation. Now even they suffer. The panting for air like jackals is an image of desperate respiratory distress — animals gasping in dry heat. Their eyes fail (kalu eineihem) describes the glazed, failing vision of animals dying from dehydration. The entire created order — noble, farmer, domestic animal, wild animal — suffers together.
14.7: Though our iniquities testify against us, act, O YHWH, for your name's sake; for our backslidings are many; we have sinned against you. The communal lament reaches its theological core: the people acknowledge that their iniquities have caused the drought. The confession is not merely formulaic; it is structurally necessary in the covenant framework. But the petition is grounded not in the people's merit (they explicitly acknowledge they have none) but in your name's sake (lema'an shemekha) — YHWH's own reputation, character, and covenantal commitment. Acting for his name's sake is acting consistently with who he has declared himself to be.
14.8: O you hope of Israel, its savior in time of trouble, why should you be like a stranger in the land, like a traveler who turns aside to tarry for a night? The lament form employs questions to press YHWH toward action. Hope of Israel (mikveh yisrael) — the same title Jeremiah will use in 17:13 — is a covenant claim: YHWH is Israel's reservoir of hope, the one they collect toward. A stranger and a traveler stopping for a night have no stake in the land — they observe suffering without commitment. The implied charge: YHWH appears uninvolved, indifferent, passing through rather than dwelling with his people.
14.9: Why should you be like a man confused, like a mighty warrior who cannot save? Yet you, O YHWH, are in the midst of us, and we are called by your name; do not leave us. The audacious image of YHWH as a confused warrior (gibor lo-yukhal lehoshi'a) is a rhetorical provocation — the community's complaint presses toward the edge of theological propriety. But it is ultimately reversed: you are in the midst of us, and we are called by your name. The appeal is to YHWH's indwelling presence (Temple theology) and name-claim (covenant relationship). These two facts — presence and name — are the bases for the final plea: do not leave us.
14.10: Thus says YHWH concerning this people: They have loved to wander; they have not restrained their feet; therefore YHWH does not accept them; now he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins. YHWH's response to the communal lament is stark refusal. The people's confession in v7 acknowledged backslidings; YHWH confirms them: they have loved to wander — the wandering is not accidental but volitional, habitual, beloved. The result is that the normal covenant mechanism of prayer-and-response does not function: YHWH does not accept them. The drought-lament is legitimate in form but cannot be effective given the state of the relationship.
14.11: YHWH said to me: Do not pray for the welfare of this people. The intercession-prohibition appears again (cf. 7:16; 11:14). The repetition of this prohibition is itself theologically significant: Jeremiah keeps wanting to intercede; YHWH keeps forbidding it. The prophet's instinct toward covenant solidarity with his people is genuine — but YHWH's judgment has passed the point where intercession can reverse it. Moses interceded successfully at the golden calf (Exod 32:11–14); Samuel interceded for Israel (1 Sam 12:23). But even those intercessors could not permanently forestall the consequences of persistent covenant violation (cf. 15:1).
14.12: Though they fast, I will not hear their cry, and though they offer burnt offering and grain offering, I will not accept them. But I will consume them by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence. The three instruments of covenant judgment — sword, famine, pestilence — form the triad that runs throughout Jeremiah (cf. 21:9; 24:10; 27:8; 29:17–18). Each represents a different vector of the curse sequence in Deuteronomy 28: military defeat (sword), agricultural failure (famine), epidemic disease (pestilence). The rejection of fasting and sacrifice as intercessory tools echoes Amos 5:21–24 and Isaiah 1:11–15: ritual performance cannot substitute for covenantal fidelity.
14.13: Then I said: Ah, Lord YHWH, behold, the prophets say to them, 'You shall not see the sword, nor shall you have famine, but I will give you assured peace in this place'. Jeremiah raises what seems like a mitigating factor: the false prophets are deceiving the people. If the people believe prophets who promise peace, can they be fully culpable? The defense is sincere but insufficient. Jeremiah is not excusing the people but pressing YHWH on the theological problem: the people are receiving contradictory messages — Jeremiah announces judgment, the other prophets announce peace. Who should the people believe?
14.14: And YHWH said to me: The prophets are prophesying lies in my name. I did not send them, nor did I command them or speak to them. They are prophesying to you a lying vision, worthless divination, and the deceit of their own minds. YHWH's answer clears away the potential defense: the false prophets are not licensed interpreters of ambiguous divine signals but frauds speaking from their own imagination. I did not send them (lo shalachtiim) is the central criterion of prophetic authority in Deuteronomy 18:20 and throughout Jeremiah (23:21; 27:15; 28:15; 29:9). The false prophet's crime is not merely error but deliberate misrepresentation of YHWH.
14.15: Therefore thus says YHWH concerning the prophets who prophesy in my name although I did not send them, and who say, 'Sword and famine shall not come upon this land': By sword and famine those prophets shall be consumed. Covenant logic applies to the false prophets with precision: they promise freedom from sword and famine; they will die by sword and famine. The punishment mirrors and refutes the false message. Deuteronomy 18:20 mandated death for prophets who spoke presumptuously in YHWH's name. The covenant legal system is being enforced.
14.16: And the people to whom they prophesy shall be cast out in the streets of Jerusalem, victims of famine and sword, with none to bury them — them, their wives, their sons, and their daughters. The false prophets and their audiences share the same fate. The absence of burial is one of the most severe of covenant curses — Deuteronomy 28:26 threatened that bodies would be food for birds and beasts, lying unburied. Burial was a fundamental duty of family and community honor in the ancient Near East; its deprivation represents the total breakdown of social fabric.
14.17: You shall say to them this word: 'Let my eyes run down with tears night and day, and let them not cease, for the virgin daughter of my people is shattered with a great wound, with a very grievous blow'. Jeremiah is commanded to weep publicly — the tears are not optional personal grief but a prophetically mandated expression. The virgin daughter of my people (betulat bat-ammi) is a standard personification for the nation in its vulnerability and innocence — a people who had a future, now shattered. The wound (shever gadol, great breaking) and blow (makah nachalah, very severe blow) convey the catastrophic magnitude of the coming disaster.
14.18: If I go out into the field, behold, those pierced by the sword! And if I enter the city, behold, the diseases of famine! For both prophet and priest ply their trade through the land and have no knowledge. A vision of the aftermath: the landscape outside the city is littered with battlefield corpses; the city interior is full of famine victims. The double horror (sword and famine, the judgment triad's two components) is everywhere. Prophet and priest ply their trade through the land and have no knowledge: the religious establishment continues its professional religious activity while the disaster unfolds around them — a devastating critique of institutionalized religion's capacity for moral blindness.
14.19: Have you utterly rejected Judah? Does your soul loathe Zion? Why have you struck us down so that there is no healing for us? We looked for peace, but no good came; for a time of healing, but behold, terror. A second communal lament resumes the liturgical pattern. The questions are more desperate than the first: Have you utterly rejected (ha-ma'os ma'asta — the infinitive absolute intensifies) — have you completely finished with us? Does your soul loathe Zion? The psychological image of divine disgust presses further than any earlier lament. The question of whether YHWH has crossed the point of no return is asked directly.
14.20: We acknowledge our wickedness, O YHWH, and the iniquity of our fathers, for we have sinned against you. The second confession is fuller than the first (v7): it acknowledges not only present sin but the iniquity of our fathers — the generational accumulation of covenant violation that the present generation has inherited and continued. This intergenerational confession mirrors Leviticus 26:40–41, which describes the restoration condition as confessing both their own sin and their fathers' transgression. The people are using the language of restoration, pressing toward the covenant's own mercy provisions.
14.21: Do not spurn us, for your name's sake; do not dishonor your glorious throne; remember and do not break your covenant with us. Three imperatives structure the plea: do not spurn, do not dishonor, remember. Each invokes a different aspect of YHWH's own self-commitment: your name's sake (his declared character), your glorious throne (his Zion dwelling-presence), your covenant (his binding commitment to Israel). The logic is: breaking the covenant would be an act of self-contradiction for YHWH. The people are not arguing from their own merit but from YHWH's obligations to himself.
14.22: Are there any among the false gods of the nations that can bring rain? Or can the heavens give showers? Are you not he, O YHWH our God? We will hope in you, for you have made all these things. The lament ends with a doxological affirmation of YHWH as the sole source of rain and fertility — a direct polemic against Baal, the Canaanite storm-and-rain deity. The rhetorical questions (are there any gods that can bring rain?) expect the answer no. The conclusion: only YHWH can end the drought, which means the drought-lament can only be addressed to YHWH. The final phrase you have made all these things grounds the hope in creation theology — the creator of weather controls weather.

15.1: Then YHWH said to me, Though Moses and Samuel stood before me, yet my heart would not turn toward this people. Send them out of my sight, and let them go. YHWH's answer to the second lament-cycle (14:19–22) is one of the most theologically sobering statements in all of Jeremiah. Moses and Samuel were the two great intercessors of Israel's history — Moses turned aside the golden-calf judgment (Exod 32:11–14) and Samuel prayed at Mizpah (1 Sam 7:5–11; 12:23). If even their intercession would be ineffective, no human prayer can reverse the coming judgment. The paradigm intercessors function here as the outer limit of what intercession can accomplish: even the best imaginable advocacy cannot obtain mercy for those whose covenant violation has crossed this threshold.
15.2: And when they ask you, 'Where shall we go?' you shall say to them, 'Thus says YHWH: Those who are for pestilence, to pestilence, and those who are for the sword, to the sword; those who are for famine, to famine, and those who are for captivity, to captivity'. The fourfold judgment triad expanded to include captivity. Each person will find their destined fate — the formula is almost mechanical, reflecting the precision of covenant-curse fulfillment. The people's question (where shall we go?) is existentially profound: when every direction means death or exile, there is nowhere to flee. The answer is not comfort but assignment.
15.3: I will appoint over them four kinds of destroyers, declares YHWH: the sword to kill, the dogs to tear, and the birds of the air and the beasts of the earth to devour and destroy. The four destroyers elaborate the judgment: sword (military death), dogs (scavenging corpses in the streets — deeply dishonorable in the ANE, cf. 1 Kgs 21:23–24 on Jezebel), birds of the air, and beasts. The image of unburied bodies consumed by dogs and birds is one of the most severe covenant-curse formulations in Deuteronomy 28:26 and appears repeatedly in the judgment oracles against kings (1 Kgs 14:11; 16:4).
15.4: And I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth because of what Manasseh the son of Hezekiah, king of Judah, did in Jerusalem. The specific naming of Manasseh is historically precise and theologically significant. Manasseh's 55-year reign (c. 697–642 BC) was the longest of any Judahite king and was characterized by the worst apostasy — he reversed Hezekiah's reforms, reinstated Baal altars, made his son pass through fire, practiced divination, and filled Jerusalem with innocent blood (2 Kgs 21:1–16). The Deuteronomistic History explicitly identifies Manasseh's sins as the cause of the Babylonian exile (2 Kgs 24:3–4). Even Josiah's reforms could not undo the generational damage.
15.5: Who will have pity on you, O Jerusalem, or who will grieve for you? Who will turn aside to ask about your welfare? The rhetorical questions function as a lament over a city already dead. The Hebrew menud (who will grieve) and yanud (who will turn aside) describe the gestures of condolence — nodding the head in sympathy, pausing to inquire. When no one performs these gestures, it signals total social abandonment. Jerusalem has lost the compassion of her neighbors, just as she lost the compassion of YHWH (v1). The city has been deserted on every side.
15.6: You have rejected me, declares YHWH; you keep going backward. So I have stretched out my hand against you and destroyed you — I am weary of relenting. I am weary of relenting (nileti hinachem) — one of the most striking divine self-descriptions in the Hebrew Bible. YHWH has repeatedly relented from judgment in response to prophetic intercession and national repentance (cf. Amos 7:1–6 where YHWH relents twice). But this relenting has been exploited — the people reformed briefly and then returned to apostasy. The exhaustion of divine patience is not a failure of divine love but the consequence of repeated manipulation of covenant mercy.
15.7: I have winnowed them with a winnowing fork in the gates of the land; I have bereaved them; I have destroyed my people; they did not turn from their ways. Winnowing — the process of throwing threshed grain into the air so the wind separates wheat from chaff — is used as a judgment image (cf. Ps 1:4; Isa 41:16; Jer 4:11). The gates of the land are the entry-points of the country — the places where people and trade flow. The judgment has already begun; YHWH has been sifting the nation. They did not turn from their ways: the purpose of earlier judgments was to produce repentance; they failed to do so.
15.8: I have made their widows more in number than the sand of the sea; I have brought against the mothers of young men a destroyer at noonday; I have made anguish and terror fall upon her suddenly. The accumulation of widows — women bereaved of husbands by military death — is measured against the sand metaphor (normally used for Abraham's innumerable descendants, Gen 22:17). The reversal is stark: where the promise was innumerable descendants, now the result is innumerable bereaved survivors. At noonday: ancient attacks typically came at dawn or dusk; a noonday assault removes even the comfort of predictable timing. The terror is total and sudden.
15.9: She who bore seven has grown faint; she has fainted away; her sun went down while it was yet day; she has been shamed and disgraced. And the rest of them I will give to the sword before their enemies, declares YHWH. She who bore seven — having seven sons was the pinnacle of maternal fulfillment in Israelite culture (cf. Ruth 4:15; 1 Sam 2:5). The woman with seven sons represents the most blessed of mothers. Even she will lose everything. Her sun went down while it was yet day: the image of untimely death — the sun that should have shone through a full day setting at noon. The contrast between expected flourishing and actual devastation could not be sharper.
15.10: Woe is me, my mother, that you bore me, a man of strife and contention to the whole land! I have not lent, nor have I borrowed, yet all of them curse me. Jeremiah's confession (the Confessions of Jeremiah — 11:18–23; 12:1–6; 15:10–21; 17:14–18; 18:19–23; 20:7–18) begins here in full force. The prophet wishes he had never been born — a sentiment that appears in Job 3 and reaches its NT echo in the cry of dereliction. The complaint that he is cursed by everyone despite personal financial integrity highlights the social isolation of the prophet's role: the messenger bearing unwelcome news is held responsible for the news itself.
15.11: YHWH said: Have I not set you free for their good? Have I not pleaded for you before the enemy in the time of trouble and in the time of distress? YHWH's response to Jeremiah's lament is a reminder of divine protection. The verse is difficult to translate but the thrust is reassurance: YHWH has been and will be Jeremiah's advocate. The prophet's suffering does not occur because YHWH has abandoned him but within a larger purpose — his ministry will ultimately benefit those who now oppose him. This is covenant protection of the covenant messenger.
15.12: Can iron break iron from the north, and bronze? The rhetorical question invokes the metallurgical metaphor: the iron and bronze from the north (Babylon and its weapons) cannot be broken by ordinary weapons. The coming enemy is stronger than anything Judah can resist. The only option is not military resistance but submission to the covenant judgment YHWH is directing. This connects to Jeremiah's later counsel to surrender to Babylon (27:8–11) as the theologically appropriate response to YHWH's judgment-through-Nebuchadnezzar.
15.13: Your wealth and your treasures I will give as spoil, without price, for all your sins, throughout all your territory. The confiscation of Judah's accumulated wealth by Babylon is framed as covenant confiscation — YHWH is taking back what he gave, because the covenant conditions for possession have been violated. Without price means the wealth will be given away cheaply, treated as worthless plunder rather than valued treasure. The reversal of the promised blessing of material abundance (Deut 28:3–8) is total.
15.14: I will make you serve your enemies in a land that you do not know, for in my anger a fire is kindled that shall burn forever. Exile service under foreign masters is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:47–48 — the failure to serve YHWH with joy and gladness results in serving enemies in deprivation. A fire that shall burn forever (esh qadechah ad-olam tuqad): the hyperbolic permanence of divine wrath communicates the severity of the judgment, not necessarily its eternal duration — olam in the Hebrew Bible frequently means an indefinitely long period rather than strict eternity.
15.15: O YHWH, you know; remember me and visit me, and take vengeance for me on my persecutors. In your forbearance take me not away; know that for your sake I bear reproach. Jeremiah's petition pivots from complaint to covenant appeal. You know (attah yadeta) — not merely cognitive knowledge but YHWH's intimate, caring attention. Remember me and visit me: the covenant language of divine attention (cf. Gen 8:1 — God remembered Noah; Gen 21:1 — YHWH visited Sarah). Jeremiah asks to be remembered before he is consumed by his persecutors. For your sake I bear reproach: the prophet's suffering is not personal but vocational — he is shamed because he serves YHWH.
15.16: Your words were found, and I ate them, and your words became to me a joy and the delight of my heart, for I am called by your name, O YHWH, God of hosts. One of the most beautiful verses in Jeremiah. The eating of the word recalls Ezekiel 2–3 (Ezekiel literally eats the scroll) and anticipates Revelation 10:9–10 (John eats the scroll — sweet in the mouth, bitter in the stomach). For Jeremiah the word of YHWH was initially pure joy — the delight of divine communication, the honor of bearing YHWH's name. The tension with vv10, 17–18 is intentional: the prophet's calling began in joy and has descended into isolation.
15.17: I did not sit in the company of merrymakers, nor did I rejoice; I sat alone, because your hand was upon me, for you had filled me with indignation. The social isolation of Jeremiah's prophetic calling is stated plainly. He could not participate in normal communal celebration because the word he carried — impending doom — made celebration impossible. Your hand was upon me: the prophetic compulsion that Jeremiah cannot evade (cf. 20:7–9 — the word burning in his bones). Filled me with indignation: YHWH's own moral outrage at Judah's apostasy has been communicated to and taken up by the prophet, making normal social participation impossible.
15.18: Why is my pain unceasing, my wound incurable, refusing to be healed? Will you be to me like a deceitful brook, like waters that fail? The most daring of Jeremiah's accusations against YHWH. The deceitful brook (nachal akhzav) — the wadis of Palestine flow in winter from rainfall and run dry in summer precisely when most needed. YHWH has seemed to Jeremiah like a wadi that offered water but dried up at the moment of greatest need. The accusation verges on blasphemy by the standards of conventional piety — but it is the lament tradition at its most honest. The Psalms of lament (Ps 22, 88) use similar language of divine abandonment.
15.19: Therefore thus says YHWH: If you return, I will restore you, and you shall stand before me. If you utter what is precious, and not what is worthless, you shall be as my mouth. They shall turn to you, but you shall not turn to them. YHWH's response challenges Jeremiah's own spiritual state. The prophet who has been accusing YHWH of being like a failing wadi needs to return himself — the call to return (teshuvah) applies to the prophet, not just the people. Utter what is precious, not what is worthless: distinguish between the authentic divine word and the complaints that sound like the false prophets. The final reversal — they shall turn to you, not you to them — is the proper posture of the prophet vis-à-vis the people.
15.20: And I will make you to this people a fortified wall of bronze; they will fight against you, but they shall not prevail over you, for I am with you to save you and deliver you, declares YHWH. The renewal of Jeremiah's commission echoes 1:18–19 almost verbatim: I have made you this day a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls. The bronze wall is not personal invulnerability — Jeremiah will suffer imprisonment, physical abuse, and near-death — but rather that his prophetic mission will not be ultimately defeated. The enemies who fight against him will not prevail because YHWH is his defender.
15.21: I will deliver you out of the hand of the wicked and redeem you from the grasp of the ruthless. The covenant rescue language (padah, redeem; natsal, deliver) closes the divine response. YHWH is promising covenant protection for his covenant messenger — not escape from suffering but ultimate vindication. The fulfilled form of this promise is visible in Jeremiah's rescue from the cistern (38:6–13), his protection by Ebed-melech, and his survival of Jerusalem's fall when others perished.

16.1: Chapter 16 introduces one of the most striking aspects of Jeremiah's prophetic vocation: he is commanded not merely to speak judgment but to embody it in his own life. The word of YHWH came to me — the chapter contains three divine prohibitions that will make Jeremiah a living sign-act before the people.
16.2: You shall not take a wife, nor shall you have sons or daughters in this place. The prohibition of marriage and family is unprecedented in the prophetic literature. The symbolic logic is direct: in a city under judgment, bringing children into the world is an act of hope; Jeremiah's childlessness enacts the hopelessness of Jerusalem's immediate future. In a culture where having descendants was the primary form of social immortality and covenant continuity, celibacy was a form of public mourning. Jeremiah's unmarried state is a walking sermon.
16.3: For thus says YHWH concerning the sons and daughters who are born in this place, and concerning the mothers who bore them and the fathers who fathered them in this land. The command is grounded in what is about to happen to children born in Jerusalem. The verse does not specify the disaster but makes clear that children born now will face a fate worse than their parents'. Jeremiah's refusal to have children is not selfish but compassionate: he is being spared the anguish of watching his own children suffer the coming destruction.
16.4: They shall die of deadly diseases. They shall not be lamented, nor shall they be buried. They shall be as dung on the surface of the ground. They shall perish by the sword and by famine, and their dead bodies shall be food for the birds of the air and for the beasts of the earth. The catalogue of horrors — disease, no lamentation, no burial, desecration as dung, sword, famine, bodies consumed by birds and beasts — is comprehensive. The absence of lamentation and burial means the deaths will not even receive the minimal dignity of communal mourning. This is covenant curse at its most extreme, recalling Deuteronomy 28:26.
16.5: For thus says YHWH: Do not enter the house of mourning, or go to lament or grieve for them, for I have taken away my peace from this people, my steadfast love and mercy, declares YHWH. The second prohibition: Jeremiah must not participate in mourning rites. The house of mourning (beit marzeach — possibly connected to the marzeach institution, a communal feast associated with death and ancestors) was the normal context for communal grief. His absence from mourning symbolizes the withdrawal of divine compassion: I have taken away my peace, my steadfast love and mercy (shalom, chesed, rachamim) — the three covenant attributes YHWH withdraws from a people who have exhausted his patience.
16.6: Both great and small shall die in this land. They shall not be buried, and no one shall lament for them or cut himself or make himself bald for them. The mourning practices mentioned — cut himself and make himself bald — were prohibited for Israelites in Leviticus 19:28 and Deuteronomy 14:1, yet were clearly practiced in some form as grief expressions (cf. Jer 41:5; Amos 8:10). The absence of even these (prohibited but culturally present) mourning gestures indicates a disaster so total that normal grief protocols collapse. When everyone is dead, there is no one to mourn the dead.
16.7: No one shall break bread for the mourner, to comfort him for the dead, nor shall anyone give him the cup of consolation to drink for his father or his mother. The provision of food and drink to mourners was a practical form of communal solidarity — bereaved families who could not cook for themselves were fed by neighbors. This meal of consolation (lechem anashim, bread of men) is attested in ancient Near Eastern contexts as a fundamental obligation of community. Its absence signals not merely poverty but the collapse of the social bonds that make community possible.
16.8: You shall not go into the house of feasting to sit with them, to eat and drink. The third prohibition: Jeremiah must not participate in communal celebrations either. He is barred from mourning (v5) and from feasting (v8) — excluded from the full range of communal life. His isolation is total and deliberate. The prophet who cannot mourn with mourners or celebrate with celebrants is a visible rupture in the social fabric, a walking sign that normal communal life is about to end.
16.9: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I will silence in this place, before your eyes and in your days, the voice of mirth and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride. The silencing of wedding celebration is a specific covenant-curse indicator. The voice of the bridegroom and the bride appears again in Jeremiah 7:34; 25:10; 33:11 — in 33:11 its return marks the restoration of covenant joy. Its silencing here marks the suspension of normal life. Wedding songs were the most public expression of communal hope; their absence signals the death of hope.
16.10: And when you tell this people all these words, and they say to you, 'Why has YHWH pronounced all this great evil against us? What is our iniquity? What is the sin that we have committed against YHWH our God?' The anticipated question from the people is one of self-justifying incomprehension: they genuinely do not see the connection between their behavior and the announced disaster. This is not entirely disingenuous — generations of religious syncretism had normalized practices that YHWH condemned. The question provides Jeremiah an opportunity to deliver the full covenant indictment.
16.11: Then you shall say to them: Because your fathers have forsaken me, declares YHWH, and have gone after other gods and have served and worshiped them, and have forsaken me and have not kept my law. The diagnosis is generational: the fathers began the apostasy. The present generation has inherited a corrupted religious environment. Forsaken YHWH… gone after other gods… forsaken YHWH… not kept my law — the double forsook (azavuni) forms a rhetorical bracket emphasizing abandonment as the core offense.
16.12: And you have done worse than your fathers, for behold, every one of you follows his stubborn, evil will, refusing to listen to me. The present generation is not merely the passive recipient of ancestral sin — they have intensified it. Every one of you follows his stubborn, evil will (sherirut lev hara' — recurring Jeremianic phrase): the hardened disposition has become universal and individual. The aggravating factor — you have done worse than your fathers — removes the possible defense that the people were simply caught in inherited patterns.
16.13: Therefore I will hurl you out of this land into a land that neither you nor your fathers have known, and there you shall serve other gods day and night, for I will show you no favor. The punishment fits the crime with covenantal precision: they chose to serve foreign gods while in the covenant land; they will serve foreign gods in a foreign land — but under compulsion rather than choice, and without any prospect of the divine favor they currently enjoy in Canaan. The exile is a perverse fulfillment of their own desires: you wanted foreign gods, you will be immersed in them.
16.14: Therefore, behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when it shall no longer be said, 'As YHWH lives who brought up the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt'. A remarkable intrusion of restoration hope into the judgment section. The Exodus was Israel's foundational confession — the formative act of divine redemption that defined YHWH's character and Israel's identity. To say that a new act of redemption will supersede it in memory is an extraordinary theological claim. The new event will be so significant that the Exodus — the reference point for all divine saving action — will recede from primary position.
16.15: But 'As YHWH lives who brought up the people of Israel out of the north country and out of all the countries where he had driven them.' For I will bring them back to their own land that I gave to their fathers. The new Exodus will be a return from the north country — Babylon — and from all the nations of the diaspora. The scope is explicitly global: all the countries where he had driven them. This is not merely a local restoration but a worldwide regathering. The New Testament understands this promise through the lens of Christ's gathering of the scattered children of God from all nations (John 11:52; Eph 2:11–22).
16.16: Behold, I am sending for many fishermen, declares YHWH, and they shall catch them. And afterward I will send for many hunters, and they shall hunt them from every mountain and every hill, and out of the clefts of the rocks. The fishermen and hunters in the context of vv14–15 are the agents of regathering — those who will seek out the scattered diaspora and bring them back. Some interpreters read these as instruments of judgment (hunting them down for punishment), others as agents of restoration. The context of the new-Exodus promise favors restoration: the scattered will be actively sought and returned.
16.17: For my eyes are on all their ways. They are not hidden from me, nor is their iniquity concealed from my eyes. YHWH's omniscience is the guarantor both of judgment (nothing is hidden from the one who judges) and of restoration (no exile is too remote to be found and gathered). The dual application of divine sight — seeing sin for judgment and seeing the scattered for restoration — is a characteristic of biblical covenant theology: the same attribute that makes YHWH a terrifying judge makes him a faithful redeemer.
16.18: But first I will doubly repay their iniquity and their sin, because they have polluted my land with the carcasses of their detestable idols, and have filled my inheritance with their abominations. The double-repayment (mishne) is the legal penalty for aggravated theft in Exodus 22:4, 7 — paying back twice what was stolen. Applied to covenant violation, it means the people will experience the full consequences of their apostasy compounded. The land-pollution framing is significant: idols are described as carcasses (nivelat) — dead bodies contaminating the ground — a term of maximum ritual impurity applied to objects the people treated as sacred.
16.19: O YHWH, my strength and my stronghold, my refuge in the day of trouble, to you shall the nations come from the ends of the earth and say: Our fathers have inherited nothing but lies, worthless things in which there is no profit. The nations' future confession of the worthlessness of their inherited gods echoes and extends the idol-polemic of chapter 10. The phrase from the ends of the earth makes this a truly universal eschatological vision: every people, having inherited religious systems built on falsehood, will abandon them for YHWH. The nations' self-condemnation (our fathers have inherited lies) mirrors Israel's own indictment throughout the book.
16.20: Can man make for himself gods? Such are not gods! The rhetorical question captures the core epistemological absurdity of idol-worship: a manufactured object cannot be a god. The nations will eventually recognize what Israel should have known all along. The verse is structurally a coda to the idol-polemic sequence that began in chapter 10 and resurfaces throughout chapters 14–16.
16.21: Therefore, behold, I will make them know, this once I will make them know my power and my might, and they shall know that my name is YHWH. The chapter closes with a declaration of divine self-revelation through judgment. They shall know that my name is YHWH — the recognition formula, prominent throughout Ezekiel, signals that the purpose of the coming events (judgment, exile, restoration) is ultimately revelatory: YHWH is making himself known to both Israel and the nations through what he is doing in history. The new-Exodus events will produce covenant knowledge where previously there was only habitual religious indifference.

17.1: The sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of a diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart, and on the horns of their altars. Chapter 17 opens with an indelibility metaphor. The iron stylus (et barzel) and diamond point (tsiporen shamir) are the hardest available writing instruments — used to inscribe on rock, not parchment. The sin of Judah is not a light smudge easily erased but a deep incision on the tablet of their heart — an ironic inversion of Proverbs 3:3 (write these things on the tablet of your heart) and anticipating the New Covenant of Jeremiah 31:33 (I will write my law on their hearts). The altars bear the same inscription: their worship system itself is marked with sin.
17.2: While their children remember their altars and their Asherah poles, beside every green tree and on the high hills. The intergenerational transmission of idolatry: their children remember their altars — the children who grow up watching parents worship at the Asherah poles and high places inherit a religious memory oriented away from YHWH. The green tree and high hill are the standard markers of Canaanite sacred sites throughout the Deuteronomistic History. The landscape of Judah has been spiritually colonized by these sites, and children are being shaped by their geography.
17.3: O my mountain in the open country — your wealth and all your treasures I will give for spoil as the price of your sin throughout all your territory. The address shifts to Judah as YHWH's mountain-possession — once his treasure, now plunder. The wealth and treasures will be taken by Babylon as the covenant price of Judah's sin. The accumulated material blessing of YHWH's covenant faithfulness (Deut 28:3–8) is transferred to enemies as the accumulated material curse of covenant violation (Deut 28:43–44).
17.4: You shall loosen your hand from your heritage that I gave to you, and I will make you serve your enemies in a land that you do not know, for in my anger a fire is kindled that shall burn forever. The loss of the covenant land is stated as a compelled act: Judah will loosen her hand from the inheritance YHWH gave — a legal image of releasing a property claim. The exile will mean serving enemies in an unknown land (cf. 15:14). The fire burning forever reiterates the hyperbolic severity of covenant judgment that will not be easily or quickly reversed.
17.5: Thus says YHWH: Cursed is the man who trusts in man and makes flesh his strength, whose heart turns away from YHWH. The wisdom section (vv5–8) presents a classic two-ways contrast that parallels Psalm 1 closely. The curse (arur) falls on the one who trusts in basar (flesh) — specifically, human military and political alliances. In Jeremiah's context this is the policy debate: should Judah trust Egypt and other human allies against Babylon, or submit to YHWH's judgment? Those who counsel trust in human power are cursed because their heart turns from YHWH — the fundamental apostasy expressed as foreign policy.
17.6: He is like a shrub in the desert, and shall not see any good come. He shall dwell in the parched places of the wilderness, in an uninhabited salt land. The one who trusts in human strength is like a desert shrub — stunted, unable to access water, existing in barren isolation. The salt land (eretz melechah) is completely infertile. Against the lush imagery of the blessed man (v8), the cursed man's image is stark: he has chosen the most inhospitable environment possible by trusting in what cannot sustain life.
17.7: Blessed is the man who trusts in YHWH, whose trust is YHWH. The blessing (baruk) is the counterpart of v5's curse. The double expression trusts in YHWH, whose trust is YHWH is emphatic — YHWH himself is the trust, not merely the object of trust. This matches the vocabulary of the lament in 14:8 (hope of Israel, mikveh yisrael) — YHWH as the reservoir toward which trust flows. The Psalm 1 parallel is clear: the blessed man's alternative is not merely different behavior but a different relational grounding.
17.8: He is like a tree planted by water, that sends out its roots by the stream, and does not fear when heat comes, for its leaves remain green, and is not anxious in the year of drought, for it does not cease to bear fruit. The image of the tree planted by streams appears in Psalm 1:3 with nearly identical language, suggesting a shared wisdom tradition. The tree's key feature is its connection to a water source that is independent of surface rainfall: even in drought (shanah batzoret — the same word as the drought of ch. 14), the deep-rooted tree has access to underground water and keeps bearing fruit. Trust in YHWH provides access to resources inaccessible to the flesh-trusting shrub.
17.9: The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick; who can understand it? One of Scripture's most cited verses on human anthropology. The Hebrew aqov halev mikol umiyashenu — the heart is more crooked than all things, and desperately weak/sick. Aqov (crooked, deceitful) shares a root with Jacob (ya'aqov) — the heel-grasper, the supplanter. The heart has the Jacob-quality of crooked self-interest. Anush (desperately sick) is terminal illness language. The rhetorical question — who can understand it? — is not rhetorical despair but preparation for the answer in v10: YHWH alone can assess the heart.
17.10: I the LORD search the heart and test the mind, to give every man according to his ways, according to the fruit of his deeds. YHWH's answer to the unknowable heart: he is the one who searches (choqer lev, examines the heart) and tests (bachen kelayot, tries the kidneys — the seat of deep emotion). This is the basis for covenant judgment that is truly just: human judges cannot see the inner life; YHWH can. The rendering according to his ways, according to the fruit of his deeds is covenant recompense — what a person sows in their inner and outer life, they reap in consequence.
17.11: Like the partridge that gathers a brood that she did not hatch, so is he who gets riches but not by justice; in the mid of his days they will leave him, and at his end he will be a fool. The partridge proverb illustrates the impermanence of unjustly acquired wealth. The partridge was believed to steal other birds' eggs and raise them as her own — but the chicks eventually recognize their true nature and leave. The wealth gathered by injustice has the same quality: it doesn't belong to the one who holds it, and it will depart. In the context of Jerusalem's impending collapse, the wealthy who have accumulated through exploitation will lose everything.
17.12: A glorious throne set on high from the beginning is the place of our sanctuary. A brief doxological interjection: YHWH's throne in the Temple is exalted from the beginning (merom merishon makom miqdashenu). The contrast with the crooked human heart (v9) and unjust wealth (v11) is the transcendent stability of YHWH's throne. The Temple as the location of divine sovereignty provides the anchor for Jeremiah's covenant theology: even amid judgment, the divine throne remains exalted.
17.13: O YHWH, the hope of Israel, all who forsake you shall be put to shame; those who turn away from you shall be written in the earth, for they have forsaken YHWH, the fountain of living waters. The hope of Israel (mikveh yisrael) recurs from 14:8. Written in the earth (beyeretzu yikatevu) contrasts with the heavenly register (the Book of Life concept): those who forsake YHWH are inscribed in dust, not in permanent record — their existence is temporary and their fate is oblivion. Fountain of living waters (meqor mayim chayyim) reappears from 2:13 — YHWH as the source of life-giving water which the people have abandoned for leaking cisterns.
17.14: Heal me, O YHWH, and I shall be healed; save me, and I shall be saved, for you are my praise. Jeremiah's personal lament resumes. The double emphasis — heal me… and I shall be healed; save me… and I shall be saved — insists that divine action is the only effective source of restoration. Human remedies for spiritual and physical illness are insufficient; YHWH's action alone heals completely. You are my praise (tehilati attah) identifies YHWH as the only proper object of Jeremiah's testimony — his whole life is a witness to YHWH's character.
17.15: Behold, they say to me, 'Where is the word of YHWH? Let it come!' The mockers challenge Jeremiah's prophetic credibility: years of announcing judgment without visible fulfillment have made his predictions seem empty. The challenge let it come is sarcastic — they are daring the judgment to arrive. This is the standard test for prophetic authenticity in Deuteronomy 18:21–22: if the predicted event does not occur, the prophet is false. The people are using the delay of Babylon's arrival as evidence against Jeremiah.
17.16: I have not run away from being your shepherd, nor have I desired the day of disaster. You know what came out of my lips; it was before your face. Jeremiah defends the sincerity of his prophetic ministry. He has not run from being shepherd (some translate hastened the day of disaster) — he did not relish announcing doom. You know what came out of my lips: the prophet's words are fully transparent before YHWH, who can verify that Jeremiah has spoken only what he was given, not added personal hostility to the divine message.
17.17: Be not a terror to me; you are my refuge in the day of evil. The petition is directed at YHWH himself — the prophet who fears his persecutors prays that YHWH will not be an additional source of terror. You are my refuge (machsi attah) — a Psalmic assertion of divine protection (Ps 46:1; 91:2) now placed on Jeremiah's lips. Even while accusing YHWH of being like a failing wadi (15:18), Jeremiah continues to pray to YHWH as his refuge. The tension is authentic rather than resolved.
17.18: Let those be put to shame who persecute me, but let me not be put to shame; let them be dismayed, but let me not be dismayed; bring upon them the day of evil; destroy them with double destruction! The imprecatory close of Jeremiah's lament. The prayer for double destruction (mishne shivron) on his persecutors mirrors the double-repayment vocabulary of covenant law (v18 of ch. 16). Jeremiah is not calling for personal revenge but for covenant enforcement: those who persecute YHWH's messenger should receive what they have attempted to bring on the messenger. The imprecatory psalms and prophetic prayers are covenant prayers, not personal vindictiveness.
17.19: Thus said YHWH to me: Go and stand in the People's Gate, by which the kings of Judah enter and by which they go out, and in all the gates of Jerusalem. The final section of chapter 17 shifts to a specific public mission: Jeremiah is to preach Sabbath observance at the city gates. The People's Gate (possibly the gate through which the general public entered, as opposed to a royal or priestly entrance) and all the gates of Jerusalem ensure maximum public exposure. The choice of gates as pulpit is significant: gates were the nerve centers of civic life — commerce, legal proceedings, military passage all occurred there.
17.20: And say to them: Hear the word of YHWH, you kings of Judah, and all Judah, and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem who enter by these gates. The audience is comprehensive: kings, all Judah, all Jerusalem. The Sabbath-gate sermon addresses every stratum of society. The king's obedience or disobedience to Sabbath is as relevant as the commoner's — there is no social exemption from covenant obligations. The inclusion of kings (plural) may reflect the political instability of Jeremiah's period, with multiple short-reigned kings.
17.21: Thus says YHWH: Take care for the sake of your lives, and do not bear a burden on the Sabbath day or bring it in by the gates of Jerusalem. Sabbath commerce — carrying goods through the city gates on the seventh day — is the specific violation Jeremiah targets. The Sabbath was an economic day of rest: no buying, selling, or trade activity. In a market-city like Jerusalem, where the gates were the commercial entry-points, Sabbath observance required deliberate suspension of the economic rhythms that defined daily life. The phrase for the sake of your lives (bishmarot nafshoteikhem) makes Sabbath-keeping a survival issue, not merely a piety requirement.
17.22: And do not carry a burden out of your houses on the Sabbath or do any work, but keep the Sabbath day holy, as I commanded your fathers. The Sabbath command is grounded in the original Decalogue obligation (Exod 20:8–11; Deut 5:12–15) — the ancestral fathers were commanded to keep it holy. The present generation is not being given new legislation but called back to foundational covenant practice. The work-prohibition extends from the gates (public commerce) to the houses (private labor), covering the full range of economic activity.
17.23: Yet they did not listen or incline their ear, but stiffened their neck, that they might not hear and receive instruction. The historical record is entered as evidence: Judah's ancestors received the same Sabbath command and refused it. Stiffened their neck (hikshachafam) — the resistance is physical, deliberate, embodied. The neck metaphor describes the stubborn animal that refuses the yoke — covenant as yoke, refusal as stiff-necked rebellion. Exodus 32:9 uses the same image for the golden calf generation.
17.24: But if you listen to me, declares YHWH, and bring in no burden by the gates of this city on the Sabbath day, but keep the Sabbath day holy and do no work on it. The conditional restoration formula: if you listen (im shamo'a tishme'un — the emphatic infinitive absolute + finite verb). The Sabbath is presented as a test case for the fundamental covenant question of hearing and obeying YHWH. It is a weekly, visible, measurable act of covenant faithfulness — or unfaithfulness. The Sabbath thus functions as a kind of covenant thermometer.
17.25: Then there shall enter by the gates of this city kings and princes who sit on the throne of David, riding in chariots and on horses, they and their officials, the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem. And this city shall be inhabited forever. The restoration promise is remarkable in its specificity: Sabbath observance will result in stable Davidic kingship and permanent city habitation. The promise connects Sabbath to the entire covenant-blessing complex: political stability (kings and princes on David's throne), military honor (chariots and horses), permanent inhabitation. The link between Sabbath and national flourishing is direct — not magical but covenantal.
17.26: And people shall come from the cities of Judah and the places around Jerusalem, from the land of Benjamin, from the Shephelah, from the hill country, and from the Negeb, bringing burnt offerings and sacrifices, grain offerings and frankincense, and bringing thank offerings to the house of YHWH. The geographic comprehensiveness of the restoration: the full tribal territory of Judah — Shephelah (lowlands), hill country, Negeb (south), Benjamin (north) — will be represented at the Temple. The sacrificial system will function as designed: worship from every corner of the covenant land flowing to the Jerusalem sanctuary. The picture is of a fully restored covenant community.
17.27: But if you do not listen to me, to keep the Sabbath day holy, and not to bear a burden and enter by the gates of Jerusalem on the Sabbath day, then I will kindle a fire in its gates, and it shall devour the palaces of Jerusalem and shall not be quenched. The threat closes the chapter with covenant symmetry: the same gates through which Sabbath-violating commerce flows will be consumed by fire. The palaces of Jerusalem — symbols of royal power and wealth — will be incinerated. Shall not be quenched echoes the unquenchable fire of v4 and 15:14. The fire of divine judgment, once kindled by accumulated covenant violation, cannot be extinguished by human effort — only by repentance and covenant return, the alternative offered in the preceding verses.

18.1: Chapter 18 introduces one of Jeremiah's most celebrated sign-acts and one of the Old Testament's richest theological metaphors: the word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH: Arise, and go down to the potter's house, and there I will let you hear my words. The potter and clay is an ancient Near Eastern image with wide currency — in Egypt, the god Khnum was depicted as a potter who shaped human beings on a wheel, and Mesopotamian creation texts describe humanity as molded from clay. Israel inherits this material but radically transforms it: the clay is not inert material shaped by capricious divine whim, but a morally responsive community whose own response shapes what the potter does.
18.2: So I went down to the potter's house, and there he was working at his wheel. Jeremiah simply goes and observes. The ceramic workshop was a familiar part of ancient Near Eastern towns — archaeologists have excavated potter's quarters with kilns, clay storage, and wheel-pits. The two-stage wheel (ovnayyim — the two stones) was foot-powered, creating the rotation that allowed shaping. The prophet watches the potter at his ordinary craft and receives a theological revelation from the mundane.
18.3: And the vessel he was making of clay was spoiled in the potter's hand, and he reworked it into another vessel, as it seemed good to the potter to do. The key observation: the spoiled vessel (nishchat haklei asher hu oseh betit) is not discarded but reworked. The clay that fails to hold its intended shape under the potter's hands is not thrown out — it is reshaped into a different vessel. This ordinary workshop practice becomes the theological crux: the spoiled vessel can become something else. The question is whether Israel is still at this reformable stage or has already been fired into a hardened, breakable vessel (cf. ch. 19).
18.4: Verse 4 presents the sign-act itself, observed before the interpretation follows. The potter's freedom to reshape what has been spoiled is presented as a matter of the potter's own will: as it seemed good to the potter to do. The sovereign freedom of the craftsman is absolute with unfired clay. This is not arbitrary power but skilled judgment — the potter knows clay, knows its limits, and knows what it can become. The theological application will follow in v6: YHWH's sovereignty over Israel is analogous but morally conditioned in a way no human potter's is.
18.5: Then the word of YHWH came to me — the interpretive oracle follows the observed action. The two-stage structure (observe → receive interpretation) mirrors other sign-act narratives in the prophets (cf. Ezek 37:1–14, where Ezekiel observes the valley of dry bones before receiving the interpretation). The observation is not optional: YHWH sends Jeremiah to see before he tells him. The visual image anchors the theological truth in embodied reality.
18.6: O house of Israel, can I not do with you as this potter has done? declares YHWH. Behold, like the clay in the potter's hand, so are you in my hand, O house of Israel. The application is direct but carefully stated: YHWH can do with Israel what the potter does with clay. The analogy is about possibility and sovereignty, not about mechanism — Israel is not literally clay. The question can I not do (halo kachozeh hazeh uchal la'asot) expects the answer yes, you can. YHWH's sovereign freedom to reshape Israel is asserted as a fact the people cannot deny.
18.7: If at any time I declare concerning a nation or a kingdom, that I will pluck up and break down and destroy it. The theological extension is remarkable: the potter-clay principle is not limited to Israel but applies to any nation or kingdom. YHWH's sovereign authority is universal. The three verbs — pluck up, break down, destroy (natash, nathots, he'avid) — are the three negative actions from Jeremiah's call (1:10), now embedded in a framework of conditional rather than absolute judgment.
18.8: And if that nation, concerning which I have spoken, turns from its evil, I will relent of the disaster that I intended to do to it. The divine flexibility is stated explicitly: announced judgment is not irrevocable. If the nation repents (shav meraatah, turns from its evil), YHWH relents (nichamti, is consoled, changes his mind). The language of divine nacham (repentance/relenting) is theologically significant and was exploited by false prophets to claim that any judgment oracle could be avoided by any degree of ritual penitence. Jeremiah's context makes clear this relenting is conditioned on genuine turning — cf. Jonah 3:10, where Nineveh's genuine repentance produces YHWH's relenting.
18.9: And if at any time I declare concerning a nation or a kingdom that I will build and plant it. The positive trajectory: YHWH also announces blessing on nations — the two remaining positive terms from 1:10 (build, plant). Divine promises of blessing are equally subject to the same moral conditionality.
18.10: And if it does evil in my sight, not listening to my voice, then I will relent of the good that I had intended to do to it. The symmetrical reverse: YHWH can relent from announced blessing just as he can relent from announced judgment. A nation that receives promise of divine favor and then turns to evil forfeits that favor. This symmetry is crucial for Jeremiah's argument throughout the book: Judah inherited the promises of the Sinaitic and Davidic covenants, but those promises do not function as unconditional guarantees against the consequences of persistent covenant violation.
18.11: Now, therefore, say to the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem: Thus says YHWH, Behold, I am shaping disaster against you and devising a plan against you. Return, every one from his evil way, and amend your ways and your deeds. The application to Judah is direct: YHWH is currently shaping disaster against them, but the process is not complete. The disaster is being devised (choshev, thinking/planning) — not yet executed. The window for repentance and return (shuvu na ish midarko hara'ah) is still open. This is perhaps Jeremiah's most explicit articulation that the judgment he announces is not meant to be deterministic but is a final warning.
18.12: But they say, 'That is in vain! We will follow our own plans, and will every one act according to the stubbornness of his evil heart'. The people's response to the potter-oracle is explicit rejection. That is in vain (no'ash) — hopeless, pointless. They declare that repentance is futile and announce their intention to continue in the stubbornness of their evil heart (sherirut lev hara'). The Jeremianic diagnostic phrase appears here as self-description — the people no longer deny their orientation but proudly embrace it. This response ends the window of possibility that v11 offered.
18.13: Therefore thus says YHWH: Ask among the nations, who has heard the like of this? The virgin Israel has done a very horrible thing. YHWH calls on the nations as witnesses to Israel's unprecedented apostasy. The rhetorical move of asking the nations to judge Israel's behavior presupposes a common moral standard accessible to all peoples. A very horrible thing (sha'aruriyah) — a term of moral horror and revulsion, used of particularly egregious covenant violation (cf. 5:30; 23:14). The virgin Israel image makes the apostasy a betrayal of first-covenant intimacy.
18.14: Does the snow of Lebanon leave the crocks of Sirion? Do the mountain waters run dry, the cold flowing streams? Two images from nature's constancy: the snowfields of Mount Lebanon (perpetually snow-capped, visible from much of northern Israel) and the flowing mountain streams are reliable, constant, natural. They do not deviate from their nature. The contrast with Israel's deviation from her own covenantal nature is the point: even inanimate features of the natural world are more faithful to their nature than Israel is to her covenant identity.
18.15: But my people have forgotten me; they make offerings to false idols; they made them stumble in their ways, in the ancient roads, and to walk into side roads, not the highway. Forgotten me (shechachuni) — the covenant is a relationship requiring active memory and attention; forgetting is the passive form of apostasy. The ancient roads (netivot olam) are the established covenant ways of YHWH — the Torah path. The side roads (derachim lo-selulab, unbuilt tracks) are the improvised routes of syncretism and foreign worship, leading away from the known covenant path.
18.16: Making their land a horror, a thing to be hissed at forever. Everyone who passes by it is horrified and shakes his head. The covenant land transformed into a spectacle of shame — the hissing and head-shaking of passersby are signs of shocked condemnation (cf. 1 Kgs 9:8; Lam 2:15–16). In the honor-shame culture of the ancient Near East, a land that draws the contemptuous gaze of travelers has experienced the ultimate form of public disgrace. The permanent nature (forever) reflects the depth of the coming devastation.
18.17: Like the east wind I will scatter them before the enemy. I will show them my back, not my face, in the day of their calamity. The east wind (qadim) from the desert was a hot, desiccating, destructive force in Palestinian geography. YHWH will scatter the people as this wind scatters dust. Show them my back, not my face — the reversal of the covenant priestly blessing (Num 6:25–26: YHWH make his face shine upon you). In the day of calamity, YHWH will present his back — the posture of one who has walked away, refusing to be a refuge. This is the enacted withdrawal of covenant protection.
18.18: Then they said, Come, let us make plots against Jeremiah, for the law shall not perish from the priest, nor counsel from the wise, nor the word from the prophet. Come, let us strike him with the tongue, and let us not pay attention to any of his words. The conspiracy against Jeremiah resurfaces. The plotters' argument is significant: they claim the religious establishment (priest, wise, prophet) will continue functioning without Jeremiah's disruptions. The existing religious infrastructure — Torah-instruction from priests, wisdom from sages, prophetic word — is sufficient. They do not need Jeremiah's threatening message. Strike him with the tongue: verbal assassination — slander, discrediting, legal accusation.
18.19: Hear me, O YHWH, and listen to the voice of my adversaries. Jeremiah's prayer begins with an appeal to divine attention. Listen to the voice of my adversaries is a bold petition: Jeremiah is asking YHWH to hear what his enemies are saying — presumably so YHWH can judge them for their words. The prophet's legal appeal to YHWH as judge (riv) continues from 11:20 and 12:1.
18.20: Should good be repaid with evil? Yet they have dug a pit for my life. Remember how I stood before you to speak good for them, to turn away your wrath from them. The moral inversion: Jeremiah has interceded for the very people who are now plotting against him. He has stood before YHWH to speak good for them — precisely the intercession YHWH forbade him to perform (7:16; 11:14; 14:11) but which Jeremiah apparently continued to attempt. The injustice is poignant: his compassion for them has produced their enmity toward him.
18.21: Therefore deliver up their children to famine; give them over to the power of the sword; let their wives become childless and widowed. May their men meet death by pestilence, their youths be struck down by the sword in battle. The imprecatory petitions escalate: children to famine, men to sword, wives to widowhood and childlessness. The covenant-curse vocabulary is precise — these are exactly the penalties of Deuteronomy 28 applied to specific household members. The prayer is not personal revenge but a call for covenantal enforcement against those who have attempted to suppress the word of YHWH.
18.22: Let a cry be heard from their houses, when you bring the raider suddenly upon them! For they have dug a pit to take me, and laid snares for my feet. The specific charge that grounds the imprecation: they have dug a pit to take me, and laid snares for my feet — images of hunting and trapping applied to judicial and physical persecution. The pit recalls Joseph (Gen 37:24) and anticipates Jeremiah's actual imprisonment in a cistern (Jer 38:6). The plotters who set the trap will hear the same sudden raider descend on their own households.
18.23: Yet you, O YHWH, know all their plotting to kill me. Forgive not their iniquity, nor blot out their sin from your sight. Let them be overthrown before you; deal with them in the time of your anger. The final imprecatory petition refuses forgiveness for the persecutors. The theological boldness is deliberate: Jeremiah is not praying for his personal enemies' salvation but for covenant justice. The same YHWH who forgives repentant sinners (v8) will not blot out the sin of those who persistently plot to kill his messenger. The prayer closes with the covenant judgment formula: deal with them in the time of your anger — the appointed time of reckoning.

19.1: Chapter 19 presents the second potter-and-clay sign-act — but with a crucial difference from chapter 18. Thus says YHWH, Go, buy a potter's earthenware flask, and take some of the elders of the people and some of the elders of the priests. The key distinction: chapter 18 used unfired clay, which can be reshaped. Chapter 19 uses a baqbuq — a fired, glazed ceramic flask. Once pottery has been fired in the kiln, it cannot be reshaped; it can only be broken. The two sign-acts together present two theological moments: the still-reformable and the now-irreversible.
19.2: And go out to the Valley of the Son of Hinnom at the entry of the Potsherd Gate, and proclaim there the words that I tell you. The location is theologically loaded: the Valley of Hinnom (Gei Ben-Hinnom) was the site of the worst apostasy in Judah's history — child sacrifice to Baal and Molech at the Topheth installation (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31; 32:35). It was also the city's rubbish dump, where refuse and broken pottery (potsherd gate) were discarded. This valley became the NT Gehenna — the garbage-and-fire valley that Jesus uses as the image of final judgment (Matt 5:22, 29–30; 18:9). The sign-act is performed at the place most associated with the worst of Judah's sin.
19.3: And say, Hear the word of YHWH, O kings of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem. Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing such disaster upon this place that the ears of everyone who hears of it will tingle. The audience includes the royal house and the general population — both are to witness the sign-act and receive the oracle. The ears of everyone who hears will tingle (tetzalnah oznav) — the same phrase used in 1 Samuel 3:11 for the announcement of Eli's house's judgment and in 2 Kings 21:12 for Manasseh's judgment. The phrasing links this announcement to the full tradition of radical divine judgment in Israel's history.
19.4: Because they have forsaken me and have profaned this place by making offerings in it to other gods whom neither they nor their fathers nor the kings of Judah have known; and because they have filled this place with the blood of innocents. The dual indictment: offerings to unknown gods (foreign cults whose identities YHWH disclaims — neither he nor the patriarchs authorized them) and blood of innocents (child sacrifice). The Hinnom Valley is the physical location of both crimes — the place where foreign altars were erected and where children were burned. The crimes are spatially concentrated, making the valley itself the primary locus of covenant violation.
19.5: And have built the high places of Baal to burn their sons in the fire as burnt offerings to Baal, which I did not command or decree, nor did it come into my mind. The triple denial — did not command, did not decree, did not enter my mind — is one of the strongest possible disavowals. YHWH is emphasizing that child sacrifice was never a possibility within the Yahwistic covenant framework; it is not an extreme form of legitimate worship but a total departure from it. This directly contradicts any theology that portrayed child sacrifice as the ultimate gift to the deity.
19.6: Therefore, behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when this place shall no more be called Topheth, or the Valley of the Son of Hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter. The renaming of the Hinnom Valley as the Valley of Slaughter (gei hahareguah) is a prophetic name-change that anticipates its transformation from a place where children were killed as sacrifice to a place where the population of Jerusalem will be killed as judgment. The location of the worst covenant violation becomes the location of the worst covenant punishment — a spatial covenantal irony.
19.7: And in this place I will make void the plans of Judah and Jerusalem, and will cause their people to fall by the sword before their enemies, and by the hand of those who seek their life. I will give their dead bodies for food to the birds of the air and to the beasts of the earth. The making void (vevaqqoti) plays on baqbuq (the flask) — a wordplay the Hebrew audience would catch: I will empty out the plans of Judah like one pours out a flask. The unburied bodies for birds and beasts is the covenant-curse standard (Deut 28:26; Jer 7:33) — this time enacted at the very place where bodies were previously burned as offerings.
19.8: And I will make this city a horror and a thing to be hissed at. Everyone who passes by it will be horrified and will hiss because of all its wounds. The Jerusalem-as-spectacle theme (cf. 18:16) is intensified: the city that was YHWH's chosen dwelling place, the pride of all the earth (Lam 2:15), will become a spectacle of horror. The hissing of passersby is the ancient equivalent of public condemnation — a sound and gesture associated with responses to moral atrocity and divine judgment in the ancient world.
19.9: And I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and their daughters, and everyone shall eat the flesh of his neighbor in the siege and in the distress, with which their enemies and those who seek their life afflict them. Siege cannibalism — the most extreme form of human suffering under prolonged blockade — is listed in Deuteronomy 28:53–57 as a specific covenant curse for covenant violation. It was historically fulfilled during the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem (Lam 4:10: the hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children). The horror of the sign-act location (Hinnom Valley) is compounded by this prediction: the parents who burned their children in the valley will be reduced to eating their children during the siege.
19.10: Then you shall break the flask in the sight of the men who go with you. The moment of the sign-act itself. The breaking of the fired clay flask is irreversible — unlike the unfired clay of chapter 18, which could be remolded, a broken ceramic vessel cannot be repaired. The witnesses (elders of the people and priests, v1) see it happen. The physical event embodies the theological claim: the moment for reshaping has passed. Jerusalem is now the fired flask, not the unfired clay.
19.11: And shall say to them, Thus says YHWH of hosts: So will I break this people and this city, as one breaks a potter's vessel, so that it can never be mended. Men shall be buried in Topheth because there will be no place else to bury. The explicit interpretation: as one breaks a potter's vessel, so that it can never be mended. The irreversibility is stressed. The burial reference is grim: so many will die that the Hinnom Valley itself — previously unusable as a burial site because of its cultic impurity — will have to be pressed into service as a cemetery. The place of child sacrifice becomes a mass grave for adults.
19.12: Thus will I do to this place and its inhabitants, declares YHWH, making this city like Topheth. The houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah shall be defiled like the place of Topheth, with all the houses on whose rooftops offerings have been made to all the host of heaven, and drink offerings have been poured out to other gods. The defilement-logic: the rooftop altars throughout Jerusalem (cf. 2 Kgs 23:12; Jer 32:29) have made every house ritually contaminated, extending the Topheth-defilement across the entire city. The worship of the host of heaven (stars, planets) was a Mesopotamian import that competed with and supplemented YHWH worship throughout the monarchy period.
19.13: The extension of the defilement judgment to every house in Jerusalem (v12) means no quarter of the city is exempt. Even the royal palace — whose rooftop had hosted astral altars installed by Manasseh (2 Kgs 21:5) and temporarily dismantled by Josiah (2 Kgs 23:12) — is included in the indictment. The thoroughness of the judgment reflects the thoroughness of the apostasy: it extended from the Hinnom Valley to the city center to the royal palace to every neighborhood rooftop.
19.14: Then Jeremiah came from Topheth, where YHWH had sent him to prophesy, and he stood in the court of the YHWH's house and said to all the people. Jeremiah brings the Hinnom Valley message into the Temple precincts — the most sacred space in Jerusalem. The movement from Topheth to the Temple court is itself symbolic: the place of worst apostasy and the place of YHWH's dwelling are brought into confrontation. The prophet's body carries the message from one sacred site to the other.
19.15: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing upon this city and upon all its towns all the disaster that I have pronounced against it, because they have stiffened their neck, refusing to hear my words. The summary verdict returns to the stiff-neck diagnosis (cf. 17:23) — the physical metaphor for obstinate refusal to accept the covenant yoke. The disaster pronounced against Jerusalem is comprehensive: this city and all its towns — not just the capital but the entire urban network of Judah. The chapter ends with the covenant sentence pronounced and the sign-act completed: the flask is broken; the judgment is sealed.

20.1: Now Pashhur the priest, the son of Immer, who was chief officer in the house of YHWH, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things.
