The institutional response to Jeremiah's Temple court sermon (19:14–15) is immediate. Pashhur ben Immer is the chief officer (paqid nagid) — a Temple security official responsible for maintaining order in the sanctuary complex. His family background (son of Immer — a priestly family; cf. 1 Chr 24:14) and his institutional role make him a representative of the religious establishment that Jeremiah has been attacking throughout chapters 7–20. The confrontation between prophet and institutional priest is inevitable.
20.2: Then Pashhur beat Jeremiah the prophet and put him in the stocks that were in the upper Benjamin Gate of the house of YHWH. Physical violence against the prophet followed by the stocks (mahpekhet) — a device that locked the victim's neck, hands, and feet into a painful bent-forward position. The upper Benjamin Gate was a prominent public location in the Temple area — the stocks were placed where maximum public shame would accompany the physical punishment. The prophet who announced that Jerusalem's gates would be shut (17:27) is now displayed in a gate, imprisoned and shamed.
20.3: The next day, when Pashhur released Jeremiah from the stocks, Jeremiah said to him, YHWH does not call your name Pashhur, but Terror on Every Side. The name-change oracle is one of Jeremiah's most dramatic pronouncements. Magor-Missaviv — Terror on Every Side — will become a refrain in Jeremiah's oracles (cf. 6:25; 20:10; 46:5; 49:29). The name Pashhur is uncertain in etymology; YHWH renames him with a title of doom. The person who attempted to silence terror of YHWH's word by physical violence will himself become an embodied reminder of terror. The announcement of this new name to Pashhur's face takes considerable courage.
20.4: For thus says YHWH: Behold, I will make you a terror to yourself and to all your friends. They shall fall by the sword of their enemies while you look on. And I will give all Judah into the hand of the king of Babylon. He shall carry them captive to Babylon, and shall strike them down with the sword. The specific judgment on Pashhur includes being forced to watch his friends die. The king of Babylon is named explicitly — one of the early passages in Jeremiah where Babylon is identified by name rather than as the abstract north-foe. The historical specificity signals that the judgment oracle has advanced from the general to the particular: the instrument of covenant judgment has been identified.
20.5: Moreover, I will give all the wealth of the city, all its gains, all its prized belongings, and all the treasures of the kings of Judah into the hand of their enemies, who shall plunder them and seize them and carry them to Babylon. The total transfer of Jerusalem's accumulated wealth to Babylon is covenant confiscation (cf. 17:3). The Temple treasury, royal treasury, and the private wealth of the city — all will be carried to Babylon. This was historically fulfilled in the deportations of 597 BC (2 Kgs 24:13) and 587 BC (2 Kgs 25:13–17).
20.6: And you, Pashhur, and all who dwell in your house, shall go into captivity. To Babylon you shall go, and there you shall die, and there you shall be buried, you and all your friends, to whom you have prophesied falsely. Pashhur's personal fate: exile to Babylon, death in Babylon, burial in Babylon. The false prophecy charge (nibeta lahem shaqer) completes the indictment: Pashhur was not merely a security official but a prophetic voice in his own right, and he prophesied peace. His physical death in exile will prove his prophecy false and Jeremiah's true.
20.7: O YHWH, you have deceived me, and I was deceived; you are stronger than I, and you have prevailed. I have become a laughingstock all the day; everyone mocks me. The sixth and greatest of Jeremiah's Confessions begins here (20:7–18). The opening verb pittitani (you have persuaded/deceived me) is the same word used in Exod 22:16 for seducing a virgin and in 1 Kgs 22:20–22 for the spirit sent to deceive Ahab's prophets. Jeremiah accuses YHWH of having deceived him into the prophetic vocation — lured him in with what seemed like covenant calling, and then the ministry produced only suffering and mockery. The charge of divine deception is the most theologically daring language in the Confessions.
20.8: For whenever I speak, I cry out, I shout, Violence and destruction! For the word of YHWH has become for me a reproach and derision all day long. The content of Jeremiah's message — violence and destruction (chamas veshod) — is the constant refrain of his preaching. Rather than producing respect or repentance, it has made him an object of contempt. Reproach and derision (cherpah veqeles): the prophet's social status has been destroyed by his message. In an honor-shame culture, this is an existential threat — the inability to function in normal social relationships because one's reputation has been destroyed by association with an unpopular message.
20.9: If I say, I will not mention him, or speak any more in his name, there is in my heart as it were a burning fire shut up in my bones, and I am weary with holding it in, and I cannot. Jeremiah tries silence — the obvious solution to his social humiliation — and finds he cannot maintain it. The burning fire shut up in my bones is one of the most psychologically precise descriptions of prophetic compulsion in the Old Testament. The word cannot be contained; attempting to suppress it produces greater internal anguish than delivering it. This is the paradox of prophetic vocation: Jeremiah did not choose the calling, cannot abandon it, and suffers because of it.
20.10: For I hear many whispering. Terror is on every side! Denounce him! Let us denounce him! say all my close friends, watching for my fall. Perhaps he will be deceived; then we can overcome him and take our revenge on him. Terror on every side (magor missaviv) — the name Jeremiah assigned to Pashhur (v3) has become the slogan of his mockers. They throw his own prophetic language back at him as a taunt. Even his close friends (anshei shlomi, men of my peace — those he trusted as allies) are watching for his stumbling, waiting to denounce him. The intimacy of the betrayal echoes 11:19 (the Anathoth conspiracy) and Psalm 41:9 (my close friend has lifted his heel against me — cited in John 13:18 of Judas).
20.11: But YHWH is with me as a dread warrior; therefore my persecutors will stumble; they will not overcome me. They will be greatly shamed, for they will not succeed. Their eternal dishonor will never be forgotten. A sudden reversal: despite the complaints of vv7–10, confidence breaks through. YHWH is with me as a dread warrior (YHWH ki be'al gibbor ariz) — the divine warrior who fights for Jeremiah. The prophet's persecutors will stumble while pursuing him; their shame will be permanent (bushat olam). The oscillation between lament and trust is the authentic texture of Jeremiah's spiritual life — he does not resolve the tension but holds both poles.
20.12: O YHWH of hosts, who tests the righteous, who sees the heart and the mind, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you have I committed my cause. The covenant-appeal formula recurs from 11:20 and 15:15 — YHWH as the one who tests (bachen) and sees (ro'eh) the hidden interior. Let me see your vengeance: the prophet asks to witness the covenant enforcement against his persecutors. I have committed my cause to you (galiti et-rivi eleycha): the legal dispute has been formally lodged with the divine judge. Jeremiah is not taking matters into his own hands but waiting for covenant justice.
20.13: Sing to YHWH; praise YHWH! For he has delivered the life of the needy from the hand of evildoers. A call to praise interrupts the complaint sequence — one of the most jarring transitions in Jeremiah. The needy (evyon) — the vulnerable and powerless one whom the system oppresses — has been delivered by YHWH. This praise is eschatological in character: Jeremiah sings as if the deliverance has already happened, in the mode of prophetic certainty. The Psalms of lament regularly include this element of anticipatory praise (cf. Ps 22:22–31 after the lament of vv1–21).
20.14: Cursed be the day on which I was born! The day when my mother bore me, let it not be blessed! The death-wish that follows the praise is one of the most shocking juxtapositions in prophetic literature. The oscillation from praise (v13) to curse (v14) captures the extremity of Jeremiah's psychological state. The cursing of his birthday is the most total rejection of one's existence possible in the Hebrew worldview: to curse the day of birth is to wish one had never been. Job 3 opens with the same formula; Ecclesiastes 4:3 calls it better to have never been born. These are not theological positions but expressions of unbearable suffering.
20.15: Cursed be the man who brought the news to my father, A son is born to you, making him very glad. The birth-announcement was a moment of great joy — a son was born (cf. Jer 33:11: the voice of the bridegroom; John 16:21: a woman who has given birth no longer remembers the anguish). Jeremiah curses the messenger who brought his father the good news, because that joy inaugurated a life of unrelenting suffering. The innocent messenger becomes the target of the prophet's anguish — an expression of the wish that the joyful report had never been made.
20.16: Let that man be like the cities that YHWH overthrew without pity; let him hear a cry in the morning and an alarm at noon. The overthrown cities are likely Sodom and Gomorrah (cf. Gen 19:24–25) — the paradigmatic examples of total divine judgment. To be like Sodom is to receive the worst possible fate. The cry in the morning and alarm at noon describe the sounds of attack and siege — Jeremiah wishes on the birth-messenger a life as catastrophically filled with alarm as his own has been.
20.17: Because he did not kill me in the womb; so my mother would have been my grave, and her womb forever great. The wish reaches its extreme: Jeremiah desires that he had died in utero. The womb as grave — the place of gestation becomes the place of burial without ever having been a place of life outside. The permanent pregnancy of the mother (verachmah harah olam) is a horrific image: the womb swollen with an unborn child who is simultaneously already dead. No birth, no life, no suffering.
20.18: Why did I come out from the womb to see toil and sorrow, and spend my days in shame? The Confession ends on the question that frames the entire chapter's existential crisis: why? (lamah). Not how or when but why — the teleological question about the purpose of existence. Jeremiah sees his life as characterized by toil and sorrow (amal veyagon) and public shame (boshet). He does not answer his own question; the chapter ends with the unanswered why. The Confession provides no resolution — it is a wound that the book leaves open, modeling the honest anguish of the covenant messenger in a world that rejects the covenant word.

21.1: Chapter 21 marks a major structural and historical shift in the book of Jeremiah. This is the word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH, when King Zedekiah sent to him Pashhur the son of Malchiah and Zephaniah the priest, the son of Maaseiah, saying. The Zedekiah setting places this chapter in the period of the final Babylonian siege of Jerusalem (588–587 BC) — approximately 20 years after the Temple sermon of chapter 7. The delegation includes a Pashhur (different from the Pashhur of chapter 20 — this is son of Malchiah, not son of Immer) and Zephaniah the priest, who appears again in 29:25–26 and 37:3. The royal delegation signals that the crisis has reached the point where even the king who earlier ignored Jeremiah now turns to him.
21.2: Please inquire of YHWH for us, for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is making war against us. Perhaps YHWH will deal with us according to all his wonderful deeds and will make him withdraw from us. Zedekiah's request is a classic covenant-consultation: inquire of YHWH (darash na ba'adenu et YHWH). The king appeals to historical precedent — his wonderful deeds (nifle'otav) — specifically, YHWH's past deliverances, perhaps including the deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib in 701 BC (2 Kgs 19:35–36: the angel destroyed 185,000 Assyrians overnight). Zedekiah is hoping for a repeat Sennacherib-miracle: the besieging army supernaturally withdrawn. The hope is not irrational given precedent; it is the wrong hope in the wrong theological moment.
21.3: Then Jeremiah said to them: Thus you shall say to Zedekiah. The answer is addressed directly to the king. Jeremiah does not soften the message for the royal delegation; he delivers the same uncompromising word to king as to commoner. The answer will directly contradict what the king hoped to hear — no miraculous deliverance, no Sennacherib-repeat, but something far more theologically disorienting.
21.4: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Behold, I will turn back the weapons of war that are in your hands and with which you are fighting against the king of Babylon and against the Chaldeans who are besieging you outside the walls. And I will bring them together into the midst of this city. The divine oracle is a reversal of Zedekiah's hope. Rather than withdrawing the Babylonian army, YHWH will turn back the weapons of Jerusalem's defenders — disable them. Rather than keeping the Babylonians outside the walls, YHWH will bring them inside the city. The Sennacherib-miracle will not be repeated; instead the opposite will happen. The theological reason: Sennacherib attacked YHWH's city without YHWH's authorization; Nebuchadnezzar attacks with YHWH's commission.
21.5: I myself will fight against you with outstretched hand and strong arm, in anger and in fury and in great wrath. The most theologically shocking verse in the chapter: I myself will fight against you (nilchamti ani etkhem). YHWH fought for Israel against Egypt with outstretched hand and strong arm (Exod 6:6; Deut 4:34; 26:8) — the Exodus-war formula now applied in reverse. YHWH is fighting against his own people with the same intensity with which he once fought for them. The covenantal framework makes this possible: the same God who saved Israel at the Exodus will judge them in the exile. The parallel use of Exodus language for judgment is one of Jeremiah's most powerful rhetorical moves.
21.6: I will strike down the inhabitants of this city, both man and beast. They shall die of a great pestilence. The pestilence component of the judgment triad is specified for the city's population — both human and animal. The inclusion of animals reflects the total ecological impact of siege and divine judgment (cf. the animal deaths in the drought oracle, 14:5–6). The besieged city will experience not only military attack but epidemic disease — a predictable result of the crowding, sanitation breakdown, and malnutrition of prolonged siege.
21.7: Afterward, declares YHWH, I will give Zedekiah king of Judah and his servants and the people in this city who survive the pestilence, sword, and famine into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and into the hand of their enemies, into the hand of those who seek their lives. He shall strike them down with the edge of the sword. He shall not pity them or spare them or have compassion. Zedekiah's personal fate is specified: he will be handed over to Nebuchadnezzar. The triple negation of divine compassion (will not pity, will not spare, will not have compassion) echoes 13:14 — the judgment has reached the point where the covenant mercy attributes are suspended. This was historically fulfilled in 2 Kings 25:6–7: Nebuchadnezzar executed Zedekiah's sons before his eyes and then blinded him.
21.8: And to this people you shall say: Thus says YHWH: Behold, I set before you the way of life and the way of death. The two-ways offer is one of Jeremiah's most striking pastoral interventions. The Deuteronomic framework of life and death (Deut 30:15–20: I set before you life and death, blessing and curse — choose life) is now recontextualized: the way of life is not covenant faithfulness leading to prosperity in the land, but surrender to Babylon. The theological inversion is complete: the natural instinct of covenant loyalty (resist the enemy) has become the way of death; surrender to the enemy has become the way of life.
21.9: Whoever stays in this city shall die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence, but whoever goes out and surrenders to the Chaldeans who are besieging you shall live and shall have his life as a prize of war. The offer is concrete: those who remain in Jerusalem to fight will die by the judgment triad (sword, famine, pestilence). Those who go out and surrender (naphel el-hakasdim, fall/defect to the Chaldeans) will preserve their lives. The phrase life as a prize of war (nafshah lo leshalal) — life as spoil, as something snatched from the enemy — suggests that survival will be bare and undignified, but it will be survival. Jeremiah will repeat this offer throughout the siege period (38:2).
21.10: For I have set my face against this city for harm and not for good, declares YHWH: it shall be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall burn it with fire. YHWH's face set against Jerusalem (rather than for it) is the ultimate reversal of the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:25–26: YHWH lift up his face to you). The Babylonian burning of Jerusalem is announced categorically: he shall burn it with fire. This is precisely what happened in 587 BC (2 Kgs 25:9). The irreversibility of the flask-sign-act (ch. 19) is here confirmed in explicit historical prediction.
21.11: And to the house of the king of Judah say, Hear the word of YHWH. A separate oracle addressed specifically to the royal house begins here (vv11–14), distinguishing the message to the Davidic dynasty from the message to the general population. The royal house still has a specific covenantal responsibility: to execute justice.
21.12: O house of David! Thus says YHWH: Execute justice in the morning, and deliver from the hand of the oppressor him who has been robbed, lest my wrath go forth like fire, and burn with none to quench it, because of your evil deeds. Execute justice in the morning (shiftu laboqer mishpat) — the king's primary covenantal obligation was judicial: to hear cases at the city gate in the morning (the customary time for judicial proceedings), to defend the oppressed and dispossess the oppressor. The royal failure in this regard (cf. Jer 22:13–17 on Jehoiakim) is what has brought the judgment. Even now, in the siege, genuine judicial reform could theoretically alter the trajectory — but the people's response to the potter-oracle (18:12: that is hopeless) suggests no such reform is forthcoming.
21.13: Behold, I am against you, O inhabitant of the valley, O rock of the plain, declares YHWH; you who say, Who shall come down against us, or who shall enter our dwellings? Jerusalem is addressed under geographical epithets: the inhabitant of the valley and rock of the plain describe the city's topographic setting — it sits on a ridge overlooking valleys, surrounded by heights. The city's rhetorical confidence in its natural defenses (who can come down against us?) is the geographic expression of Zion theology: the impregnable fortress that YHWH defends. Jeremiah announces that the very natural defenses the city trusts will be overcome.
21.14: I will punish you according to the fruit of your deeds, declares YHWH; I will kindle a fire in her forest, and it shall devour all that is around her. The judgment closes with the fire-in-the-forest image — the dense woodland that surrounded Jerusalem (the forests of the hill country) consumed by fire, extending the destruction beyond the city walls into the surrounding territory. According to the fruit of your deeds (kifri ma'aleleikhem): the covenant-recompense formula. The punishment is not arbitrary but the natural consequence of the covenant violations catalogued across the preceding chapters. The chapter ends with the irreversible verdict, and the loop of judgment-announced-judgment-executed closes on Jerusalem after 20 chapters of warning.

22.1: Chapter 22 begins the Royal Oracle Collection — a sustained indictment of the Davidic monarchy that runs through chapter 23. Thus says YHWH: Go down to the house of the king of Judah and speak there this word. Jeremiah is sent directly to the palace — not the Temple but the seat of political power. The prophet's access to the royal house is not incidental; it reflects the ancient Near Eastern institution of court prophets who addressed kings directly. Jeremiah, however, comes not as court prophet giving favorable counsel but as covenant prosecutor delivering indictment.
22.2: And say, Hear the word of YHWH, O king of Judah, you who sit on the throne of David, you, and your servants, and your people who enter these gates. The audience at the palace gates is the same as at the Temple gates (17:19–27) — king, servants, all who pass through. The throne of David is the theological anchor: the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12–16) promised a permanent dynasty, but that covenant was conditional on the king's faithfulness (1 Kgs 2:4; 9:4–5). The king sitting on David's throne has inherited a promise and an obligation simultaneously.
22.3: Thus says YHWH: Do justice and righteousness, and deliver from the hand of the oppressor him who has been robbed. And do no wrong or violence to the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow, nor shed innocent blood in this place. The royal covenant obligation is stated with precision: justice and righteousness (mishpat utzedaqah) — the paired terms that define the social order YHWH requires. The specific vulnerable categories — resident alien, fatherless, widow — are the covenant's protected classes throughout the Torah (Exod 22:21–24; Deut 10:18; 24:17–21). The king who fails to protect these is not merely ineffective; he is violating the covenant mandate of the Davidic office.
22.4: For if you will indeed obey this word, then there shall enter the gates of this house kings who sit on the throne of David, riding in chariots and on horses, they and their servants and their people. The conditional form of the Davidic covenant emerges: obedience to the justice mandate results in stable, continuing dynasty. The entry through the palace gates of chariots and horses signals normal royal function and national strength. The promised blessing is not theological abstraction but concrete political continuity — the very thing the kings most want.
22.5: But if you will not obey these words, I swear by myself, declares YHWH, that this house shall become a desolation. The oath by YHWH himself (bi nishba'ti) is the strongest possible form of divine commitment — the same formula used for the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 22:16) and the Davidic promise. Here it is sworn for judgment rather than blessing: YHWH pledges by his own nature that the palace will become a ruin if justice is not practiced. The Davidic covenant itself becomes the source of the condemnation.
22.6: For thus says YHWH concerning the house of the king of Judah: You are like Gilead to me, like the summit of Lebanon, yet surely I will make you a desert, an uninhabited city. Gilead (Transjordanian hill country) and Lebanon (famous cedar forests) were bywords for forested splendor and fruitfulness. The royal house, once as magnificent as these landscapes, will be made a desert — the same inversion as the vine-to-wilderness image in Isaiah 5. The specific application to the palace building, whose cedar beams came from Lebanon (cf. v14–15), gives the Lebanon image added irony.
22.7: I will prepare destroyers against you, each with his weapons, and they shall cut down your choicest cedars and cast them into the fire. The destroyers with axes cutting the cedars literalize the threat of v6: the Lebanon-cedars that built the palace will be cut by Babylonian soldiers. The image plays against the earlier Solomonic pride in cedar construction (1 Kgs 5:6–10; 7:1–12) — the cedars that symbolized royal magnificence are destroyed by the very weapons YHWH has prepared.
22.8: And many nations will pass by this city, and every man will say to his neighbor, Why has YHWH done thus to this great city? The nations-as-witnesses theme recurs (cf. 18:13–16): passersby will interrogate the ruins. The question why has YHWH done this? assumes what the theology of the book teaches: the nations will know that YHWH was the agent of Jerusalem's destruction, not merely that Babylon conquered it. The theological knowledge is universal — even the nations recognize the divine agency in historical catastrophe.
22.9: And they will answer, Because they have forsaken the covenant of YHWH their God and worshiped other gods and served them. The nations' answer is the Deuteronomistic explanation: covenant abandonment and idol-worship. The knowledge transferred to the nations is theological: YHWH judges covenant violation. This serves a double purpose — it honors YHWH's reputation (the judgment is just, not capricious) and provides a warning for the nations about the consequences of rejecting YHWH.
22.10: Weep not for him who is dead, nor grieve for him; but weep bitterly for him who goes away, for he shall return no more to see his native land. The oracle about Jehoahaz (Shallum) and Josiah. Him who is dead is Josiah, killed at Megiddo by Pharaoh Neco (609 BC) — the last good king, whose death was mourned greatly (2 Chr 35:24–25). But Jeremiah instructs the people not to mourn Josiah's death but to mourn him who goes away — Jehoahaz, Josiah's son, who was deposed by Egypt and taken captive to Egypt (2 Kgs 23:33–34) where he died. Josiah died in battle; that was a warrior's death. Exile is the more grievous fate.
22.11: For thus says YHWH concerning Shallum the son of Josiah, king of Judah, who reigned instead of Josiah his father, and who went away from this place: He shall return here no more. Shallum is the birth-name of Jehoahaz (cf. 1 Chr 3:15), used here perhaps polemically to distinguish him from the throne-name. Who reigned instead of Josiah: the people chose Jehoahaz over his older brother Jehoiakim, presumably because he shared his father's reforming sympathies. Pharaoh Neco overrode the choice and installed the compliant Jehoiakim. The people's choice went into Egyptian exile.
22.12: But in the place where they have carried him captive, there shall he die, and he shall never see this land again. Jehoahaz died in Egypt (2 Kgs 23:34). The oracle is simple and specific: no return. The covenant land remains beyond his reach forever. The contrast with Moses, who also died without entering the land, is implicit — but Moses died having accomplished his mission; Jehoahaz dies as a captive, having accomplished nothing.
22.13: Woe to him who builds his house by unrighteousness, and his upper rooms by injustice, who makes his neighbor serve him for nothing and does not give him his wages. The oracle against Jehoiakim (r. 609–598 BC) — one of the sharpest royal condemnations in the prophets. The woe (hoy) formula introduces a death-lament over one still living — the prophetic anticipation of inevitable doom. The specific charge: building with forced labor without pay. This violates Deuteronomy 24:14–15 (pay workers the same day) and the fundamental covenant prohibition on exploitation. Jehoiakim's palace expansion was funded by corvée labor.
22.14: Who says, I will build myself a great house with spacious upper rooms, cutting out windows for it, paneling it with cedar and painting it with vermilion. The architectural ambition is satirized through specific details: spacious upper rooms (aliyyot merchavim), windows, cedar paneling, vermilion paint. The cedar echoes Solomon's Temple and palace (1 Kgs 6–7), but Solomon's building was for YHWH's dwelling and funded by willing trade (1 Kgs 5:12); Jehoiakim's is personal aggrandizement funded by unpaid labor. The vermilion paint is a sign of conspicuous wealth — imported pigment used by the Mesopotamian courts.
22.15: Do you think you are a king because you compete in cedar? Did not your father eat and drink and do justice and righteousness? Then it was well with him. The comparison with Josiah is devastating. Josiah ate and drank like anyone else (the point being: he did not demand luxury) and practiced justice. Then it was well with him (az tov lo) — the covenant logic of blessing following obedience. The question do you think you are a king because you compete in cedar? strips away the ideology of royal building programs: greatness is not architectural achievement but justice. The ANE kings measured their legitimacy partly by building projects; Jeremiah inverts this measure entirely.
22.16: He judged the cause of the poor and needy; then it was well. Is not this to know me? declares YHWH. One of Jeremiah's most important theological statements: to know me (lada'at oti) = judging the cause of the poor and needy. Da'at (knowledge) in the Hebraic sense is not intellectual apprehension but covenantal relationship expressed in action. Knowing YHWH is functionally identical to practicing justice for the vulnerable. This is the covenant epistemology at its sharpest: you cannot claim to know YHWH while exploiting the poor. The NT echo is Matthew 25:31–46 — serving Christ in serving the hungry, thirsty, stranger, naked, sick, imprisoned.
22.17: But you have eyes and heart only for your dishonest gain, for shedding innocent blood, and for practicing oppression and violence. Jehoiakim's character is summarized in three charges: dishonest gain (betz'a — profit through exploitation), shedding innocent blood (cf. 2 Kgs 24:4 — Manasseh's innocent blood, which Jehoiakim continued; also the execution of the prophet Uriah, Jer 26:20–23), and oppression and violence. The three form a complete picture of a ruler who has inverted the royal covenant mandate from protection to predation.
22.18: Therefore thus says YHWH concerning Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah: They shall not lament for him, saying, Ah, my brother! or Ah, sister! They shall not lament for him, saying, Ah, lord! or Ah, his majesty! The absence of mourning (lo-yispedun lo) is the covenant-curse mirror of his abuse of laborers. He denied wages to those who labored for him; he will be denied the labor of mourning from those who should lament him. The specific mourning formulas — ah, my brother / ah, sister / ah, lord / ah, majesty — cover the range of communal grief responses. None will be voiced for Jehoiakim.
22.19: With the burial of a donkey he shall be buried, dragged and dumped beyond the gates of Jerusalem. The donkey burial is the most dishonoring death-fate imaginable: no tomb, no ceremony, no lamentation — the body dragged out and discarded like a dead animal. Some scholars debate whether this was literally fulfilled (2 Kgs 24:6 notes he died and his son succeeded him — no burial description), but the theological point is clear: the king who denied wages will be denied dignity. The contrast with David (buried in the city of David with honor) could not be more stark.
22.20: Go up to Lebanon, and cry out, and lift up your voice in Bashan; cry out from Abar, for all your lovers are destroyed. Jerusalem is addressed (feminine) — the political alliances Judah cultivated (Lebanon, Bashan/north, Abar/east) have failed. The lovers (me'ahavayikh) are the foreign allies on whom Judah relied instead of YHWH — Egypt, Assyria, Babylon (cf. Ezek 16, 23). The geographic arc (north, east) encompasses the directions from which Judah sought help. All of them have been destroyed or have failed her.
22.21: I spoke to you in your prosperity, but you said, I will not listen. This has been your way from your youth, that you have not obeyed my voice. The covenant audit: YHWH spoke during prosperity — when it would have been easy to obey, when obedience carried no cost. Judah refused. The refusal goes back to your youth (min-ne'ureyikh) — the period of the wilderness, the earliest formation of the nation (cf. Hos 11:1–4; Jer 2:2). The pattern of rejecting YHWH's voice is not recent apostasy but the entire national history — a long arc of covenant failure interrupted only by brief reforms.
22.22: The wind shall shepherd all your shepherds, and your lovers shall go into captivity; then you will be ashamed and confounded because of all your evil. The shepherds (political leaders) will be herded by the wind — the ironic reversal of their role. Shepherds manage flocks; the wind will manage them. Wind shepherding shepherds pictures the leaders driven into exile by the uncontrollable forces of Babylonian military conquest. The lovers (allies) go into captivity rather than providing rescue. The shame and confusion that results from misplaced trust is the appropriate response to the collapse of every humanly constructed security.
22.23: O inhabitant of Lebanon, nested among the cedars, how you will be pitied when pangs come upon you, pain as of a woman in labor! The Lebanon-cedar image returns for Jerusalem — the city that built with cedar (v14) and nested in cedar as a false security. The labor-pains image (chevlim kiyoledah) for sudden, overwhelming, unavoidable distress is a standard prophetic metaphor (cf. Isa 13:8; Jer 6:24; Mic 4:9–10; 1 Thess 5:3 — destruction coming like labor pains on a pregnant woman).
22.24: As I live, declares YHWH, though Coniah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, were the signet ring on my right hand, yet I would tear you off. The oracle against Coniah (Jehoiachin — also called Jeconiah) is one of the most dramatic in the royal oracle collection. The signet ring (chotem) was the king's personal seal — used to authenticate royal documents, worn on the right hand, the most intimate symbol of royal authority and personal identity. YHWH says: even if Jehoiachin were as close to me as my own signet ring, I would tear him off. The image is total rejection from the most intimate possible proximity.
22.25: And I will give you into the hand of those who seek your life, into the hand of those of whom you are afraid, even into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and into the hand of the Chaldeans. The specific identification of Nebuchadnezzar as the agent of judgment (cf. 21:7). The phrase of whom you are afraid suggests that Jehoiachin already knew his political situation was precarious. He had reason to fear Nebuchadnezzar — and YHWH confirms that this fear is well-founded: not only is there danger, YHWH will actively deliver him into Nebuchadnezzar's hands.
22.26: I will hurl you and the mother who bore you into another country, where you were not born, and there you shall die. Jehoiachin and his mother Nehushta (2 Kgs 24:8) both go into exile — a detail confirmed by the historical record. The mother-queen (gevirah) had significant political influence in the Judahite monarchy (cf. 13:18 where Jeremiah addressed king and queen mother together). Their joint exile ends the functioning Davidic court in Jerusalem.
22.27: But to the land to which they will long to return, there they shall not return. The longing for return is anticipated — exile produces homesickness for the covenant land. The oracle declares that this longing will not be satisfied for Jehoiachin. He will die in Babylon. (Historically, Jehoiachin lived in Babylonian captivity for 37 years — Evil-merodach released him from prison in 562 BC, 2 Kgs 25:27–30 — and he apparently died in Babylon, never returning to Judah.)
22.28: Is this man Coniah a despised, broken pot, a vessel no one cares for? Why are he and his children hurled and cast into a land that they do not know? The rhetorical question voices what might seem a puzzling divine action: why treat the Davidic king like worthless broken pottery? The image of the broken pot, vessel no one cares for recalls the broken flask of chapter 19 — another ceramic image for irreversible rejection. The community's potential confusion is acknowledged: this seems like the end of the Davidic promise.
22.29: O land, land, land, hear the word of YHWH! The triple address to the land (eretz, eretz, eretz) is unprecedented in Jeremiah — only Amos 3:1 uses a comparable triple summons. The covenant land itself is called as a witness to what YHWH is about to declare. The land's testimony is needed because what follows is the most drastic declaration in the entire royal oracle section.
22.30: Thus says YHWH: Write this man down as childless, a man who shall not succeed in his days, for none of his offspring shall succeed in sitting on the throne of David and ruling again in Judah. The Coniah oracle's stunning conclusion: he is declared childless (ariri) not in the physical sense (he had seven sons, 1 Chr 3:17–18) but in the dynastic sense — none of his descendants will sit on the throne of David. This created a profound messianic problem for later Jewish readers: the Matthean genealogy (Matt 1:11–12) runs through Jeconiah/Coniah. Matthew's genealogy includes the cursed king, which means Jesus' legal lineage passed through the curse. The theological resolution in Matthew is that Jesus is born of a virgin (not physically descended through Coniah's curse-bearing lineage) but legally inherits the Davidic throne through Joseph's legal paternity.

23.1: Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter the sheep of my pasture! declares YHWH. Chapter 23 opens with a comprehensive indictment of Judah's leadership under the shepherd metaphor that was ubiquitous in ancient Near Eastern royal ideology. In Mesopotamian tradition, the king as shepherd (re'û in Akkadian) was one of the primary royal titles — the king who gathers and cares for the people-flock. Jeremiah's woe reverses this: the shepherds of Israel have been the destroyers and scatterers rather than the gatherers and protectors.
23.2: Therefore thus says YHWH, the God of Israel, concerning the shepherds who care for my people: You have scattered my flock and have driven them away, and you have not attended to them. Behold, I will attend to you for your evil deeds, declares YHWH. The covenant recompense formula is precise: they failed to attend to (paqad) the flock; YHWH will attend to them — but as judge rather than shepherd. The same verb paqad that means caring visitation when YHWH acts on behalf of his people (Exod 3:16; Ruth 1:6) becomes the verb of judicial visitation when directed against covenant violators.
23.3: Then I will gather the remnant of my flock out of all the countries where I have driven them, and I will bring them back to their fold, and they shall be fruitful and multiply. The restoration promise follows immediately on the judgment: YHWH himself will become the shepherd where the human shepherds failed. The fruitful and multiply (paru verabu) formula is the creation blessing (Gen 1:28) applied to the restored remnant — a new-creation community gathered from the nations. The ingathering from all the countries is broader than the Babylonian exile, anticipating a universal diaspora restoration.
23.4: I will set shepherds over them who will care for them, and they shall fear no more, nor be dismayed, neither shall any be missing, declares YHWH. New, faithful shepherds are promised — human leaders who will perform the function the current leaders have abandoned. None shall be missing (velo-yippaqedu) — the same paqad verb again: none will be unaccounted for. The restored flock will be fully complete, not scattered and lost as at present. This anticipates the Good Shepherd discourse of John 10 (I lay down my life for the sheep; I know my own and my own know me; I lose none of all that the Father has given me — John 10:28–29).
23.5: Behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch, and he shall reign as king and deal wisely, and shall execute justice and righteousness in the land. The Righteous Branch (tzemach tsaddiq) oracle is one of the most important messianic passages in the Hebrew Bible. The Branch (tzemach) imagery for the Davidic Messiah appears also in Isaiah 11:1 (a shoot from the stump of Jesse), Zechariah 3:8 and 6:12 (my servant the Branch), and anticipates NT titles for Jesus as the shoot from David's root (Rev 22:16). Deal wisely (hiskil) — the wisdom-king of Isaiah 11:2 and the sapient ideal of Solomonic literature. Justice and righteousness: the paired terms of vv3 and 5 form the defining marks of the messianic reign, contrasting with the failed justice of the current kings.
23.6: In his days Judah will be saved, and Israel will dwell securely. And this is the name by which he will be called: The LORD Is Our Righteousness. YHWH Tsidkenu — The LORD is Our Righteousness — is the messianic king's throne-name. It is a pointed wordplay on the name of the reigning king Zedekiah (Tzidqiyahu = YHWH is my righteousness), who was righteous in name only and whose kingdom was collapsing. The true king who will come will actually embody the righteousness that Zedekiah's name promised but his life denied. Paul picks up this theme: Christ has become for us righteousness (1 Cor 1:30); we become the righteousness of God in him (2 Cor 5:21).
23.7: Therefore, behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when they shall no longer say, As YHWH lives who brought up the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt. The new-Exodus motif from 16:14–15 reappears: the Messiah's reign will inaugurate a new saving act so great that it supersedes the Exodus as Israel's primary confession of divine deliverance. The repetition of this theme (16:14–15 and 23:7–8) signals that it is a central element of Jeremiah's eschatological hope — not replacement of the Exodus memory but its fulfillment in a grander act of divine redemption.
23.8: But as YHWH lives who brought up and led the offspring of the house of Israel out of the north country and out of all the countries where he had driven them. Then they shall dwell in their own land. The new Exodus is from the north country — Babylon — and from all nations. The offspring of the house of Israel is broader than Judah: the promise encompasses the reunification of the divided kingdoms, all twelve tribes gathered into the covenant land. The NT reads this through the lens of Christ's gathering of the scattered children of God from all nations (John 11:52).
23.9: Concerning the prophets: My heart is broken within me; all my bones shake; I am like a drunken man, like a man overcome by wine, because of YHWH and because of his holy words. The oracle against false prophets (vv9–40) begins with Jeremiah's physical response to what he is about to say. The prophet's body is convulsed by the message before he delivers it — a mark of authentic prophetic experience. Because of YHWH and because of his holy words: the holiness of the divine word makes its violation — by false prophets who claim to speak it — a cause of existential anguish for anyone who has genuinely received it.
23.10: For the land is full of adulterers; because of the curse the land mourns, and the pastures of the wilderness are dried up. Their course is evil, and their might is not right. The land's suffering (drought, dried pastures) is linked to spiritual adultery — the metaphor for idolatry and covenant infidelity that runs throughout the prophets (Hosea, Ezekiel 16). The curse (alah) — the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28 — has already taken effect in the landscape. The prophets' course is evil and their might is not right: they are vigorous in the wrong direction.
23.11: Both prophet and priest are ungodly; even in my house I have found their evil, declares YHWH. The combined indictment of prophet and priest (cf. 2:8; 6:13; 8:10) in the Temple itself — the most sacred space — is the worst possible location for religious corruption. Evil in YHWH's house is a direct desecration of the covenant relationship. The Temple, supposed to be the place of divine encounter and covenant maintenance, has become the location of institutionalized spiritual corruption.
23.12: Therefore their way shall be to them like slippery paths in the darkness, into which they shall be driven and fall, for I will bring disaster upon them in the year of their punishment, declares YHWH. The slippery paths in darkness are the paths of those who walk without YHWH's light — the false prophets who lead people into darkness will themselves find no footing. The year of their punishment (shenat pequdatam) is the appointed time of covenant reckoning (cf. 11:23; 46:21; 50:27).
23.13: In the prophets of Samaria I saw an unsavory thing: they prophesied by Baal and led my people Israel astray. The prophets of the northern kingdom (destroyed by Assyria in 722 BC) prophesied by Baal — explicit pagan prophecy. This is presented as unsavory (tiphel, tasteless, disgusting) but at least consistent — they were openly aligned with Baal-worship.
23.14: But in the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen a horrible thing: they commit adultery and walk in lies; they strengthen the hands of evildoers, so that no one turns from his evil; all of them have become like Sodom to me, and its inhabitants like Gomorrah. Jerusalem's prophets are worse than Samaria's: they commit adultery (literal and metaphorical covenant infidelity), walk in lies (false prophecy), and — most damagingly — strengthen the hands of evildoers. False prophecy is not merely error; it actively prevents repentance by telling people what they want to hear. Like Sodom… like Gomorrah: the paradigmatic examples of divine judgment for moral corruption. Jerusalem's prophets have brought the city to Sodom's moral level.
23.15: Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts concerning the prophets: Behold, I will feed them with bitter food and give them poisoned water to drink, for from the prophets of Jerusalem ungodliness has gone out into all the land. The prophets' food and water — wormwood and poisoned water — are the covenant curses turned inward. The spreading of ungodliness (chanuppah, profaneness, pollution) from the capital outward into the entire nation makes the prophets instruments of national corruption. False prophecy is an epidemic disease spread through the religious system.
23.16: Thus says YHWH of hosts: Do not listen to the words of the prophets who prophesy to you, filling you with vain hopes. They speak visions of their own minds, not from the mouth of YHWH. The criterion for false prophecy: visions of their own minds (chazon libam yedabberu, they speak the vision of their heart). The authentic prophetic word originates with YHWH and passes through the prophet; false prophecy originates with the prophet's own desires and preferences and is then falsely attributed to YHWH. Filling you with vain hopes (mehavlim etkhem — making you hevel, vapor, vapor-filling you): the false prophets produce the illusion of security with no substance behind it.
23.17: They say continually to those who despise the word of YHWH, It shall be well with you; and to everyone who stubbornly follows his own heart, they say, No disaster shall come upon you. The specific content of false prophecy: unconditional shalom (peace, well-being) for those who despise YHWH's word and follow their own stubborn hearts. This is the pastoral malpractice that Jeremiah has condemned since 6:14 (peace, peace, when there is no peace). False prophecy enables wickedness by removing the covenant consequences. It is the prophetic equivalent of grace that allows sin to continue (cf. Rom 6:1).
23.18: For who among them has stood in the council of YHWH to see and to hear his word, or who has paid attention to his word and listened? The divine council (sod YHWH) is the heavenly assembly where YHWH deliberates and makes decisions, attended by the heavenly beings (cf. 1 Kgs 22:19–23; Isa 6:1–8; Job 1–2; Ps 82:1). The prophetic claim to authority was grounded in actual access to this council — the prophet is YHWH's messenger from the divine assembly. Amos 3:7 states the principle: YHWH does nothing without revealing his secret (sod) to his servants the prophets. The false prophets have never had this access; they speak without council authorization.
23.19: Behold, the storm of YHWH! Wrath has gone forth, a whirling tempest; it will burst upon the head of the wicked. The storm-theophany announces the divine judgment that the false prophets denied would come. The whirling tempest (sa'ar mitchollel) is both cosmic imagery and specific historical event — the Babylonian army advancing like a divine storm. The false prophets said no disaster shall come (v17); the divine storm is already in motion.
23.20: The anger of YHWH will not turn back until he has executed and accomplished the intents of his heart. In the latter days you will understand it clearly. The judgment is irreversible until it has run its course. In the latter days you will understand it clearly (be'acharit hayamim titbonenu bah binah): the people who are currently refusing to believe Jeremiah's message will understand it when events unfold. Historical fulfillment will produce the theological clarity that prophetic announcement could not. This is not vindictive but pastoral: the understanding will come, even if too late to prevent the judgment.
23.21: I did not send the prophets, yet they ran; I did not speak to them, yet they prophesied. The two criteria of authentic prophecy restated negatively: I did not send (cf. 14:14 — the sent/not-sent criterion of Deut 18:20) and I did not speak to them. The false prophets' activity has all the outward form of prophecy — running, prophesying, delivering oracles — without the divine commissioning or content. It is form without substance, performance without divine backing.
23.22: But if they had stood in my council, then they would have proclaimed my words to the people, and they would have turned them from their evil way, and from the evil of their deeds. The counterfactual: had the false prophets genuinely stood in YHWH's council, they would have proclaimed the actual divine word — which would have produced repentance. The test of true prophecy is ultimately its fruit: a message that enables people to turn from evil is more likely to be from YHWH than one that confirms them in it. The fruitlessness of the false prophets' ministry is itself evidence of its inauthenticity.
23.23: Am I a God at hand, declares YHWH, and not a God far away? One of Jeremiah's most theologically significant statements on divine omnipresence. The question anticipates the assumption of the false prophets: that YHWH can be treated as a local deity (elohim mikarov, a nearby god) whose activity is spatially limited. If YHWH were merely a local temple-deity, the false prophets could claim access to his immediate presence while he remained ignorant of their actual standing in the heavenly council.
23.24: Can a man hide himself in secret places so that I cannot see him? declares YHWH. Do I not fill heaven and earth? declares YHWH. The divine omnipresence is asserted as the basis for divine omniscience: I fill heaven and earth (et-hashamayim ve'et ha'aretz ani male'). There is no hiding place. The false prophets who deceive the people cannot deceive YHWH — he sees the vision of their own hearts (v16) for what it is. The omnipresence argument defeats the false prophets' possibility of operating in YHWH's name without YHWH's knowledge.
23.25: I have heard what the prophets have said who prophesy lies in my name, saying, I have dreamed, I have dreamed! Dreams as prophetic vehicles were standard in the ancient Near East — Egyptian dream interpretation was a royal science; Mesopotamian dream-omens filled omen-text collections. Dreams were a legitimate medium of divine communication in Israel (Gen 28:12; 37:5–11; 1 Kgs 3:5). The false prophets exploit this legitimate form: I have dreamed is a claim to divine revelation through the standard channel, but the content is self-generated.
23.26: How long shall there be lies in the heart of the prophets who prophesy lies, and who prophesy the deceit of their own heart? The question how long? (ad matay) presses toward the end of the false-prophecy epidemic. The heart is the source of both true prophecy (YHWH writes on hearts, 31:33) and false prophecy (the prophets speak from their own hearts, v16). The difference is whether the heart has been shaped by the divine word or has merely generated its own desires and labeled them divine.
23.27: Who think to make my people forget my name by their dreams that they tell one another, even as their fathers forgot my name for Baal. The false prophets' dreams function in exactly the same way as Baal worship: they cause the people to forget YHWH's name (lishekoach et-shemi). The goal of authentic prophecy is to keep YHWH's name in the forefront of national consciousness; the effect of false prophecy is name-forgetting — the same apostasy that the entire book of Jeremiah is diagnosing.
23.28: Let the prophet who has a dream tell the dream, but let him who has my word speak my word faithfully. What has straw in common with wheat? declares YHWH. The straw-versus-wheat contrast is the criterion of prophetic content. Wheat is substantive, nourishing, real; straw is the chaff left after threshing — light, worthless, blown away. Dream-prophecy and the genuine word of YHWH are as different as straw and wheat. Jeremiah does not forbid dream-prophecy but relativizes it: let those who have dreams tell them, but they should not be confused with the actual divine word.
23.29: Is not my word like fire, declares YHWH, and like a hammer that breaks the rock in pieces? Two images for the power of the genuine divine word: fire that burns and consumes (cf. 20:9 — the burning fire shut up in Jeremiah's bones), and a hammer that breaks rock. The word of YHWH is not a gentle suggestion but an irresistible force that shatters even the hardest resistance. The contrast with dream-prophecy (straw, v28) could not be more stark: straw drifts in the wind; the hammer word breaks rock.
23.30: Therefore, behold, I am against the prophets, declares YHWH, who steal my words from one another. The false prophets plagiarize — they steal divine words (ganvim devari) from each other. The charge of stealing could mean appropriating authentic prophetic oracles and falsely applying them, or recycling each other's fabricated oracles as if they were divine revelation. The communal circulation of false prophecy creates an echo-chamber effect: each prophet reinforces the others, producing an illusion of corroborating testimony.
23.31: Behold, I am against the prophets, declares YHWH, who use their tongues and declare, declares the Lord. The formula ne'um YHWH (declares the LORD / oracle of YHWH) appears hundreds of times in the prophetic books as the stamp of divine authority. The false prophets have mastered the formula without having received the divine word it is supposed to authenticate. The form of prophetic authority (declares the LORD) is used to give false content the appearance of divine backing.
23.32: Behold, I am against those who prophesy lying dreams, declares YHWH, and who tell them and lead my people astray by their lies and their recklessness, when I did not send them or charge them. So they do not profit this people at all, declares YHWH. The triple indictment of false prophets — lying dreams, recklessness, unsent — concludes with the practical criterion: they do not profit this people at all. The test of prophetic ministry is its actual benefit to the community. False prophecy, whatever its form, produces no genuine spiritual profit because it leads people away from the covenant relationship that alone provides real benefit.
23.33: When one of this people, or a prophet or a priest asks you, What is the burden of YHWH? you shall say to them, You are the burden, and I will cast you off, declares YHWH. The massa oracle (vv33–40) is a complex wordplay. Massa means both burden and oracle/prophetic pronouncement. The false prophets have been using massa as a standard designation for their utterances — the oracle of YHWH. Jeremiah turns the word against them: you are the burden. The double meaning creates a devastating pun: the people who keep asking for YHWH's oracle are themselves the burden YHWH will cast off.
23.34: And as for the prophet, priest, or one of the people who says, The burden of YHWH, I will punish that man and his household. The prohibition on using massa (burden/oracle) for prophetic utterances is radical: YHWH is banning the standard prophetic formula because it has been so thoroughly corrupted. The corruption of religious language — using the right words to convey wrong content — is treated as a capital-level offense, extending punishment to the entire household.
23.35: Thus shall you say, every one to his neighbor and every one to his brother: What has YHWH answered? or What has YHWH spoken? An alternative formula is provided: instead of what is the burden of YHWH? (the corrupted formula), the people should ask what has YHWH answered? What has YHWH spoken? The distinction is subtle but real: the new formula emphasizes receptivity (what has he answered/spoken?) rather than a title for the prophetic genre (burden). It positions the speaker as recipient rather than authoritative deliverer.
23.36: But the burden of YHWH you shall mention no more, for the burden is every man's own word, and you pervert the words of the living God, YHWH of hosts, our God. The ban on massa: the formula has been so colonized by false prophecy that it now means the opposite of what it should — every man's own word masquerading as divine speech. To use it is to pervert the words of the living God (vatehafechu et-divrei Elohim chayyim). The divine title living God (Elohim chayyim) — the God who is actually alive, who actually speaks — stands against the dead idols and their fraudulent prophets.
23.37: Thus you shall say to the prophet: What has YHWH answered you? or What has YHWH spoken? The alternative formula is to be used even when addressing prophets. The prophet is not exempt from the standard of honest reporting — a genuine prophet will have an actual divine answer to report. The question puts the prophet on notice that YHWH's actual speaking (or non-speaking) is the real question, not the form of the prophetic delivery.
23.38: But if you say, The burden of YHWH, thus says YHWH: Because you have said these words, The burden of YHWH, when I sent to you, saying, You shall not say, The burden of YHWH. The judgment for continued use of the banned formula is severe: YHWH has explicitly commanded against it; to continue using it is deliberate disobedience of a direct divine command. The severity of the coming punishment matches the deliberateness of the violation.
23.39: Therefore, behold, I will surely lift you up and cast you away from my presence, you and the city that I gave to you and your fathers. The punishment for false prophecy and the perversion of divine speech is total: lifting up and casting away from YHWH's presence. The verb nasa (lift/carry away) continues the massa wordplay: you wanted to carry YHWH's burden-oracle; YHWH will carry you — to exile. The city given to them and their fathers will also be taken away: the covenant gift is revoked.
23.40: And I will bring upon you everlasting reproach and perpetual shame, that will not be forgotten. The chapter ends with the permanent shaming of false prophets: everlasting reproach and perpetual shame (cherpat olam ubushat olam). In the honor-shame culture of the ancient Near East, permanent shame was the worst possible social fate — the destruction of one's name and legacy. The false prophets who sought honor through claiming divine speech will receive everlasting dishonor. Their names will be remembered — but as bywords for prophetic fraud.

24.1: After Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had taken into exile from Jerusalem Jeconiah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, together with the officials of Judah, the craftsmen, and the metal workers, and had brought them to Babylon, YHWH showed me this vision: behold, two baskets of figs placed before the Temple of YHWH. Chapter 24 is dated to after the first Babylonian deportation of 597 BC, when Jehoiachin and the first group of exiles (including Ezekiel and the skilled artisans who made the city defensible) were taken to Babylon. The vision of the two fig baskets is one of the most theologically counterintuitive in Jeremiah: those who have been deported (seemingly punished) are the good figs; those who remain in Jerusalem (seemingly preserved) are the bad figs.
24.2: One basket had very good figs, like first-ripe figs, but the other basket had very bad figs, so bad that they could not be eaten. The contrast is absolute: very good (tovot me'od) versus very bad (ra'ot me'od), so bad as to be inedible. The two baskets represent two populations — not two categories of personal righteousness but two groups defined by their relationship to YHWH's covenant judgment: those who accepted the exile as YHWH's discipline and those who resisted or evaded it. The quality of the figs is not earned but determined by YHWH's sovereign disposition toward each group.
24.3: And YHWH said to me, What do you see, Jeremiah? I said, Figs, the good figs very good, and the bad figs very bad, so bad that they cannot be eaten. The dialogical form (what do you see?) appears in the call narrative (1:11, 13 — what do you see?) and in Amos 7–8 (amos: what do you see?). The question-answer form involves the prophet actively in the revelation process: he names what he sees before receiving the interpretation. This participatory reception marks authentic prophetic experience.
24.4: Then the word of YHWH came to me — the interpretive oracle follows the vision. The two-stage structure (vision + word) is standard in prophetic literature. The interpretation will make the theological point that the natural reading of the situation (those deported are punished; those remaining are preserved) is the exact reverse of YHWH's actual assessment.
24.5: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Like these good figs, so I will regard as good the exiles from Judah, whom I have sent away from this place to the land of the Chaldeans. The exile is reframed: YHWH sent the exiles — they were not merely conquered but providentially directed. The theological claim is radical: the Babylonian deportation of 597 BC was YHWH's act of grace disguised as judgment. Those removed to Babylon are under YHWH's good disposition (akkirem letovah, I will acknowledge them for good). The exile is not abandonment but a form of covenant protection — removing the remnant from the coming total destruction of Jerusalem.
24.6: I will set my eyes on them for good, and I will bring them back to this land. I will build them up, and not tear them down; I will plant them, and not uproot them. The restoration promise for the good-fig exiles uses the construction/planting vocabulary from Jeremiah's call (1:10) — but now entirely in the positive register: build… not tear down; plant… not uproot. The six terms of 1:10 (pluck up, break down, destroy, overthrow, build, plant) are reduced here to the two final positive terms. The work of judgment is complete; the work of restoration can begin.
24.7: I will give them a heart to know me, that I am YHWH, and they shall be my people and I will be their God, for they shall return to me with their whole heart. The good-fig exiles will receive a heart to know (lev lada'at) YHWH — a phrase that anticipates the New Covenant of 31:33 (I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts). The covenant formula they shall be my people and I will be their God is the center of the entire covenant tradition, from Sinai through the prophets to Revelation 21:3. Whole heart (lev shalem): total covenant orientation, the undivided heart that Deuteronomy 6:5 commanded (love YHWH with all your heart).
24.8: But like the bad figs that are so bad they cannot be eaten, so will I treat Zedekiah the king of Judah, his officials, the remnant of Jerusalem who remain in this land, and those who dwell in the land of Egypt. The bad figs include three groups: Zedekiah and his court (who remained in Jerusalem after 597), the Jerusalem remnant (those not deported), and the Egyptian community (those who fled to Egypt rather than submit to Babylon). The theological logic: all three groups avoided the exile — either by remaining in the land or by fleeing to Egypt — and YHWH regards all three as bad figs. Evasion of YHWH's appointed judgment is itself a form of covenant rejection.
24.9: I will make them a horror, a calamity, to all the kingdoms of the earth, to be a reproach, a byword, a taunt, and a curse in all the places where I shall drive them. The bad-fig fate is a comprehensive shame-catalogue: horror, calamity, reproach, byword, taunt, curse. Each term represents a different dimension of public disgrace in the ancient Near East. A byword (mashal) means one's name becomes a proverb of misfortune. A curse (qelalah) means one's fate is invoked when threatening others with the worst possible outcome. The covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:37 (become a byword, a taunt among all the nations) are precisely fulfilled.
24.10: And I will send sword, famine, and pestilence upon them, until they shall be utterly destroyed from the land that I gave to them and their fathers. The final verse applies the judgment triad to the bad figs: sword, famine, pestilence — the same three instruments announced throughout Jeremiah (14:12; 15:2; 21:7–9). Utterly destroyed from the land: the bad-fig population loses all covenant standing in the covenant land. The land, YHWH's gift to the fathers, will be stripped from those who refused to submit to YHWH's judgment. The chapter ends on this note of total judgment for evasion — making the theological point that accepting YHWH's correction leads to restoration, while evading it leads to destruction.

25.1: Chapter 25 provides a programmatic summary of Jeremiah's ministry at a pivotal historical moment: the word that came to Jeremiah concerning all the people of Judah, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah (that was the first year of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon). The convergence of dates is significant: 605 BC is when Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt at Carchemish and effectively became the dominant power in the ancient Near East. This is the moment when the abstract north-foe of Jeremiah's earlier oracles becomes a historically identified, present military reality.
25.2: Jeremiah the prophet spoke to all the people of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem. The audience is universal within Judah — not just the Temple crowd or the royal court but the entire population of the nation and capital. The scope of the address matches the scope of the indictment: everyone has been involved in the covenant violation, and everyone will be subject to the coming judgment.
25.3: For twenty-three years, from the thirteenth year of Josiah the son of Amon, king of Judah, to this day, the word of YHWH has come to me, and I have spoken persistently to you, but you have not listened. Jeremiah's ministry began in 627 BC, the thirteenth year of Josiah. Twenty-three years of preaching — through Josiah's reform, through Josiah's death, through Jehoahaz, through Jehoiakim — and the people have not listened. The inventory of faithful prophecy unheeded makes the coming judgment all the more inexorable: YHWH has exhausted the ordinary channels of covenant communication.
25.4: You have neither listened nor inclined your ears to hear, although YHWH persistently sent to you all his servants the prophets. The full prophetic tradition is invoked: not just Jeremiah but the entire succession of covenant messengers YHWH sent before him. Persistently sent (hashkem veshloach, rising early and sending) — the idiom of diligent, urgent effort. YHWH did not send prophets occasionally; he sent them relentlessly, rising early in his urgency to communicate the covenant message. The people consistently refused to incline their ears.
25.5: They said, Turn now, every one of you, from his evil way and evil deeds, and dwell upon the land that YHWH has given to you and your fathers from of old and forever. The content of the prophets' message was simple: repent (shuvu, turn) and remain in the covenant land. The land was given from of old (me'olam) and forever (ad-olam) — the unconditional Abrahamic promise of the land. But the conditional Sinaitic covenant determines whether the current generation occupies it.
25.6: Do not go after other gods to serve and worship them, or provoke me to anger with the work of your hands. Then I will do you no harm. The covenant demand in its simplest form: one God, no idols. Provoke me to anger with the work of your hands (lehach'iseni bema'aseh yedekhem) — idol worship is an act of deliberate provocation, not innocent religious diversity. The promise I will do you no harm frames the entire warning negatively: the prophetic message has been mercy all along, offering escape from the judgment that the people's own choices are drawing down on themselves.
25.7: Yet you have not listened to me, declares YHWH, that you might provoke me to anger with the work of your hands to your own harm. The irony of self-destructive rebellion: by refusing to listen, they have provoked YHWH to their own harm (lema'an hara' lachem). They thought idol-worship served their interests — security, fertility, political alignment with the dominant cultures. They were wrong: it was harm to themselves, guaranteed.
25.8: Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts: Because you have not obeyed my words. The therefore (lachen) is the pivot from indictment to judgment — the logical-covenantal connection between the people's disobedience and YHWH's response. The judgment that follows is not arbitrary; it is the consequence directly connected to the cause.
25.9: Behold, I will send for all the tribes of the north, declares YHWH, and for Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, my servant, and I will bring them against this land and its inhabitants, and against all these surrounding nations. I will devote them to destruction, and make them a horror, a hissing, and an everlasting desolation. Nebuchadnezzar my servant (avdi) — one of the most remarkable designations in the entire book. Eved (servant) is the term for Israel's covenant mediators: Moses was YHWH's servant (Deut 34:5), David was YHWH's servant (2 Sam 7:5), the prophets are YHWH's servants (Jer 7:25; 25:4). Now the pagan king of Babylon receives the same title — not as a convert but as an instrument of covenant judgment. YHWH's sovereignty over history extends to using unbelieving empires for covenantal purposes.
25.10: And I will banish from them the voice of mirth and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride, the grinding of the millstones and the light of the lamp. The silencing of Jerusalem catalogues the sounds and lights of normal daily life: wedding celebration (cf. 7:34; 16:9 — the returning voice of bride and bridegroom marks restoration in 33:11), grain-grinding (the rhythm of domestic food preparation), lamplight (the sign of inhabited homes). All will be extinguished. The city that was full of the sounds of life will become silent as a tomb.
25.11: This whole land shall become a ruin and a waste, and these nations shall serve the king of Babylon seventy years. The seventy years oracle is one of the most discussed chronological statements in the Hebrew Bible. Seventy was a round number of conventional completeness in the ancient Near East — a full human lifespan (Ps 90:10), a number associated with divine deliberations (Deut 32:8 — seventy nations), and a period long enough to encompass multiple generations. An Esarhaddon inscription describes Babylon's desolation by Sennacherib in 689 BC as a seventy-year punishment (later numerically inverted to eleven years). The seventy years functioned as a symbol of thorough, complete judgment that would outlast the generation that caused it.
25.12: Then after seventy years are completed, I will punish the king of Babylon and that nation, the land of the Chaldeans, for their iniquity, declares YHWH, making the land an everlasting waste. The judgment of Babylon is announced from the outset: YHWH's use of Babylon as his instrument does not exempt Babylon from accountability for its own cruelty and pride. The Babylonian empire that serves YHWH's purposes will itself face YHWH's judgment when its instrument-role is complete. This dual structure — Babylon as servant and Babylon as sinner — runs through chapters 50–51 where the oracles against Babylon are concentrated.
25.13: I will bring upon that land all the words that I have uttered against it, everything written in this book, which Jeremiah prophesied against all the nations. The reference to this book (hasefer hazeh) suggests that by the time of chapter 25's composition/compilation, a written collection of Jeremiah's oracles already existed. The oracles against the nations (chapters 46–51) are thus anticipated here. The judgment of Babylon brings the entire written prophetic corpus to completion: what was written will be executed.
25.14: For many nations and great kings shall make slaves even of them, and I will recompense them according to their deeds and the work of their hands. Babylon's fall is described as subjugation: the great empire that made slaves of nations will itself become enslaved. The covenant-recompense formula (according to their deeds and work of their hands) is applied universally — the standard that judged Judah's idolatry will judge Babylon's imperial cruelty.
25.15: Thus YHWH, the God of Israel, said to me: Take from my hand this cup of the wine of wrath, and make all the nations to whom I send you drink it. The cup of wrath oracle (vv15–29) is one of the most vivid prophetic sign-acts in the book — though whether Jeremiah literally carried a cup to each nation or the action was visionary and symbolic is debated. The cup of YHWH's wrath (kos hachemah) is a judicial image: the defeated king is forced by the victor to drink the cup of defeat and humiliation. YHWH as cosmic king forces the nations to drink his judgment.
25.16: They shall drink and stagger and be crazed because of the sword that I am sending among them. The wine of wrath produces not intoxicating pleasure but disorienting madness (hitgale'lu vehitholalu, stagger and act like madmen) — a picture of armies and peoples thrown into confusion and panic by YHWH's judgment. The sword sent among them is Babylon, now YHWH's instrument for universal judgment.
25.17: So I took the cup from YHWH's hand, and made all the nations to whom YHWH sent me drink it. Jeremiah's obedient reception and administration of the cup follows the typical sign-act pattern: YHWH commands, Jeremiah executes. The prophet's role as YHWH's agent in distributing judgment mirrors his role as messenger of the divine word — both activities make the divine will concrete in the world.
25.18: Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, its kings and officials, to make them a desolation and a waste, a hissing and a curse, as at this day. Jerusalem and Judah are listed first among the cup-recipients — judgment begins with the house of YHWH (cf. 1 Pet 4:17; Ezek 9:6). The phrase as at this day suggests a post-587 perspective — the narrator is looking back at a Jerusalem that has already been destroyed. This is consistent with the editorial layers of the book.
25.19: Pharaoh king of Egypt, his servants, his officials, all his people, and all the foreign mix; all the kings of the land of Uz and all the kings of the land of the Philistines (Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron, and the remnant of Ashdod). The cup-list encompasses Egypt (the southern power Judah sought as an ally against Babylon), the land of Uz (associated with Job — the eastern desert region), and the Philistine cities. The geographic sweep moves from Judah's immediate neighbors to more distant nations, building toward the comprehensive judgment of all nations.
25.20: The nations receiving the cup of wrath continue: Edom, Moab, the sons of Ammon — the Transjordanian neighbors whose complex relationship with Israel included ancient kinship (Edom = Esau, Moab and Ammon = Lot's sons) alongside persistent hostility. Their inclusion in the cup-judgment is significant: covenantal proximity does not confer immunity. All the kings of Tyre, all the kings of Sidon, and the kings of the coastland across the sea — the Phoenician maritime cities that controlled Mediterranean trade.
25.21: The cup-list continues across the eastern and southern regions: Dedan, Tema, Buz, and all who cut the corners of their hair — Arabian tribes and territories. The description who cut the corners of their hair (qotzei pe'ah) refers to a distinctive desert-tribe practice of shaving the temples, mentioned in Herodotus and associated with various Arabian groups. The Torah prohibited this practice for Israelites (Lev 19:27) precisely because of its association with non-Israelite religious customs.
25.22: All the kings of Arabia and all the kings of the mixed tribes who dwell in the desert; all the kings of Zimri, all the kings of Elam, and all the kings of Media. The cup-list moves east: Elam (southwestern Iran, east of Babylon) and Media (northwestern Iran) — both of which will eventually be part of the coalition that overthrows Babylon (cf. Isa 13:17: I will stir up the Medes against them). The inclusion of Babylon's future destroyers in the cup-list anticipates the reversal of fortunes that chapters 50–51 will develop.
25.23: All the kings of the north, far and near, one after another, and all the kingdoms of the world that are on the face of the earth. And after them the king of Babylon shall drink. The cup-list culminates with the global sweep — all kingdoms of the world — and then Babylon itself. The sequential structure is important: Babylon is the last to drink, having served as the instrument of YHWH's judgment on all the others. Babylon's cup comes after it has executed the others' judgment. The structure mirrors the oracles against the nations in chapters 46–51, where Babylon occupies the longest and culminating section.
25.24: The nations listed span the full known world of the 7th-century BCE Near East: Egypt, Arabia, Philistia, Phoenicia, Transjordan, Persia, Media. The comprehensiveness of the list communicates YHWH's universal sovereignty. He is not a regional deity whose judgment is limited to his own territory; his cup of wrath reaches every kingdom under heaven.
25.25: The cup-list continues with more specific identifications of Arabian and desert-fringe peoples: Zimri (possibly in Arabia), the kings of Elam, and the extensive listing builds the picture of a global judgment that leaves no nation exempt. The theological claim embedded in the cup-oracle is the same claim Jeremiah made in chapters 10 and 23: YHWH is the God of all nations, not merely of Israel.
25.26: And after them the king of Babylon shall drink — the culmination and reversal: the cup-bearer becomes the cup-drinker. The instrument of judgment becomes the recipient of judgment. Sheshach (an Atbash cipher for Babel/Babylon — substituting reversed alphabet letters) appears here and in 51:41. The cipher may have served originally as a form of discretion when writing about Babylon was politically dangerous, or as a literary device signaling that the readers are meant to decode the reference.
25.27: Then you shall say to them, Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Drink, be drunk and vomit, fall and rise no more, because of the sword that I am sending among you.
