The vomiting and falling-to-rise-no-more moves beyond the metaphor of disorienting intoxication to the full picture of those killed in battle — the nations struck down by the sword will not recover. Rise no more signals the permanence of the judgment on nations that resist YHWH's sovereign authority.
25.28: And if they refuse to accept the cup from your hand to drink, then you shall say to them, Thus says YHWH of hosts: You must drink! The nations' resistance to the cup-judgment is addressed: even if they refuse, they must drink. The sovereign compulsion is absolute — YHWH's judgment cannot be declined by will or political calculation. The rhetorical force anticipates the nations' response to Jeremiah's message: they will not want to accept the judgment-cup, but the announcement of it is itself the beginning of its execution.
25.29: For behold, I begin to work disaster at the city that is called by my name, and shall you go unpunished? You shall not go unpunished, for I am summoning a sword against all the inhabitants of the earth, declares YHWH of hosts. The theological logic of universal judgment: if YHWH judges the city that bears his name — Jerusalem, the most privileged and protected place on earth — then no other city or nation can claim exemption. The judgment of his own people is the proof that YHWH's standard is universal, not tribal. Paul uses similar logic in Romans 2:1–11: the divine impartiality that judged Israel applies equally to Gentiles.
25.30: You, therefore, shall prophesy against them all these words, and say to them: YHWH will roar from on high, and from his holy habitation utter his voice; he will roar mightily against his fold, and shout, like those who tread grapes, against all the inhabitants of the earth. The cosmic theophany of YHWH as the roaring lion (cf. Amos 1:2; Hos 11:10; Joel 3:16 — YHWH roars from Zion) pictures the divine judge making his voice heard across the created order. The harvest-shout of the grape-treaders is a sound of both joy (harvest celebration) and violence (trampling) — YHWH treads the nations as a winepress (cf. Isa 63:1–6; Rev 19:15).
25.31: The clamor will resound to the ends of the earth, for YHWH has an indictment against the nations; he is entering into judgment with all flesh, and the wicked he will put to the sword, declares YHWH. YHWH has an indictment (rib, a covenant lawsuit) against the nations — the legal-covenant framework applied universally. The ends of the earth indicates the scope is no longer national but cosmic. Judgment with all flesh: the entire biological category of mortal life is subject to YHWH's judicial evaluation. This is the most universalizing statement in a universalizing chapter.
25.32: Thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, disaster is going forth from nation to nation, and a great tempest is stirring from the farthest parts of the earth. The disaster cascades: nation to nation, from the farthest ends of the earth toward the center. The military campaigns of the ancient world spread destruction in waves — Babylon conquers one nation, which destabilizes the next, which affects the next. YHWH is the choreographer of this historical cascade.
25.33: And those pierced by YHWH on that day shall extend from one end of the earth to the other. They shall not be lamented, or gathered, or buried; they shall be dung on the surface of the ground. The bodies of the slain scattered across the entire earth, unburied and unlamented, enacts the covenant curse at its most extreme (cf. Deut 28:26) on a global scale. The absence of burial — the most basic human funeral rite — signifies the total collapse of the social order that normally maintains covenant dignity even in death.
25.34: Wail, you shepherds, and cry out, and roll in ashes, you lords of the flock, for the days of your slaughter and dispersion have come, and you shall fall like a choice vessel. The shepherds (national leaders, kings) are called to mourn their own imminent destruction. Roll in ashes (hitpalashtem): ashes on the head was the most extreme public mourning gesture. Fall like a choice vessel: the contrast between the precious ceramic vessel and its fragility — the leaders thought themselves valuable and protected; they will shatter like fine pottery thrown down.
25.35: No refuge will remain for the shepherds, nor escape for the lords of the flock. The military situation is described in terms of total blockade: no escape routes, no refuges. The leaders who fed off their flocks rather than protecting them will find that neither their political resources nor their military alliances provide any escape from YHWH's judgment.
25.36: Hark! The cry of the shepherds, and the wail of the lords of the flock! For YHWH is laying waste their pasture. The cry of the leaders is heard — not the triumphant cry of victory but the wailing of those whose domain is being destroyed. YHWH is laying waste their pasture: the pastures that the leaders controlled (their nations, territories, spheres of influence) are devastated. The shepherd-leaders lose the very lands they exploited.
25.37: And the peaceful folds are devastated because of the fierce anger of YHWH. Peaceful folds (nevot hashalom) — the prosperous, settled territories that once appeared to be under YHWH's blessing. The fierce anger (charon af) — the burning anger of YHWH's covenant indignation — is the force that transforms peaceful prosperity into waste. The transformation is sudden and total.
25.38: Like a lion he has left his lair, for their land has become a waste because of the sword of the oppressor, and because of his fierce anger. The lion-leaving-his-lair image pictures YHWH as a predator emerging from concealment to attack. The oppressor's sword (Babylon, YHWH's instrument) and YHWH's fierce anger operate together — human military action and divine wrath are two aspects of the same judgment event. The chapter ends with the land laid waste: the covenant land that was YHWH's gift has become a wasteland through covenant violation and divine response.

26.1: Chapter 26 is the narrative counterpart to the Temple Sermon of chapter 7: where chapter 7 gives the content of the sermon, chapter 26 gives the story of what happened when Jeremiah delivered it. In the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came from YHWH. The dating to Jehoiakim's accession year (608/607 BC) places this shortly after Josiah's death at Megiddo and Jehoahaz's brief reign followed by Egyptian imposition of Jehoiakim. The political context matters: Jehoiakim was an Egyptian puppet, hostile to the reform traditions of his father Josiah, and deeply opposed to Jeremiah's prophetic message.
26.2: Thus says YHWH: Stand in the court of YHWH's house, and speak to all the cities of Judah that come to worship in the house of YHWH all the words that I command you to speak to them; do not hold back a word. The command do not hold back a word (al tigra' davar) is both a commission and a warning: the completeness of the message is non-negotiable. Any softening of the oracle would constitute disobedience. The Temple court is the most public possible location — pilgrims from all Judah's cities gather there, making it the ideal place for a message to the entire nation.
26.3: It may be they will listen, and every one turn from his evil way, that I may relent of the disaster that I intend to do to them because of their evil deeds. The perhaps (ulai) maintains the possibility of repentance even while the judgment is being announced. The divine flexibility established in chapter 18 (if the nation repents, I relent) is operative here: the sermon is not a mechanical announcement of inevitable doom but a genuine call to repentance with real potential for course-correction. YHWH's sovereign freedom to relent means the prophetic word is never simply fatalistic.
26.4: You shall say to them, Thus says YHWH: If you will not listen to me, to walk in my law that I have set before you. The conditional form: covenant obedience (walking in YHWH's Torah) is the prerequisite for covenant blessing and protection. The failure to listen (shama) and walk (halak) — hearing and living — is the two-part covenant failure that Jeremiah diagnoses throughout the book.
26.5: And to listen to the words of my servants the prophets whom I send to you urgently, though you have not listened. The servants-the-prophets formula recurs (cf. 7:25; 25:4): YHWH has maintained a continuous succession of covenant messengers, sent urgently and persistently. The though you have not listened is the historical record embedded in the invitation — the people's past track record makes the outcome predictable, but the invitation remains genuine.
26.6: Then I will make this house like Shiloh, and I will make this city a curse for all the nations of the earth. The Shiloh comparison is the theological bomb of the Temple Sermon: Shiloh was the original location of the Tabernacle and the Ark before the Philistines captured the Ark (1 Sam 4–6). Shiloh was destroyed — archaeologically confirmed by a destruction layer from the late 11th century BC. If YHWH abandoned Shiloh, the place of his first dwelling among Israel, he can abandon Jerusalem too. The inviolability theology that protected the false prophets' peace-message is demolished by historical precedent.
26.7: The priests and the prophets and all the people heard Jeremiah speaking these words in the house of YHWH. The audience is precisely those who have the most to lose from Jeremiah's message: the priests (Temple establishment), the (false) prophets (who have been announcing peace), and the general population (who have been reassured by the false prophets). All three groups hear the Shiloh comparison simultaneously.
26.8: And when Jeremiah had finished speaking all that YHWH had commanded him to speak to all the people, then the priests and the prophets and all the people laid hold of him, saying, You shall die! The institutional response is immediate violence: you shall die (mot tamut). The death-penalty formula is the legal language of capital punishment. The charge will be prophesying against the Temple and the city (v11) — using Jeremiah's own words as the indictment. False prophesying against a city in the ancient Near East was a capital offense; the priests and false prophets believe they have a legitimate legal basis for execution.
26.9: Why have you prophesied in the name of YHWH, saying, This house shall be like Shiloh, and this city shall be desolate, without inhabitant? And all the people gathered around Jeremiah in the house of YHWH. The accusation quotes Jeremiah's words precisely. The gathering of the people in the Temple court is a proto-judicial assembly — in the ancient Near East, major legal cases were heard in public spaces where the community could witness. The case against Jeremiah will be decided by this assembly.
26.10: When the officials of Judah heard these things, they came up from the king's house to the house of YHWH and took their seat in the entry of the New Gate of the house of YHWH. The officials (sarim) come from the palace to hold a formal hearing at the Temple gate — the standard ancient Near Eastern location for legal proceedings. Their arrival shifts the proceeding from a mob threat to a formal judicial process, which paradoxically increases Jeremiah's chances of survival: mob violence might have killed him immediately; a formal trial gives him the opportunity to speak in his defense.
26.11: Then the priests and the prophets said to the officials and to all the people, This man deserves the sentence of death, because he has prophesied against this city, as you have heard with your own ears. The prosecution presents its case: Jeremiah has committed the capital offense of prophesying against the city. The charge is technically accurate — he did prophesy that the city would be like Shiloh. But the prosecution has confused the form of the message (prophecy of doom) with false prophecy. Jeremiah's message was authorized by YHWH; the false prophets' message was not.
26.12: Then Jeremiah spoke to all the officials and all the people, saying, YHWH sent me to prophesy against this house and this city all the words you have heard. Jeremiah's defense is simple and direct: YHWH sent me (YHWH shalachani). The sent/not-sent criterion that distinguishes true from false prophecy (cf. 14:14; 23:21; 27:15; 28:15) is now Jeremiah's legal defense. If YHWH sent him, executing him is murdering YHWH's messenger — a capital crime itself.
26.13: Now therefore mend your ways and your deeds, and obey the voice of YHWH your God, and YHWH will relent of the disaster that he has pronounced against you. Jeremiah uses his own trial as an opportunity to deliver the core message again: repentance produces divine relenting. Even in the midst of being threatened with death, the prophet offers the path to life. This is the pastoral courage of genuine prophecy — the announcement of judgment is always in service of the call to repentance.
26.14: But as for me, behold, I am in your hands. Do with me as seems good and right to you. The remarkable surrender: Jeremiah places himself entirely in the hands of the court. He does not resist, flee, or compromise the message to save himself. The willingness to die for the message is itself a testimony to its authenticity — a false prophet would recant under threat of death. Jeremiah's acceptance of the court's authority while refusing to alter his message models the prophetic integrity that the book has been teaching throughout.
26.15: Only know for certain that if you put me to death, you will bring innocent blood upon yourselves and upon this city and its inhabitants, for in truth YHWH sent me to you to speak all these words in your ears. The counter-charge: executing Jeremiah will bring innocent blood (dam naqui) on the city. The shedding of innocent blood was itself a capital sin (Deut 19:10–13; 2 Kgs 24:4) and a source of covenant pollution. Jeremiah is warning the court that killing him will not avert judgment but will add a new capital crime to the existing list of covenant violations.
26.16: Then the officials and all the people said to the priests and the prophets, This man does not deserve the sentence of death, for he has spoken to us in the name of YHWH our God. The verdict: acquittal. The officials and people reverse the verdict of the priests and prophets. The theological reason given is precisely the criterion of true prophecy: he has spoken in the name of YHWH our God. The name of YHWH functions as the authorization that distinguishes true from false prophecy. The crowd recognizes something genuine in Jeremiah's bearing and message that the professional religious establishment was suppressing.
26.17: And certain of the elders of the land arose and spoke to all the assembled people, saying. The elders provide historical precedent — the wisdom tradition of the community. In the ancient Near East, elders were the keepers of historical memory; their invocation of past cases established legal precedent. The elders' intervention introduces a crucial historical argument that will shift the case decisively.
26.18: Micah of Moresheth prophesied in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, and said to all the people of Judah: Thus says YHWH of hosts, 'Zion shall be plowed as a field; Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins, and the mountain of the house a wooded height'. The elders cite Micah 3:12 — one of the most famous doom-prophecies in the prophetic tradition. Micah prophesied the destruction of Jerusalem in the late 8th century BC (contemporary with Isaiah). His message was the same as Jeremiah's: Jerusalem will be destroyed because of covenant violation.
26.19: Did Hezekiah king of Judah and all Judah put him to death? Did he not fear YHWH and entreat the favor of YHWH, and did not YHWH relent of the disaster that he had pronounced against them? But we are about to bring great disaster upon ourselves. Hezekiah's response to Micah becomes the legal precedent: the king repented (feared YHWH and entreated his favor) and YHWH relented. Executing Micah would have been a capital crime; Hezekiah wisely accepted the prophetic word. The implication for Jeremiah's case is clear: repeating Hezekiah's wisdom (repentance, not execution) will produce the same result (divine relenting). Repeating the opposite will produce the opposite.
26.20: There was another man who prophesied in the name of YHWH, Uriah the son of Shemaiah from Kiriath-jearim. He prophesied against this city and against this land in words like those of Jeremiah. The counter-example: Uriah, a contemporary of Jeremiah who also prophesied doom against Jerusalem and Judah. Uriah's case represents the worst-case scenario — what happens when the king executes a true prophet. The detail that he prophesied in words like those of Jeremiah confirms that Jeremiah was not speaking an unprecedented or aberrant message but a consistent covenant warning.
26.21: And when King Jehoiakim, with all his warriors and all the officials, heard his words, the king sought to put him to death. But when Uriah heard of it, he was afraid and fled and escaped to Egypt. Uriah's fear led him to flee to Egypt — the typical destination for Judahite political refugees. But Egypt was no sanctuary from a king who had Pharaoh Neco's support. The flight to Egypt is presented as human prudence overriding prophetic commitment.
26.22: Then King Jehoiakim sent to Egypt certain men, Elnathan the son of Achbor and others with him, and they took Uriah from Egypt and brought him to King Jehoiakim, who struck him down with the sword and dumped his body in the burial place of the common people. Uriah was extradited from Egypt and executed — the donkey-burial threat of 22:19 enacted on a lesser-known prophet before Jehoiakim. The burial place of the common people is a dishonorable mass grave, not a family tomb — the maximum available indignity for the executed. The contrast with Jeremiah's acquittal is deliberate: Uriah fled and was executed; Jeremiah stayed and was acquitted. Faithfulness in the face of threat produced a better outcome than flight.
26.23: Verse 23 summarizes the Uriah incident already described: his extradition, execution, and dishonorable burial. The narrative detail underlines the political reality of Jehoiakim's reign — this is a king who killed prophets. Jeremiah's survival is therefore all the more remarkable and requires the explanation provided in the next verse.
26.24: But the hand of Ahikam the son of Shaphan was with Jeremiah so that he was not given over to the people to be put to death. Ahikam ben Shaphan — a prominent court official whose family will appear throughout Jeremiah. Shaphan was the scribe who read the law-scroll to Hezekiah (2 Kgs 22:8–14) during Josiah's reforms; Ahikam was part of the delegation sent to Huldah the prophetess (2 Kgs 22:12–14); Gedaliah ben Ahikam will become the governor of Judah after Jerusalem's fall (Jer 40:5). The Shaphan family represents a thread of covenant fidelity running through the political establishment — a remnant of faithful officials who protected YHWH's prophet.

27.1: Chapter 27 introduces one of Jeremiah's most confrontational sign-acts: the yoke. In the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from YHWH. There is a textual discrepancy: the narrative that follows (vv3, 12) refers to Zedekiah, not Jehoiakim. Most scholars conclude the chapter belongs to Zedekiah's reign (c. 594 BC, just after a coalition of western nations met to discuss rebellion against Babylon), and the Jehoiakim reference is a scribal error or an older heading that was not updated. The Zedekiah setting is confirmed by the parallel narrative in chapter 28.
27.2: Thus YHWH said to me: Make yourself straps and yoke bars, and put them on your neck. The yoke (ol) is a wooden frame fitting over the neck and shoulders of a draft animal, used to harness it to a plow or wagon. Wearing a yoke publicly was the ultimate symbol of subjugation — a human being reduced to the status of a work animal under a master's authority. Jeremiah is commanded to wear this yoke as he delivers his message to foreign ambassadors, making the physical object an extension of the spoken oracle.
27.3: Send word to the king of Edom, the king of Moab, the king of the sons of Ammon, the king of Tyre, and the king of Sidon by the hand of the envoys who have come to Jerusalem to Zedekiah king of Judah. The historical occasion: representatives of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon had gathered in Jerusalem — likely to discuss a coordinated rebellion against Babylonian control. Jeremiah sends yoke-messages to all five kings through their own ambassadors: they should each receive not just a verbal message but an additional symbolic object — a yoke of their own.
27.4: Give them this charge for their masters: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: this is what you shall say to your masters. The message is delivered by Jeremiah but addressed to the kings through their envoys. The political audacity is remarkable: Jeremiah is effectively counter-lobbying the diplomatic conference that had gathered in Jerusalem to plan resistance to Babylon. His counter-message is the theological opposite of what the coalition was there to promote.
27.5: It is I who by my great power and my outstretched arm have made the earth, with the men and animals that are on the earth, and I give it to whoever it seems right to me. The creation-sovereignty claim grounds the political message: YHWH made the earth and all its inhabitants — human and animal. The sovereign creator has the right to allocate political dominion as he wills. Outstretched arm (zero'i netuyah) is the Exodus-war language (Exod 6:6; Deut 4:34) — the same power that liberated Israel from Egypt now assigns the earth's nations to Nebuchadnezzar's governance.
27.6: Now I have given all these lands into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar, the king of Babylon, my servant, and I have given him also the beasts of the field to serve him. The my servant designation for Nebuchadnezzar reappears from 25:9. The addition of the beasts of the field to serve him extends the sovereignty claim: even the wild animals of the region are under Nebuchadnezzar's providential authority during this period. The theological claim is staggering in its scope: YHWH has assigned world dominion to a pagan king as an act of divine sovereignty.
27.7: All the nations shall serve him and his son and his grandson, until the time of his own land comes. Then many nations and great kings shall make him their slave. The temporal limit on Babylonian dominion: three generations (Nebuchadnezzar, his son, his grandson) — then Babylon itself will be subjugated. The historical fulfillment came with the Persian conquest of Babylon in 539 BC under Cyrus. The theological logic is consistent: Babylon is a temporary instrument of YHWH's judgment, not a permanent world empire. All human empires operate within YHWH's permissive will for a limited period.
27.8: But if any nation or kingdom will not serve this Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and put its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon, I will punish that nation with the sword, with famine, and with pestilence, declares YHWH, until I have consumed it by his hand. Resistance to Babylon is resistance to YHWH's appointed order for this historical period. The judgment triad (sword, famine, pestilence) applies to nations that resist — using the same instruments YHWH uses to judge Judah. The political counsel to submit to Babylon is thus simultaneously covenant theology: accept YHWH's judgment as the path to survival.
27.9: So do not listen to your prophets, your diviners, your dreamers, your fortune-tellers, or your sorcerers, who are saying to you, You shall not serve the king of Babylon. The false prophets of the surrounding nations are giving the same message as Judah's false prophets: do not submit to Babylon. The entire prophetic establishment of the region is aligning against Jeremiah's counter-message. Diviners, dreamers, fortune-tellers, and sorcerers — the full range of ANE prophetic and mantic practices — are all delivering the politically comfortable message of resistance.
27.10: For it is a lie that they are prophesying to you, with the result that you will be removed far from your land, and I will drive you out, and you will perish. The false prophets' message is a lie (sheqer) — not merely incorrect information but a deliberate deception that leads to destruction. The nations that trust the false prophets and resist Babylon will be removed from their lands. The specific threat of removal from the land applies to Gentile nations in the same way it applies to Israel: the connection between covenant faithfulness and land-tenure operates across national boundaries.
27.11: But any nation that will bring its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serve him, I will leave on its own land, to work it and dwell there, declares YHWH. Submission to Babylon guarantees land-retention. The paradox is complete: what appears to be humiliating defeat (wearing the yoke) is actually the path to survival and continued possession of the land. What appears to be honorable resistance is actually the path to destruction and exile. YHWH's ways are not the ways of human political logic.
27.12: To Zedekiah king of Judah I spoke in like manner: Bring your necks under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serve him and his people and live. The message to Zedekiah is identical to the message to the foreign kings: submit and live. The political theology applies to the Davidic king as much as to the pagan rulers surrounding him. Zedekiah repeatedly consulted Jeremiah (chs. 21, 34, 37, 38) but ultimately could not bring himself to surrender — partly from fear of the Judaean anti-Babylonian faction (38:19). His inability to act on Jeremiah's counsel sealed Jerusalem's fate.
27.13: Why will you and your people die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence, as YHWH has spoken concerning any nation that will not serve the king of Babylon? The rhetorical question pressing Zedekiah toward action: the three instruments of judgment are preventable. Submission averts them; resistance guarantees them. The simplicity of the choice makes the failure to choose wisely all the more tragic.
27.14: Do not listen to the words of the prophets who are saying to you, You shall not serve the king of Babylon, for it is a lie that they are prophesying to you. The false prophets in Zedekiah's court are telling him what political logic and national pride demand: resist, do not submit. Jeremiah identifies their message as sheqer (lie) — not merely a different political analysis but a false theological claim about YHWH's intentions.
27.15: I did not send them, declares YHWH, but they are prophesying falsely in my name, with the result that I will drive you out and you will perish, you and the prophets who are prophesying to you. The I did not send formula is the definitive criterion: unauthorized speech in YHWH's name is false prophecy regardless of its political popularity. The consequence — I will drive you out, you will perish — applies to both the king who follows the false counsel and the prophets who gave it. False prophecy is not a victimless crime; it destroys both the deceiver and the deceived.
27.16: Then I spoke to the priests and to all this people, saying, Thus says YHWH: Do not listen to the words of your prophets who are prophesying to you, saying, Behold, the vessels of YHWH's house will now shortly be brought back from Babylon, for it is a lie that they are prophesying to you. The Temple vessels had been taken to Babylon in 597 BC when Jehoiachin was deported (2 Kgs 24:13). The false prophets were promising a quick return of these sacred objects — a claim that would have been politically and religiously popular, suggesting that the exile was temporary and YHWH's honor would soon be restored. Jeremiah declares this claim a lie.
27.17: Do not listen to them; serve the king of Babylon and live. Why should this city become a desolation? The pastoral urgency of Jeremiah's message: why should this city become a desolation? He does not want Jerusalem destroyed — he weeps for it (9:1; 14:17). The call to submit to Babylon is not treachery but compassion: the prophet who prophesies surrender is the one trying to prevent the city's destruction, while the patriots who resist are ensuring it.
27.18: If they are prophets, and if the word of YHWH is with them, then let them intercede with YHWH of hosts, that the vessels that are left in the house of YHWH, in the house of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem may not go to Babylon. Jeremiah challenges the false prophets: if you genuinely have access to YHWH's word, use it to intercede for the remaining Temple vessels. This is a practical test of prophetic authenticity: the true prophet can intercede effectively (cf. Moses, Samuel); the false prophet's intercession is empty. The challenge presupposes that Jeremiah knows the intercession will fail — the remaining vessels are going to Babylon too.
27.19: For thus says YHWH of hosts concerning the pillars, the sea, the stands, and the rest of the vessels that are left in this city. The specific Temple furnishings are named: the bronze pillars Jachin and Boaz (1 Kgs 7:15–22), the molten sea (the great bronze basin, 1 Kgs 7:23–26), and the bronze stands (1 Kgs 7:27–39). These objects taken to Babylon in 597 were the largest and most significant Temple furnishings; the ones remaining are also important. Jeremiah's specificity underlines the certainty of what he is predicting.
27.20: Which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon did not take away, when he took into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon Jeconiah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, and all the nobles of Judah and Jerusalem. The historical specification: the 597 BC deportation took some vessels but left others. The false prophets pointed to the remaining vessels as evidence that YHWH was still protecting Jerusalem's sacred objects. Jeremiah declares that the remaining vessels are still in Jerusalem not because of YHWH's protection but because Babylon has not yet come for them.
27.21: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, concerning the vessels that are left in the house of YHWH, in the house of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem. The oracle concerning the remaining vessels is delivered with formal authority: the full divine title YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel. The solemnity signals the importance of what follows.
27.22: They shall be carried to Babylon and remain there until the day when I visit them, declares YHWH. Then I will bring them back and restore them to this place. The oracle is both devastating and hopeful: the remaining vessels will go to Babylon — confirming Jeremiah's message against the false prophets — but they will return when YHWH visits (paqad) them. The same verb used for divine judgment (visitation for punishment) here means visitation for restoration. The exile of the Temple vessels anticipates the exile of the people; the return of the vessels anticipates the return of the people. Historically, the Temple vessels were returned under Cyrus in 538 BC (Ezra 1:7–11), just as Jeremiah predicted.

28.1: Chapter 28 stages the most dramatic prophetic confrontation in the book: the clash between Jeremiah and Hananiah in the Temple. In that same year, at the beginning of the reign of Zedekiah king of Judah, in the fifth month of the fourth year, Hananiah the son of Azzur, the prophet from Gibeon, spoke to me in the house of YHWH. The date is approximately 594 BC — the same year as Jeremiah's yoke sign-act (chapter 27). Hananiah is from Gibeon (a Levitical city, Josh 21:17), has prophetic credentials, and speaks in the Temple — all marks of institutional legitimacy. He is not presented as a charlatan from the fringes but as an established prophetic figure.
28.2: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon. Hananiah uses the authoritative prophetic formula (thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel) — the same formula Jeremiah uses. He claims divine authorization and invokes the same divine title, making his message externally indistinguishable from Jeremiah's. The claim itself — I have broken the yoke of Babylon — directly contradicts Jeremiah's yoke-sign-act of chapter 27. Two prophets, same formula, opposite messages: this is the crisis of discernment that chapter 28 forces the reader to navigate.
28.3: Within two years I will bring back to this place all the vessels of YHWH's house, which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon took away from this place and carried to Babylon. The two-year timeline is specific, measurable, and falsifiable — it will either happen or it will not. Hananiah's prophecy is testable in a way that Jeremiah's 70-year oracle is not (no living person in 594 BC would survive to verify the 70-year prediction). The short timeline gives Hananiah's message immediate political appeal and makes Jeremiah's long-term oracle seem less urgent.
28.4: I will also bring back to this place Jeconiah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, and all the exiles from Judah who went to Babylon, declares YHWH, for I will break the yoke of the king of Babylon. Hananiah extends the promise to include Jehoiachin and all the first-deportation exiles (597 BC). This is politically maximalist: not only the Temple vessels but the deported king and entire first exile community will return within two years. The promise taps directly into the hope of the exiles and their families, making it powerfully attractive.
28.5: Then the prophet Jeremiah spoke to Hananiah the prophet in the presence of the priests and all the people who were standing in the house of YHWH. The setting is public and formal — the Temple court with priests and people as witnesses. Jeremiah's response is not private consultation but a public counter-oracle in the same space where Hananiah spoke. The confrontation cannot be dismissed as a private theological dispute; it is a public crisis of prophetic authority.
28.6: And the prophet Jeremiah said, Amen! May YHWH do so; may YHWH make the words that you have prophesied come true, and bring back to this place from Babylon the vessels of the house of YHWH, and all the exiles. Jeremiah's response is startling: he says amen — may it be so! He does not immediately denounce Hananiah as a liar but expresses genuine desire that the restoration prophecy be true. The prophet who has been announcing judgment genuinely wishes the optimistic oracle were accurate. This models the complex pastoral reality: prophets who announce doom are not gleeful about it (cf. 20:17–18; Lam 3:1–20).
28.7: Yet hear now this word that I speak in your hearing and in the hearing of all the people. The qualifier follows the amen: despite his wish that Hananiah were right, Jeremiah must speak what he has received. The yet (akh) signals a critical qualification. The structure — wish it were true, but here is the reality — is the model of honest prophetic speech that neither pretends to wish disaster nor suppresses the accurate message.
28.8: The prophets who preceded you and me from ancient times prophesied war, famine, and pestilence against many countries and great kingdoms. Jeremiah invokes the prophetic tradition as a criterion: the historical pattern of genuine prophecy was primarily judgment-announcement. The prophets before them — Amos, Hosea, Micah, Isaiah — predominantly announced covenant consequences (war, famine, pestilence) rather than unconditional restoration. This statistical argument is not a denial that restoration prophecy is ever genuine, but an observation that the burden of proof lies with those who prophesy peace.
28.9: As for the prophet who prophesies peace, when the word of that prophet comes to pass, then it will be known that YHWH has truly sent that prophet. The Deuteronomic criterion of prophetic truth (Deut 18:21–22): fulfillment proves authenticity. The irony is that Hananiah's two-year prophecy will be precisely testable — within two years, the exiles will not have returned, the Temple vessels will still be in Babylon, and Jehoiachin will still be in prison. The fulfillment criterion cuts against Hananiah's short-term, falsifiable prediction far more than against Jeremiah's long-term oracle.
28.10: Then the prophet Hananiah took the yoke bar from the neck of Jeremiah the prophet and broke it. Hananiah performs a counter-sign-act: he takes the wooden yoke from Jeremiah's neck and breaks it. In sign-act logic, the physical action enacts the message — breaking the yoke symbolizes breaking Babylon's dominion. This is a direct challenge to Jeremiah's sign-act authority. The crowd watching would have seen Hananiah's dramatic action as a compelling visual claim: the yoke of Babylon will be broken just as this wooden bar is broken.
28.11: And Hananiah spoke in the presence of all the people, saying, Thus says YHWH: Even so will I break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon from the neck of all the nations within two years. But Jeremiah the prophet went his way. Hananiah doubles down on the two-year promise, now with the dramatic visual of the broken yoke. Jeremiah went his way — a remarkable sentence. Jeremiah does not counter-argue, does not produce a dramatic response, does not attempt to out-perform Hananiah's sign-act. He simply leaves. The prophet's authority does not require winning a public performance contest.
28.12: Sometime after the prophet Hananiah had broken the yoke bar from off the neck of Jeremiah the prophet, the word of YHWH came to Jeremiah. The divine word comes to Jeremiah after the confrontation, not during it — another mark of authentic prophecy versus performance. Genuine prophets do not manufacture responses on demand; they wait for the divine word to come and then speak it.
28.13: Go, tell Hananiah, Thus says YHWH: You have broken wooden bars, but you have made in their place bars of iron. The divine response to Hananiah's dramatic sign-act: the broken wooden yoke is replaced with iron. The counter-sign is more severe than the original. Encouraging resistance to Babylon with false prophecy will not produce freedom but a harder, more inescapable form of subjugation. The attempt to break YHWH's appointed instrument of judgment makes the judgment worse rather than averting it.
28.14: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: I have put upon the neck of all these nations an iron yoke to serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and they shall serve him, for I have given to him even the beasts of the field. The iron yoke reiterates and intensifies 27:6: YHWH has commissioned Nebuchadnezzar's dominion over nations and beasts. The wooden yoke that Hananiah broke was symbolic; the iron yoke YHWH establishes is the historical reality of Babylonian military dominance that no prophetic sign-act can break.
28.15: And Jeremiah the prophet said to the prophet Hananiah, Listen, Hananiah, YHWH has not sent you, and you have made this people trust in a lie. The direct confrontation Jeremiah avoided in vv6–11 arrives now with divine backing. YHWH has not sent you — the definitive criterion of prophetic invalidity. The harm Hananiah has done is specified: you have made this people trust in a lie (batach al-sheqer). False hope is not neutral; it actively prevents the repentance and surrender that might save lives. Hananiah's false prophecy is not merely wrong — it is an act of spiritual harm.
28.16: Therefore thus says YHWH: Behold, I will remove you from the face of the earth. This year you shall die, because you have uttered rebellion against YHWH. The death-oracle against Hananiah is specific and measurable: this year (hashanah hazot), not within two years but within one year. The charge is rebellion against YHWH (sarah dibbarta el-YHWH) — false prophecy is not mere error but an act of covenant treason. Speaking rebellion in YHWH's name is the crime of Deuteronomy 18:20.
28.17: In that same year, in the seventh month, the prophet Hananiah died. The brief, clinical fulfillment: Hananiah's death within the year proves Jeremiah's prophecy accurate and Hananiah's false. The narrative provides no theological commentary — it simply records the outcome. The reader draws the conclusion: the falsifiable prediction of the true prophet was fulfilled; the falsifiable prediction of the false prophet (two-year restoration) was not. Prophetic authentication by fulfillment operates precisely as Deuteronomy 18:21–22 described.

29.1: Chapter 29 contains Jeremiah's letter to the Babylonian exiles — one of the most pastorally significant documents in the Hebrew Bible. These are the words of the letter that Jeremiah the prophet sent from Jerusalem to the surviving elders of the exiles, and to the priests, the prophets, and all the people, whom Nebuchadnezzar had taken into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon. The letter was sent to those deported in 597 BC — the first exile that included Jehoiachin, Ezekiel, and the skilled artisans. The recipients include the community leadership (elders, priests, prophets) and the general population.
29.2: This was after King Jeconiah and the queen mother, the eunuchs, the officials of Judah and Jerusalem, the craftsmen, and the metal workers had departed from Jerusalem. The list of the 597 deportation matches 2 Kings 24:14–16: king, court, craftsmen, metal workers. The inclusion of craftsmen and metal workers is historically significant — these were the people who could manufacture weapons and military equipment. Babylon's removal of them was strategic: it left Jerusalem militarily weakened. Their absence from Jerusalem is part of the political-military context Jeremiah is addressing.
29.3: The letter was sent by the hand of Elasah the son of Shaphan and Gemariah the son of Hilkiah, whom Zedekiah king of Judah sent to Babylon to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. The letter traveled with official royal messengers — Zedekiah was sending a delegation to Nebuchadnezzar, and Jeremiah sent his letter through their hands. Elasah ben Shaphan is from the Shaphan family that has been consistently supportive of Jeremiah (cf. Ahikam ben Shaphan, 26:24). Gemariah ben Hilkiah — possibly the son of the high priest who found the law-scroll in Josiah's reign (2 Kgs 22:8). These are trustworthy carriers from reform-sympathetic families.
29.4: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, to all the exiles whom I have sent into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon. The divine authorship is asserted immediately: whom I have sent into exile (asher hig'alti). YHWH sent the exiles, not merely permitted the Babylonians to deport them. The theological framing of the exile as YHWH's purposive act — not just a military defeat — is the foundation of everything that follows. If YHWH sent them, he is with them in Babylon; he can be sought there; he can restore them from there.
29.5: Build houses and live in them; plant gardens and eat their produce. The counterintuitive pastoral counsel begins: put down roots. The natural response to involuntary exile — maintaining refugee mentality, refusing to invest in the host land, preserving resources for the expected quick return — is exactly what Hananiah's false prophecy was encouraging. Jeremiah's counsel is the opposite: build, plant, eat. Long-term investment in the place of exile is YHWH's instruction, not compromise or disloyalty.
29.6: Take wives and have sons and daughters; take wives for your sons, and give your daughters in marriage, that they may bear sons and daughters; multiply there, and do not decrease. The creation mandate (be fruitful and multiply, Gen 1:28) is applied to the Babylonian exile community: perpetuate the covenant people in the diaspora. The instruction to arrange marriages for children implies a multi-generational commitment: this is not a two-year waiting period (contra Hananiah) but an extended sojourn that will require the next generation to be established. Do not decrease: maintaining population is a form of covenant faithfulness.
29.7: But seek the welfare of the city where I have sent you into exile, and pray to YHWH on its behalf, for in its welfare you will find your welfare. The most theologically provocative verse in the letter: seek the shalom of the city (dirshu et-shalom ha'ir). Shalom encompasses peace, welfare, prosperity, wholeness — the comprehensive flourishing of Babylonian society. The covenant community sent into exile is instructed not merely to tolerate Babylon but to actively seek its welfare through prayer and civic engagement. The theological grounding: the exiles' welfare is bound up with Babylon's welfare. This is the foundation for the diaspora theology that shaped post-exilic Judaism and, through it, the NT understanding of the church as sojourners who pray for and serve the civil communities where God has placed them (1 Tim 2:1–2; 1 Pet 2:11–17).
29.8: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Do not let your prophets and your diviners who are among you deceive you, and do not listen to the dreams that they dream. The exile community has its own false prophets (cf. Ahab and Zedekiah, vv21–23; Shemaiah, vv24–32) who are encouraging the same false hope Hananiah promoted in Jerusalem: quick return, no need to settle in. The warning against exilic false prophecy is the same as the warning to Jerusalem: prophets who promise what you want to hear rather than what YHWH has actually said are deceiving you.
29.9: For it is a lie that they are prophesying to you in my name; I did not send them, declares YHWH. The I did not send criterion applies as much in Babylon as in Jerusalem. YHWH's authentication standards are not geographically variable — the same principle that distinguishes true from false prophecy in the Temple court applies equally in the exile community. Distance from the land does not suspend covenant obligations or prophetic accountability.
29.10: For thus says YHWH: When seventy years are completed for Babylon, I will visit you, and I will fulfill to you my promise and bring you back to this place. The 70-year oracle from 25:11–12 is now addressed directly to the exiles. I will visit you (afqod etkhem) — the divine visitation-for-good that reverses the visitation-for-judgment (cf. 1:10 — I pluck up and destroy; 31:28 — I build and plant). Fulfill my promise (aqqim et-devari hatov): the good word YHWH has spoken about restoration will be executed, not merely announced. The promise is a commitment, not merely an aspiration.
29.11: For I know the plans I have for you, declares YHWH, plans for welfare and not for evil, to give you a future and a hope. Perhaps the most well-known verse in Jeremiah. I know the plans I have for you (ki anochi yada'ti et-hamachashavot asher anochi choshev aleikhem): the divine intentionality toward the covenant community is personal, deliberate, and forward-looking. Welfare and not evil (shalom velo lera'ah): YHWH's ultimate purpose for his people is shalom — the comprehensive flourishing of the covenant community. Future and hope (acharit vetiqvah): the exile that appears to be an ending is actually a pivot toward a future that YHWH has designed. The verse is set in the specific historical context of Babylonian exile, but its principle — divine intentionality toward covenant flourishing — has been appropriated across all contexts of disorientation and suffering.
29.12: Then you will call upon me and come and pray to me, and I will hear you. The restoration of prayer access — which had been withdrawn from Jerusalem (7:16; 11:14; 14:11) — is promised for the exile community. When the 70 years are completed and YHWH visits them, prayer will once again function as a genuine channel of covenant communication. The hearing of prayer (shama'ti) is the restoration of the broken covenant relationship.
29.13: You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart. The seeking-and-finding promise is one of the most important restoration statements in the prophets. With all your heart (bechol-levavchem): the undivided heart of Deuteronomy 6:5 (love YHWH with all your heart) is the condition for finding YHWH in exile. The exile has stripped away the Temple, the land, the political structures — what remains is the heart-orientation toward YHWH that was always the core of the covenant. The exile's function in Jeremiah's theology is precisely to reduce Israel to this core.
29.14: I will be found by you, declares YHWH, and I will restore your fortunes and gather you from all the nations and all the places where I have driven you, declares YHWH, and I will bring you back to the place from which I sent you into exile. The restoration is comprehensive and personal: YHWH will be found — the seeker will discover that YHWH was present all along. The gathering from all nations and all places anticipates a diaspora broader than the Babylonian exile — a worldwide scattering from which YHWH will gather. The NT reads this as the gathering of the church from all nations (Matt 24:31; John 11:52; Eph 2:11–22).
29.15: Because you have said, YHWH has raised up prophets for us in Babylon. The exiles are appealing to their own prophetic tradition in Babylon to counter Jeremiah's letter. The false prophets among the exiles were claiming YHWH's authorization — YHWH has raised up prophets for us — in support of their restoration-within-two-years message. Jeremiah must address this specific claim: having prophets is not itself proof of divine authorization.
29.16: Thus says YHWH concerning the king who sits on the throne of David, and concerning all the people who dwell in this city, your kinsmen who did not go out with you into exile. Jeremiah addresses the relative situations of the exiles and the Jerusalem remnant. The exiles might be tempted to think that those left in Jerusalem are better off — still in the covenant land, still in the city, still near the Temple. Jeremiah's oracle reverses this assessment.
29.17: Thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I am sending on them sword, famine, and pestilence, and I will make them like vile figs that are so bad they cannot be eaten. The bad-fig oracle of chapter 24 is restated: those who remained in Jerusalem (and will remain through the coming decade until Jerusalem's fall in 587) face the judgment triad. The vile figs that cannot be eaten image the complete worthlessness of those who evaded YHWH's appointed exile. The exiles are the good figs of chapter 24; those in Jerusalem are the bad figs.
29.18: I will pursue them with sword, famine, and pestilence, and will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth, to be a curse, a terror, a hissing, and a reproach among all the nations where I shall drive them. The shame-catalogue for the bad-fig population (cf. 24:9): horror, curse, terror, hissing, reproach. Every form of public disgrace in the ancient honor-shame framework will fall on those who remain in Jerusalem and resist YHWH's judgment. The pursuit of the judgment triad is relentless — there is no escaping into Egypt or any other refuge.
29.19: Because they did not listen to my words, declares YHWH, that I persistently sent to them by my servants the prophets, but you would not listen, declares YHWH. The covenant audit: persistent sending, persistent non-listening. The prophetic tradition has been consistent and urgent; the people's response has been consistent and deaf. The formula summarizes the entire history of covenant communication that chapter 25 catalogued — 23 years of Jeremiah's ministry plus the full tradition of prophets before him.
29.20: Hear the word of YHWH, all you exiles whom I sent away from Jerusalem to Babylon. Jeremiah refocuses on the exiles as his primary audience after the digression addressing the Jerusalem situation. The exiles need to receive this word to navigate their situation wisely — understanding that their present condition is YHWH's purposive act, that they have a genuine future, and that the false prophets among them are dangerous.
29.21: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, concerning Ahab the son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah the son of Maaseiah, who are prophesying a lie to you in my name: Behold, I will deliver them into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and he shall strike them down before your eyes. Two specific false prophets in Babylon are named and condemned: Ahab ben Kolaiah and Zedekiah ben Maaseiah. Their fate is judicial execution by Nebuchadnezzar — not martyrdom for their faith but punishment as political troublemakers whose false prophecy destabilized the exile community.
29.22: Because of them this curse shall be used by all the exiles from Judah in Babylon: YHWH make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire. The executions were by fire — a Babylonian method of capital punishment documented in the ancient sources. The names become a curse formula: YHWH make you like Zedekiah and Ahab — their fate so terrible that it becomes the template for invocation of maximum punishment. Daniel 3 provides the background: Babylonian execution by fire was a known institution. These two false prophets died the death they implicitly invited on others by their false prophecy.
29.23: Because they have done an outrageous thing in Israel: they have committed adultery with their neighbors' wives, and they have spoken in my name lying words that I did not command them. I am the one who knows, and I am witness, declares YHWH. The double indictment: sexual immorality (literal adultery) and prophetic fraud (lying in YHWH's name). The combination echoes chapter 23's indictment of Jerusalem's prophets who commit adultery and walk in lies (23:14). The false prophets of Babylon have replicated the corruption of Jerusalem's prophetic establishment in the exile community. I am witness (ani eid): YHWH is the self-declaring divine witness — sufficient testimony without human corroboration.
29.24: To Shemaiah of Nehelam you shall say. A third false prophet in Babylon is addressed — Shemaiah of Nehelam (the location is unknown but possibly a family or place designation). Shemaiah had written letters to Jerusalem attempting to have Jeremiah arrested, and the narrative of his case and Jeremiah's counter-oracle runs through vv25–32.
29.25: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: You have sent letters in your name to all the people who are in Jerusalem, and to Zephaniah the son of Maaseiah the priest, and to all the priests, saying. Shemaiah's letters were sent to the Jerusalem religious authorities, specifically to Zephaniah the priest (who appears in 21:1 and 37:3 as part of the delegations sent to Jeremiah). The letter attempted to invoke the authority of the Temple establishment to suppress Jeremiah's letter.
29.26: YHWH has made you priest instead of Jehoiada the priest, to have charge in the house of YHWH over every madman who prophesies, to put him in the stocks and neck irons. Shemaiah's letter argued that the priest had jurisdiction over those who claimed to be prophets but behaved like madmen. The word for madman (meshugga) — the same root used by Elisha's servant (2 Kgs 9:11: this madman came to you) — was applied to those who behaved with prophetic ecstasy. Shemaiah is arguing that Jeremiah's behavior falls within this category and that the Temple authorities should restrain him.
29.27: Now why have you not rebuked Jeremiah of Anathoth who is prophesying to you? Shemaiah's rhetorical question to Zephaniah the priest: why are you allowing Jeremiah to continue? The fact that this question could be sent suggests that Shemaiah was aware of Jeremiah's letter to the exiles and found it dangerously subversive — counseling submission to Babylon and long-term investment in exile was politically destabilizing to the exile community's hope for quick return.
29.28: For he has sent to us in Babylon, saying, Your exile will be long; build houses and live in them, and plant gardens and eat their produce. Shemaiah quotes Jeremiah's counsel as evidence of its dangerous quality: your exile will be long. This is exactly what Jeremiah said — but Shemaiah presents it as proof of Jeremiah's false and harmful prophecy. The contested interpretation of the exile's duration is the central dispute: Hananiah said two years; Shemaiah agrees with Hananiah; Jeremiah says 70 years.
29.29: Zephaniah the priest read this letter in the hearing of Jeremiah the prophet. Rather than acting on Shemaiah's demand to arrest Jeremiah, Zephaniah read him the letter — the same kind of support Ahikam had provided at the Temple-sermon trial (26:24). The reform-sympathetic strand within the priesthood (traceable back to the Shaphan family) continues to protect Jeremiah from institutional suppression. Zephaniah will later consult Jeremiah on behalf of Zedekiah (37:3; 52:24).
29.30: Then the word of YHWH came to Jeremiah: the divine response to Shemaiah's letter. The pattern is consistent: Jeremiah receives divine authorization for his counter-oracles through the normal prophetic channel. He does not respond impulsively to the letter but waits for the divine word.
29.31: Send to all the exiles, saying, Thus says YHWH concerning Shemaiah of Nehelam: Because Shemaiah prophesied to you when I did not send him, and made you trust in a lie. The I did not send verdict is issued against Shemaiah — the same verdict against Hananiah (28:15) and the false prophets generally (23:21). The harm specified: made you trust in a lie — exactly the charge against Hananiah (28:15). The false prophets' sin is consistently the same: using YHWH's name to create false security that prevents the repentance and reorientation that would actually bring life.
29.32: Therefore thus says YHWH: Behold, I will punish Shemaiah of Nehelam and his offspring. He shall not have anyone living among this people, and he shall not see the good that I will do to my people, declares YHWH, for he has spoken rebellion against YHWH. Shemaiah's punishment is exclusion from the restoration: he will have no one living among the covenant community and will not see the good that YHWH does for his people in the end. This is the harshest possible prophetic punishment — not physical death but covenant exclusion. The false prophet who promised quick return will not participate in the actual return. His offspring — the extension of his legacy — will share the exclusion.

30.1: Chapter 30 opens the Book of Consolation (chapters 30–33) — the concentrated section of restoration oracles that stands as the theological counterweight to all the judgment prophecies preceding them. The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Write in a book all the words that I have spoken to you. The command to write down the restoration oracles is unusual in the prophetic literature — most prophetic oracles were delivered orally. The writing command implies that these words are especially important for preservation and later consultation.
30.2: For behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will restore the fortunes of my people, Israel and Judah, declares YHWH, and I will bring them back to the land that I gave to their fathers, and they shall take possession of it. The restoration promise encompasses both kingdoms — Israel and Judah. The division of the kingdom (931 BC) and the Assyrian destruction of the north (722 BC) left the 10 northern tribes in perpetual exile. Jeremiah's restoration vision is not merely of Judah from Babylon but of the full reunification of the covenant people. The New Testament reads this as fulfilled in Christ's gathering of the scattered children of God from all nations (Eph 2:11–22).
30.3: The restoration promise is grounded in the original patriarchal land-grant: the land that I gave to their fathers. The gift is prior to the exile, prior to the covenant violation, prior to the judgment — YHWH's original intent for the covenant people was land-possession and flourishing. The exile is an interruption, not a cancellation, of the original divine purpose.
30.4: These are the words that YHWH spoke concerning Israel and Judah. The double-address (Israel and Judah) confirms that the restoration vision is comprehensive. The oracles in chapters 30–31 alternate between specific addresses to Ephraim (the north) and Judah (the south), reflecting the comprehensive scope of YHWH's restoration intention.
30.5: Thus says YHWH: We have heard a cry of panic, of terror, and no peace. The opening of the restoration vision paradoxically begins with judgment imagery: a cry of panic, terror, and no peace. The no peace (ein shalom) inverts the false-prophet message of peace — before there is genuine shalom, there must be the honest acknowledgment of the present shalom-lessness. The Book of Consolation begins with a realistic description of the crisis before offering the redemption.
30.6: Ask now, and see, can a man bear a child? Why then do I see every man with his hands on his stomach like a woman in labor? Why has every face turned pale? The labor-pain image here applied to men is a grotesque picture of panic — men clutching their stomachs in the posture of a woman in labor, their faces pale with terror. In a culture where male composure in battle was a mark of honor, this image of men reduced to labor-pains posture captures the complete psychological collapse that accompanies the Babylonian invasion and siege.
30.7: Alas! That day is so great there is none like it; it is a time of distress for Jacob; yet he shall be saved out of it. Jacob's trouble (tzarat ya'aqov) — one of the most debated phrases in prophetic eschatology. The day of Jacob's trouble is incomparably great — no historical event like it. Yet the sentence contains the hinge word yet (uminenah): out of this incomparable distress, Jacob shall be saved. The darkness is real and comprehensive; the salvation is equally real and comprehensive. This verse becomes a touchstone for later Jewish and Christian eschatology regarding a period of unprecedented tribulation preceding final redemption (cf. Dan 12:1; Matt 24:21).
30.8: And it shall come to pass in that day, declares YHWH of hosts, that I will break his yoke from off your neck, and I will burst your bonds, and foreigners shall no more make a servant of him. The yoke-breaking imagery from chapter 27–28 returns — but now YHWH himself breaks the yoke, and it stays broken. The wooden yoke Hananiah broke was replaced by iron (28:13); the iron yoke YHWH will break will not be replaced. The freedom from foreign servitude is permanent. The foreigners shall no more make a servant is the definitive end of exile and subjugation.
30.9: But they shall serve YHWH their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them. The restored community will serve YHWH (not foreign powers) and David their king (et-YHWH Eloheihem ve'et David malcham). The reference to David after David's death (c. 971 BC) cannot be the historical David but the Davidic Messiah — the coming king in David's line who will reign over the reunited people (cf. Hos 3:5; Ezek 34:23–24; 37:24–25). The NT identifies this David-king as Jesus, the son of David (Matt 1:1; Luke 1:32–33; Rev 22:16).
30.10: Then fear not, O Jacob my servant, declares YHWH, nor be dismayed, O Israel; for behold, I will save you from far away, and your offspring from the land of their captivity. Jacob shall return and have quiet and ease, and none shall make him afraid. The covenant reassurance formula — fear not (al-tira) — is one of the most common divine addresses in the prophets (cf. Isa 41:10, 43:1; Jer 46:27–28). Quiet and ease (shaqat vasha'anan): the shalom of settled, untroubled life — the opposite of the panic and labor-pains of vv5–7. The transformation from Jacob's trouble to Jacob's quiet-ease is YHWH's act of redemption.
30.11: For I am with you to save you, declares YHWH; I will make a full end of all the nations among whom I scattered you, but of you I will not make a full end. I will discipline you in just measure, and I will by no means leave you unpunished. The covenant distinction: YHWH will make a full end (kalah) of the nations that served as his instruments of judgment, but not of Israel. The discipline (yisartika) is just and measured — not the full covenant destruction. The covenant relationship is the reason Israel survives while the nations are consumed: Israel belongs to YHWH in a way the instrument-nations do not.
30.12: For thus says YHWH: Your hurt is incurable, and your wound is grievous. The restoration vision pauses to acknowledge the full severity of the present situation before the healing oracle. Incurable hurt (shever enoush) and grievous wound (machalaych) — terms of terminal illness applied to the national situation. The healing that follows will be all the more remarkable because it is healing of what is, from a human standpoint, beyond healing.
30.13: There is none to uphold your cause, no medicine for your wound, no healing for you. The political situation: no ally, no mediator, no therapy that can address the problem. The abandonment of the divine-medicine-for-your-wound framing anticipates the healing oracle that follows — YHWH himself will do what no human physician or political ally can do.
30.14: All your lovers have forgotten you; they do not seek you; for I have dealt you the blow of an enemy, the punishment of a merciless foe, because your guilt is great, because your sins are flagrant. The lovers (political allies, cf. 22:20) have abandoned the wounded Israel. The wound Israel suffers is not from a hostile foreign enemy in the ordinary sense but from YHWH himself — the divine lover has struck the unfaithful partner. The blow of an enemy from YHWH's own hand is the shock that drives the healing oracle: the one who wounded can also heal.
30.15: Why do you cry out over your hurt? Your pain is incurable. Because your guilt is great, because your sins are flagrant, I have done these things to you. The rhetorical question challenges the self-pity that focuses only on the suffering without acknowledging its covenantal source. The wound is self-inflicted in the covenant framework: Israel's great guilt and flagrant sins are the cause of YHWH's blow. Acknowledging this is necessary before healing can occur.
30.16: Therefore all who devour you shall be devoured, and all your foes, every one of them, shall go into captivity; those who plunder you shall be plundered, and all who prey on you I will make a prey. The reversal oracle begins: the devourer will be devoured, the plunderer plundered. This is the lex talionis applied to nations: the judgment they meted out on Israel will be applied to them. The nations that served as YHWH's instruments will not escape accountability for their cruelty (cf. Isa 10:5–19 — YHWH uses Assyria, then judges Assyria for pride and excess; Hab 2:6–20).
30.17: For I will restore health to you, and your wounds I will heal, declares YHWH, because they have called you an outcast: It is Zion, for whom no one cares! The healing oracle arrives: I will restore health to you (arukah e'aleh lach). The incurable wound (v12) that no physician or ally could treat, YHWH will heal. The motivation for the healing is partly YHWH's response to the nations' contemptuous dismissal: they called Israel an outcast (niddach) — cast out, rejected, valueless. YHWH's restoration of Israel is a vindication of his own covenant commitment against those who wrote Israel off as permanently abandoned.
30.18: Thus says YHWH: Behold, I will restore the fortunes of the tents of Jacob and have compassion on his dwellings; the city shall be rebuilt on its mound, and the palace shall stand where it used to be. The physical restoration: the tent-settlements (the broader dwelling-places of the people) and the city of Jerusalem will be rebuilt. On its mound (al-tilibah): the city will rise from its ruins on the same site, not relocated. This is a claim about physical continuity — the same city, the same location, rebuilt by the same God who allowed its destruction.
30.19: Out of them shall come songs of thanksgiving, and the voices of those who celebrate. I will multiply them, and they shall not be few; I will make them honored, and they shall not be small. The restoration community will be characterized by thanksgiving and celebration — the sounds of joy that the judgment section silenced (7:34; 16:9; 25:10). Multiply, not few; honored, not small: the reversal of exile's demographic and social diminishment. The restored community will grow and gain honor, the opposite of the shameful scattering.
30.20: Their children shall be as they were of old, and their congregation shall be established before me, and I will punish all who oppress them. The children of the restored community will be like the children of the covenant's best days — the ideal of each generation reflecting the covenant faithfulness of their ancestors. The congregation established before me (edatam lefanai takun): the covenant assembly reconstituted and presented before YHWH. The protection of the restored community is guaranteed: YHWH will punish their oppressors.
30.21: Their prince shall be one of themselves; their ruler shall come out from their midst; I will make him draw near, and he shall approach me, for who would dare of himself to approach me? declares YHWH. The restored community's leader will be a native son — not a foreign overlord but one from within the covenant people. The leader's access to YHWH (draw near, approach me) is the language of priestly mediation — this figure combines royal and priestly functions, approaching YHWH in the way the high priest approached the holy of holies. The question who would dare of himself to approach me? signals the uniqueness and sacredness of this access: only YHWH can authorize such direct access. The NT reads this through the lens of Christ as the one mediator who has opened access to the Father (Heb 10:19–22; 1 Tim 2:5).
30.22: And you shall be my people, and I will be your God. The covenant formula in its simplest and most ancient form (cf. Gen 17:7–8; Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12; Jer 24:7; 31:33; Ezek 36:28; Rev 21:3). The entire complex of judgment, exile, and restoration in chapters 1–30 is ultimately in service of this simple covenant statement. The purpose of everything YHWH has done — the plucking up and tearing down, the building and planting — is the restoration of this fundamental covenant relationship.
30.23: Behold the storm of YHWH! Wrath has gone forth, a whirling tempest; it will burst upon the head of the wicked. A brief return to judgment imagery (cf. 23:19 — the same verse nearly verbatim) within the restoration section. The storm of YHWH's wrath still falls on the wicked — the restoration of Israel does not mean the abolition of divine justice. The covenant community's restoration is not at the expense of righteousness but through and after the full execution of divine justice.
30.24: The fierce anger of YHWH will not turn back until he has executed and accomplished the intents of his heart. In the latter days you will understand it. The closing verse of chapter 30 (nearly identical to 23:20) anticipates the latter days (be'acharit hayamim) as the time when full understanding of YHWH's purposes will come. The restoration oracles of chapters 30–33 are not fully transparent in their original historical setting — their full meaning will be understood retrospectively, from the vantage point of their fulfillment. This eschatological reservation applies both to Israel's post-exilic restoration and, in Christian reading, to the fuller fulfillment in Christ and the new covenant.

32.1: Chapter 32 is one of the most dramatically ironic passages in the Hebrew Bible: Jeremiah purchases a field while the Babylonian army is besieging Jerusalem, acting as a sign-act of restoration hope precisely when all visible evidence points to national extinction. The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH in the tenth year of Zedekiah king of Judah, which was the eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar. The date is 588/587 BC — the final siege of Jerusalem. Nebuchadnezzar began the siege in Zedekiah's ninth year (2 Kgs 25:1) and took the city in his tenth year (Jer 39:1–2). Jeremiah is purchasing real estate during the last months before Jerusalem's fall.
32.2: At that time the army of the king of Babylon was besieging Jerusalem, and Jeremiah the prophet was shut up in the court of the guard that was in the palace of the king of Judah. The setting is doubly confined: the city is besieged (enclosed by the Babylonian army) and Jeremiah is imprisoned (enclosed in the palace guard-court). The prophet who counseled surrender is under house arrest, the city he prophesied would fall is being besieged. His prophetic word is being fulfilled in real time around him.
32.3: For Zedekiah king of Judah had imprisoned him, saying, Why do you prophesy and say, Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am giving this city into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall capture it. Jeremiah's imprisonment is a direct consequence of his prophetic message. Zedekiah has imprisoned him for the same reason Jehoiakim wanted to kill Uriah (26:20–23): the prophet announced surrender as YHWH's will, which amounted to treason in a time of active resistance. The charge is accurate — Jeremiah did say exactly this.
32.4: Zedekiah king of Judah shall not escape out of the hand of the Chaldeans, but shall surely be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and shall speak with him face to face and see him eye to eye. The specific oracle about Zedekiah's personal fate: he will be captured and brought face-to-face with Nebuchadnezzar. This was historically fulfilled — Zedekiah was captured near Jericho, brought to Nebuchadnezzar's headquarters at Riblah, watched his sons executed, was blinded, and taken to Babylon (2 Kgs 25:4–7; Jer 39:4–7). Face-to-face with the king, as Jeremiah predicted.
32.5: And he shall take Zedekiah to Babylon, and there he shall remain until I visit him, declares YHWH. Though you fight against the Chaldeans, you shall not succeed. The covenant visit (paqad) of YHWH upon Zedekiah in Babylon — a visit for mercy or judgment, the text is deliberately ambiguous. Though you fight against the Chaldeans, you shall not succeed: resistance is futile. The declaration of futility applies to all military options — no strategy, no alliance, no desperate last-stand will change what YHWH has determined.
32.6: Jeremiah said, The word of YHWH came to me: Hanamel the son of Shallum your uncle is going to come to you and say, Buy my field that is at Anathoth, for the right of redemption by purchase is yours. The divine foreknowledge confirmed: YHWH tells Jeremiah that his cousin Hanamel will come to him with a field-redemption offer. When Hanamel actually arrives (v8), this prediction is Jeremiah's confirmation that the purchase is YHWH's instruction, not mere family obligation. The anticipation of the visit functions as a prophetic sign: the one who can predict family visits can be trusted with promises about the nation's future.
32.7: Then Hanamel my cousin came to me in the court of the guard, in accordance with the word of YHWH, and said to me, Buy my field that is at Anathoth in the land of Benjamin, for the right of possession and redemption is yours; buy it for yourself. Hanamel's arrival precisely as predicted confirms that the subsequent field purchase is a divinely commanded sign-act, not merely family business. The right of redemption (mishpat hage'ulah) is the kinsman-redeemer provision of Leviticus 25:25: when a clan member is forced to sell his land, the nearest kin has the right (and obligation) to buy it and keep it within the family. Jeremiah is the appropriate redeemer for Hanamel's distressed property.
32.8: Then I knew that this was the word of YHWH. The confirmatory function of the prediction: Hanamel's arrival validates the divine origin of the purchase command. The prophet who was uncertain whether to comply with an apparently bizarre instruction (buy land during a siege?) finds in the fulfilled prediction the confirmation he needed. This is an important insight into how prophetic guidance worked in practice: confirmation through fulfilled prior knowledge.
32.9: And I bought the field at Anathoth from Hanamel my cousin, and weighed out the money to him, seventeen shekels of silver. The actual transaction: seventeen shekels of silver for the Anathoth field. This is a real price for a real property transaction — archaeologists have found ancient Near Eastern field sale contracts with comparable amounts. The specific price details authenticate the historicity of the transaction. Jeremiah pays from whatever resources he has while imprisoned, investing in property in a city about to fall to an enemy who will level it.
32.10: I signed the deed, sealed it, got witnesses, and weighed the money on scales. The legal procedure is scrupulous: signature, sealing, witnesses, and weighed silver. This is authentic ancient Near Eastern real estate protocol. The meticulous legal formality in the midst of siege and imprisonment makes the transaction even more remarkable — Jeremiah is performing the full legal requirements for a valid property transfer as if future enforcement of the deed were fully expected.
32.11: Then I took the sealed deed of purchase, containing the terms and conditions and the open copy. Ancient Near Eastern property deeds were typically written in two copies on a single clay tablet: the sealed copy (protected against tampering) and an open copy (accessible for reference). The sealed copy would be stored in a clay jar; the open copy could be consulted when needed. This dual-copy system provided both security and accessibility — Jeremiah is following established legal practice.
32.12: And I gave the deed of purchase to Baruch the son of Neriah son of Mahseiah, in the presence of Hanamel my cousin, in the presence of the witnesses who signed the deed of purchase, and in the presence of all the Judeans who were sitting in the court of the guard. Baruch ben Neriah is Jeremiah's scribe and primary disciple — the one who wrote down Jeremiah's oracles (36:4), read them in the Temple (36:6–10), and eventually compiled/edited the book of Jeremiah. His custody of the deed of purchase places the most important sign-act in Jeremiah's ministry in the hands of the man who will preserve Jeremiah's entire prophetic legacy.
32.13: And I charged Baruch in their presence, saying. Jeremiah delivers specific instructions to Baruch regarding the deed's preservation — the instructions that follow explain the purpose of the entire sign-act.
32.14: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Take these deeds, both this sealed deed of purchase and this open deed, and put them in an earthenware vessel, that they may last for a long time. Clay jars preserve documents extremely well — the Dead Sea Scrolls, preserved in ceramic jars for 2,000 years, demonstrate the effectiveness of this archival method. The instruction to preserve the deed for a long time is itself the theological point: the deed will need to be kept long enough for conditions to change, for the siege to end, for Babylon to fall, for exiles to return and claim their ancestral properties. The jar-preservation anticipates a long wait.
32.15: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land. The theological explanation of the entire sign-act, delivered in the most economically absurd possible moment.
