Pharaoh Necho II attacked Gaza/Kadytis around 609 BC (Herodotus 2.159 mentions this), shortly after defeating and killing King Josiah at Megiddo. The "before" chronology places the oracle in the period when Egyptian power was still dominant on the coastal plain — before Carchemish reversed the geopolitical situation. Gaza as an Egyptian target reflects the contested control of the Philistine coastal corridor.
47.2: The waters rising from the north — a swelling torrent that will overflow the land and the cities within it, with all inhabitants crying out — returns to Jeremiah's characteristic northern-threat imagery. The flood metaphor (cf. 46:7-8 where Egypt rose like the Nile) is now applied to the Babylonian advance overcoming the Philistine cities. The torrent is unstoppable: it covers the land and its inhabitants, cities and those dwelling in them. The geographical transition from Carchemish (Egypt) to the Philistine coast is geopolitically coherent: Babylon's conquest followed the coastal highway.
47.3: The sound of thundering hooves, the rattling of chariots and rumbling of wheels — the Babylonian war machine's advance is described as pure sound, a technique that conveys overwhelming force through auditory imagery. Fathers do not look back for their children — the noise of the oncoming army produces panic-flight so total that parental bonds are overridden. In ancient battle accounts, the sound of approaching chariots was a primary psychological weapon; the chariot was the tank of its day, combining speed, elevation, and shock value.
47.4: The purpose — to destroy all the Philistines, to cut off all survivors who might help Tyre and Sidon — places the oracle in the geopolitical context of Babylon's systematic reduction of the Levantine city-states. Tyre and Sidon (Phoenician cities north of Israel) and Gaza, Ashkelon, and Ashdod (Philistine cities south) formed a coastal arc of city-states between Babylon and Egypt. The Philistines are called remnant of the coastland of Caphtor — Caphtor is Crete or the Aegean coast, the Philistines' legendary place of origin (Deut 2:23; Amos 9:7), reflecting knowledge of the Bronze Age migration traditions.
47.5: Baldness has come upon Gaza; Ashkelon has been silenced — mourning rites (shaving the head was a standard ANE mourning practice, cf. Amos 8:10; Mic 1:16) are applied to the Philistine cities personified. The question — how long will you gash yourselves? — uses the mourning self-laceration practice condemned elsewhere in the Torah (Lev 19:28; Deut 14:1) but here presented as the natural Philistine response to their annihilation. The question implies that the mourning will continue indefinitely because the destruction is irreversible.
47.6: The rhetorical address to the sword of YHWH — how long until you rest? Return to your scabbard; cease and be still — is a dramatic personification unique in prophetic literature. The sword is addressed as an autonomous agent that can be implored to stop. The cry "how long" (ʿad matay) is the lament question of the psalms applied to the divine instrument of judgment. The oracle's speaker (perhaps a Philistine voice) pleads for the sword's cessation — but receives no answer.
47.7: The oracle's final word — how can it rest, when YHWH has given it a charge? — is the theological climax of the brief oracle. The sword cannot rest because YHWH has commanded it (YHWH tsivah lo). Against Ashkelon and the seacoast, against the appointed place (moʿad), the sword has a divine appointment. The word moʿad (appointed time/place) ties the judgment to divine scheduling: this is not random violence but YHWH's appointed act at YHWH's appointed time. The brevity of the oracle — seven verses — gives it an almost haiku-like concentration of judgment.

48.1: The oracle against Moab — the longest of the OAN in Jeremiah — introduces a nation with deep and complex ties to Israel. Moab occupied the plateau east of the Dead Sea between the Arnon gorge (north) and the Zered River (south). The Mesha Stele (also called the Moabite Stone, c. 840 BC, now in the Louvre) is the longest ancient inscription found in the Levant and provides extensive documentation of Moabite culture, religion, and politics — including Moab's wars with Israel under Omri and Ahab, and the worship of Chemosh, Moab's national deity.
48.2: The punning wordplay on Moabite city names begins here and runs through the chapter — a feature of the OAN genre called paronomasia (sound-based word play). Heshbon (ḥeshbon, meaning calculation/thought) — they have devised evil against it (ḥashvu). The silence of Madmen (Madmen) — they will be silenced (dammî ṭaddôm). Dimon and Nimrim will become desolate. These puns would have been immediately legible to Hebrew-speaking audiences and created a form of prophetic "name magic" — the city's name encodes its fate.
48.3: A voice of crying from Horonaim, desolation and great destruction! — Horonaim was a Moabite city mentioned in the Mesha Stele, establishing the oracle's geographical realism. The triple cascade — crying, desolation, destruction — mirrors the triple covenant curse (sword, famine, plague) but applied through its effects rather than its instruments. The escalating intensity of the cry-desolation-destruction sequence portrays the collapse moving through the Moabite plateau from city to city.
48.4: Moab is destroyed — her little ones make a cry heard. The image of children crying amid the destruction emphasizes the totality of the disaster: it affects the most vulnerable, not just warriors. The focus on children's voices (zeʿaqah shemaʿu tsaʿareyha) is the prophetic technique of representing communal catastrophe through its impact on the most innocent. Even the youngest and most helpless cannot escape judgment when it comes upon a nation.
48.5: The weeping on the road from Luhith to Horonaim — the descent road is full of crying — evokes the image of refugees streaming out of destroyed cities, weeping as they flee. The ascent of Luhith appears in Isaiah 15:5 in a parallel Moab oracle; the two prophetic traditions share geographical knowledge of the specific routes crossing the Moabite plateau. These are real roads known to the ancient world, not generic landscapes of destruction.
48.6: The counsel — flee, save yourselves! Be like a juniper in the desert (keʿaroʾer bamidbar) — is ambiguous: a juniper/tamarisk in the desert is either an image of loneliness (stripped of everything) or survival (alone but tenacious). The phrasing echoes 17:6, where the man who trusts in man is compared to a bush in the desert — a withered, isolated existence. Surviving the Moabite catastrophe means being reduced to bare life, stripped of all the social and cultic supports of civilization.
48.7: The indictment of Moab — because you trusted in your own works and your treasures, you also will be taken — identifies the theological root of Moab's fall: self-reliance rather than recognition of YHWH's sovereignty. Chemosh will go into exile — the national deity of Moab, whose worship is documented in the Mesha Stele ("I have built this high place for Chemosh...") and mentioned as a Moabite patron throughout the historical books (Num 21:29; 1 Kgs 11:7, 33), will be deported along with his people. The gods of the nations travel into exile with their nations — a standard ANE concept of divine power being carried in defeat.
48.8: The comprehensive judgment — the destroyer shall come upon every city, and no city shall escape — is the territorial claim of complete conquest. In ANE conquest accounts, total conquest was claimed as a demonstration of divine mandate; Jeremiah appropriates this totalizing language for YHWH's judgment against Moab. The valley and plateau alike — representing different geographical zones of Moab — are equally under judgment. No topographical feature provides shelter from YHWH's decreed destruction.
48.9: The image — give wings to Moab, for she will fly away — is either a command to provide the means of escape (give her wings to flee) or a lament that she must flee like a bird. The city of Kir-hareseth (meaning "City of the Potsherd" or "Wall of Kiln-Baked Brick," modern Kerak in Jordan) was Moab's capital fortress, mentioned in 2 Kings 3:25 and Isaiah 16:7. Its destruction signals the fall of Moab's political center. Archaeological excavations at Kerak have confirmed its ancient fortress character.
48.10: The extraordinary warning — cursed is he who does the work of YHWH with slackness, and cursed is he who keeps back his sword from bloodshed — addresses the Babylonian army as YHWH's instrument, warning against half-hearted execution of the divine commission. This verse is among the most disturbing in Jeremiah: YHWH's curse falls on those who fail to execute judgment completely. It is a theological claim about divine commission that does not soften or mitigate the destruction but insists on its thorough completion.
48.11: The famous wine-on-its-lees metaphor — Moab has been at ease from his youth and has settled on his dregs; he has not been emptied from vessel to vessel nor has he gone into exile, so his taste remains in him and his scent has not changed — describes Moab's undisturbed historical security. Wine left on its lees (sediment) preserved its flavor without being clarified by decanting. Moab, never having experienced the discipline of exile or conquest that Israel had endured, had preserved its cultural character unchanged — but also its spiritual complacency. The metaphor is a classic of ANE wine-making practice.
48.12: YHWH's judgment — therefore behold, the days are coming when I will send tilters who will tilt him; they will empty his vessels and shatter his jars — turns the wine metaphor into a destruction oracle. The "tilters" are those who pour wine from vessel to vessel to clarify it; here they are Babylon's armies who will decant Moab from his security into displacement. The shattering of the jars (we-nevaleihem yenapatsetsu) adds the violence of conquest: not just emptied but shattered beyond use.
48.13: Then Moab will be ashamed of Chemosh as the house of Israel was ashamed of Bethel, their confidence — the comparison to Bethel is striking. Bethel was the northern kingdom's royal sanctuary where Jeroboam I had set up the golden calves (1 Kgs 12:29-30), which the Deuteronomists treated as idolatrous. When the northern kingdom fell to Assyria in 722 BC, Bethel's cult was exposed as powerless. Moab's experience with Chemosh will parallel Israel's embarrassment over Bethel: the national deity will prove unable to protect his people.
48.14: The taunt — how can you say, we are heroes and mighty men of valor? — challenges Moab's military self-assessment. The phrase ʾanashim giborim we-ʾanshe ḥayil lamiḥama (men of might and valor for war) is the standard idiom for elite warriors. The rhetorical question exposes the gap between Moab's self-conception (a warrior nation) and the reality of imminent annihilation. Military self-confidence without YHWH's protection is empty boasting.
48.15: The divine declaration — Moab is destroyed and his cities have been taken; his finest young men have gone down to slaughter, declares the King, YHWH of hosts is his name — grounds the oracle's authority in YHWH's royal title. The appellation hamelekh YHWH tsevaoʾt shemo (the King, whose name is YHWH of hosts) is a rare formal title combining kingship and the military-cosmic epithet "of hosts" (meaning armies/angelic hosts). This title asserts YHWH's supremacy over all military power, human and divine.
48.16: The disaster of Moab is near to come, and his calamity hastens swiftly — the urgency formula signals the imminence of judgment. The word qarovah ʿal lavo (near to come) creates a sense of prophetic imminence that makes the oracle feel like advance warning rather than retrospective commentary. Throughout the OAN, the judgment is presented as future but imminent — close enough to prepare for but not yet arrived, giving the oracle its warning character.
48.17: The lamentation over Moab by his neighbors — mourn for him, all you who are around him; all who know his name, say, how the strong scepter is broken, the glorious staff! — invites the surrounding nations to mourn. The broken scepter (maqqel ʿoz) and broken staff (maqqel tifʾaret) are royal symbols of authority. Moab's political power, represented by the royal staff, will be shattered. The neighboring nations are mourners — a theatrical device that conveys the international significance of Moab's fall.
48.18: The address to daughter Dibon, come down from your glory and sit in thirst — descend and sit in thirst — uses the personification of the city-woman sitting in mourning posture. Dibon was the Moabite capital where the Mesha Stele was discovered in 1868; the stele itself mentions Dibon as Mesha's royal city. The descent from glory (kavod) to sitting in the dust was the classic posture of the defeated — city-lament literature from Mesopotamia (the Lament over the Destruction of Ur) uses identical imagery.
48.19: The cry from Aroer — the city on the edge of the Arnon gorge — to fleeing Moabites: what has happened? Aroer (modern Arair, in Jordan) sat at a key crossing of the Arnon gorge and appears in both Mosaic-period boundary texts (Num 32:34; Deut 2:36) and historical accounts. The question mah hayah (what has happened?) is the natural question of a survivor watching the destruction — unable to comprehend the scale of the catastrophe unfolding before them.
48.20: Moab is put to shame, for it is broken down; wail and cry! Tell it by the Arnon that Moab is destroyed — the proclamation of Moab's fall is to be announced at the Arnon, Moab's northern boundary river. The Arnon gorge (Wadi Mujib) is one of the most dramatic geographical features of Transjordan, cutting deeply into the plateau. In antiquity it marked the northern border of Moabite territory; proclaiming the disaster at this boundary is to announce it to all who cross the border.
48.21: The enumeration of destroyed Moabite cities — judgment has come upon the plateau, upon Holon, Jahaz, Mephaath, Dibon, Nebo, Beth-diblathaim, Kiriathaim, Beth-gamul, Beth-meon, Kerioth, Bozrah — reads like a geographical survey of the Moabite plateau. Many of these cities appear in the Mesha Stele, demonstrating the oracle's use of accurate geographical knowledge. The systematic listing of defeated cities is a literary form borrowed from ANE victory inscriptions, now deployed as a lament catalog.
48.22: The continuation of the city list establishes the comprehensiveness of the judgment. The repeated naming of specific sites rather than a general reference to "Moab" achieves the effect of total conquest — no city, however remote, has been spared. The literary technique mirrors Babylonian and Assyrian conquest inscriptions that listed every city taken; Jeremiah appropriates this genre to proclaim YHWH's judgment rather than a human king's victory.
48.23: The list continues through the Moabite plateau cities. The use of real place names — many confirmable from the Mesha Stele, Numbers, and archaeological survey — grounds the oracle in historical-geographical reality rather than rhetorical abstraction. The reader/hearer familiar with Transjordanian geography would recognize the systematic sweep of judgment across the entire plateau, from north to south, from the Arnon to the Zered.
48.24: The inclusion of Kerioth and Bozrah along with all the cities of the land of Moab, far and near — extending the judgment to Moab's southern cities — completes the geographical sweep. Kerioth may be the burial city associated with Moab (the name means "cities" or possibly a specific cultic city). The dual designation "far and near" (harehaqot we-haqqerovot) is a totality formula meaning without exception — every settlement, however remote or central, falls under judgment.
48.25: The horn of Moab is cut off and his arm is broken, declares YHWH — the horn (qeren) and arm (zeroa) are paired symbols of royal and military power in ANE iconography. The horn represents strength and authority (cf. the Bull iconography of Ugaritic and Canaanite royal imagery); the arm represents the military force to project that power. Cutting the horn and breaking the arm in a single declaration dismantles both the symbolic prestige and the practical military capacity of the Moabite state.
48.26: Make him drunk, because he magnified himself against YHWH — the cup of divine wrath that Jeremiah has described throughout his ministry (25:15-17) is now applied to Moab. Moab's sin is specified: he magnified himself against YHWH (higdil ʿal-YHWH). The specific pride is anti-YHWH arrogance. The drunkenness metaphor leads to wallowing in vomit — a deliberately undignified image for the mighty nation reduced to helpless disgrace. He also becomes a laughingstock (lisḥoq).
48.27: The indictment — was not Israel a laughingstock to you? — identifies the specific sin underlying Moab's judgment. Moab had mocked Israel; now Moab becomes the object of mockery. The principle of measure-for-measure (midah keneged midah) operates throughout the OAN: nations are judged by the same standard they applied to others. Moab's shaking of heads at the sight of captured Israelites will be replicated when others shake their heads at captured Moabites. The boomerang of mockery returns on those who deploy it.
48.28: The call to flee the cities and dwell in the rock; be like the dove that nests in the sides of the mouth of a gorge — invokes the dove's instinctive nesting in cliff crevices as an image of the desperate search for safety. The Moabite plateau's dramatic gorges (the Arnon, the Zered) would be the natural refuge for those fleeing city destruction. The dove image conveys the smallness and vulnerability of the survivors — reduced from a nation inhabiting fortified cities to individuals nesting in cliff faces.
48.29: The comprehensive indictment of Moab's pride — we have heard of the pride of Moab, he is very proud; his arrogance, pride, presumption, and haughty heart — lists four Hebrew words for pride: gaʾavah, geʾeh, gaʾon, gobah lev. This fourfold vocabulary of arrogance functions as a theological diagnosis: Moab's fundamental failure is the refusal to acknowledge YHWH's sovereignty. The same indictment appears in Isaiah 16:6, suggesting a shared prophetic tradition about Moab's character.
48.30: I know his insolence, declares YHWH, but it is futile; his boasts are futile — YHWH's direct claim to know Moab's arrogance is a sovereignty claim. Nothing is hidden from YHWH's knowledge; Moab's self-aggrandizement has been observed and assessed. The word badav (idle words/boasts) may refer to Moab's empty oracles or propaganda. The futility of Moab's pride is its foundation: pride built on autonomous human strength, without YHWH's support, cannot sustain itself.
48.31: The lamentation — therefore I wail for Moab; I cry out for all Moab — introduces the remarkable feature of divine mourning over the judgment. YHWH (or the prophet speaking for YHWH) weeps over the people being judged. This divine pathos — YHWH lamenting the destruction he himself has decreed — is theologically distinctive of Jeremiah (cf. 8:18-9:3, where the divine voice weeps over Israel). The men of Kir-heres are mourned; the divine lamentation is genuine, not performative.
48.32: The weeping over the vine of Sibmah — mourned more than the weeping of Jazer — invokes Moab's agricultural prosperity. Sibmah was famous for its vineyards (cf. Isa 16:8-9, parallel passage). The vine's tendrils crossing the sea and reaching to Jazer describes the vast spread of Moabite viticulture. On Moab's summer fruit and vintage, the destroyer has fallen — the joyful harvest season is replaced by the silence of destroyed vineyards. The absence of harvest celebration marks the end of normal life.
48.33: The removal of joy and gladness from the fruitful land and the land of Moab — specifically the end of harvest celebration with the treading of the winepress — removes the central communal joy of the agricultural year. The treaders tread no wine in the presses; I have put an end to the shouting — harvest songs and the festive noise of wine-treading cease. In Mediterranean agricultural cultures, the grape harvest was the year's great celebration; its absence signaled total social dissolution.
48.34: The cry from Heshbon to Elealeh, to Jahaz across the whole Moabite plateau — from the north (Heshbon) across to Zoar, Eglath-shelishiyah in the south, even to Nimrim — maps the spatial extent of the lamentation. The waters of Nimrim will become desolate: the springs (mei Nimrim) that supported settlement and agriculture will dry up or be abandoned. The combination of weeping voices from the cities and the drying of waters creates a picture of total human and ecological devastation.
48.35: The specific judgment — I will bring to an end in Moab those who offer sacrifice on the high place and those who burn incense to their gods — targets the Moabite cultic system directly. The high places (bamot) of Moab were the outdoor worship sites where sacrifices were offered, mentioned in Numbers 22-25 in connection with Balaam and Baal-Peor. The end of Moabite sacrifice marks the end of Moabite religious community — the cult cannot continue without the nation.
48.36: The divine lament — therefore my heart moans for Moab like flutes, my heart moans like flutes for the men of Kir-heres — uses the image of funeral flutes, the standard instrument of mourning in the ancient Near East (cf. Matt 9:23). The phrase levi yehemeh kechalalim (my heart moans like flutes) is extraordinarily intimate: YHWH's internal state is described as the mournful sound of the instrument played at funerals. The riches made by Moab have perished — economic prosperity and cultic worship are jointly destroyed.
48.37: The mourning rites enumerated — every head is shaved, every beard cut off; every hand is gashed; on every waist is sackcloth — represent the complete repertoire of ancient Near Eastern grief expression. Shaved head, cut beard, gashed hands, and sackcloth all appear in ANE lament texts and practice (cf. Jer 41:5 for Israelite mourning; Ezekiel 27:31 for Tyrian mourning). The total coverage — every head, every beard, every hand, every waist — means the entire Moabite population is simultaneously in mourning.
48.38: The public space of mourning — on all the housetops of Moab and in the squares there is nothing but lamentation — uses the flat rooftops of ancient Levantine houses as communal gathering places for mourning, as they were for other social activities. YHWH's own summary — I have broken Moab like a vessel for which no one cares (kekheli ʾein ḥephets bo) — is devastating: Moab is smashed not like a valuable jar but like worthless pottery carelessly discarded. The image of the broken, unwanted vessel conveys complete dismissal.
48.39: The triple lamentation — how it is broken! Wail! How Moab has turned his back in shame! — is a public cry of devastation. That Moab has become a laughingstock and an object of horror (lemahattah) to all his neighbors inverts the opening indictment (v. 26-27): Moab who mocked Israel now becomes the object of mockery and horror for surrounding nations. The principle of poetic justice is complete: the mockers become the mocked, the proud become the pitiable.
48.40: The declaration — look, he flies like an eagle and spreads his wings over Moab — introduces the conqueror as an eagle swooping on prey. The eagle (nesher) is the standard biblical image for swift, powerful conquest (cf. Deut 28:49; Hab 1:8; Ezek 17:3). The enemy swooping like an eagle from the sky combines speed and inevitability: the prey cannot outrun the bird of prey. The image is ANE-wide; Assyrian victory inscriptions and Neo-Babylonian texts use the eagle as a royal and martial symbol.
48.41: The captured cities — Kerioth is taken and the strongholds are seized — move the oracle toward completion. The military heart of Moab is gone. The warriors' anguish — the heart of Moab's warriors on that day will be like the heart of a woman in labor — uses the labor-pain metaphor for anguish and helplessness that is widespread in the prophets (cf. Isa 13:8; 21:3; Jer 4:31; 22:23). The strongest men reduced to the involuntary agony of childbirth captures the complete loss of control and the overwhelming nature of the suffering.
48.42: The final theological indictment — Moab will be destroyed and no longer be a people, because he magnified himself against YHWH — restates the root sin identified in v. 26. Anti-YHWH arrogance is both the cause and the justification of the judgment. The national destruction is proportional to the theological offense: a nation that made itself the measure of its own significance, refusing to acknowledge YHWH's sovereignty, loses its national existence. The judgment matches the sin.
48.43: The triple terror formula — terror, pit, and snare are before you, O inhabitant of Moab — echoes the famous triple-terror verse of Isaiah 24:17-18, which Jeremiah appears to quote here. The sequence of terror-pit-snare creates an inescapable trap: those who flee the terror fall into the pit; those who climb out of the pit are caught in the snare. The chain of inescapable fates was a rhetorical device for expressing total judgment; Isaiah and Jeremiah both deploy it in OAN contexts.
48.44: The inescapability is developed — he who flees from the terror will fall into the pit, and he who climbs out of the pit will be caught in the snare — as an elaborate description of the closing trap. The triple-terror sequence borrowed from Isaiah 24:17-18 and reapplied to Moab demonstrates the prophetic tradition's intertextual use of prior oracles. The year of their punishment (shenat peqqudatam) is coming — the appointed time of reckoning that cannot be avoided.
48.45: The refugees who fled to Heshbon — the Amorite capital before its conquest by Moses (Num 21:21-31) — standing exhausted in its shadow, the fire going out from Heshbon and devouring Moab, recalls the ancient Song of Heshbon (Num 21:27-30), which described the original Amorite conquest of Moab. Jeremiah's oracle thus frames Moab's fall as a repetition of its original conquest: the same fire that once burned through Moab from Heshbon burns through it again. History repeats as judgment.
48.46: The lament — woe to you, Moab! The people of Chemosh are destroyed — uses the funeral cry (oy lekha) over the nation and its patron deity. Chemosh's worshippers are destroyed — the national deity has failed his people. Your sons are taken into exile and your daughters into captivity — the deportation formula completes the picture of national destruction. The people of Chemosh will experience what the people of YHWH have experienced: exile, captivity, the dissolution of national existence. Chemosh proves as powerless to prevent this as lesser gods had been.
48.47: The surprising restoration promise — yet I will restore the fortunes of Moab in the latter days, declares YHWH — is one of several such restoration promises appended to the OAN for Moab (here), Ammon (49:6), and Elam (49:39). These eschatological restoration promises signal that the judgment, though severe, is not YHWH's final word even to Israel's historic enemies. The phrase ʾaḥarit hayamim (in the latter days/end of days) is an eschatological marker pointing beyond present judgment to a transformed future order. The book closes even this harsh judgment with a horizon of hope.

49.1: The Oracle Against Ammon opens with a territorial accusation: the Ammonites (Benei Ammon) had occupied the land of Gad (the Transjordanian tribe) after Assyria deported the northern Israelite tribes in 722 BCE. Milcom (Malcam) is the national god of Ammon, related to the divine name Molech. His worship involved child sacrifice (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 32:35). The question — 'Has Israel no heir?' — is a rhetorical challenge: the Ammonite occupation of vacated Israelite land violated the principle that the land belongs to YHWH and his covenant people, not to be permanently absorbed by pagan neighbors. Rabbah of the Ammonites is modern Amman, Jordan — the capital city whose name (rabbah = great/capital) persists in the modern city name.
49.2: The war cry (teri'ah) against Rabbah of the Ammonites anticipates its desolation: it will become a tel (ruin-mound), a desolation heap (gal shomemah). Tel is the archaeological term for the layered mound left by successive ancient settlements — the very word used for archaeological tells across the Near East (Tell es-Sultan = Jericho, Tell el-Hesi, etc.). The verse precisely anticipates what happened: ancient Rabbah is buried under layers of occupation. The daughter cities (surrounding settlements) will be burned, and Israel will re-inherit what Ammon took — a reversal of the dispossession named in v. 1.
49.3: Heshbon (originally Moabite/Reubenite territory, now near Ammonite borders) is called to wail. Ai here is not the Canaanite Ai of Joshua 7-8 but an Ammonite city in Transjordan. The daughters of Rabbah — surrounding settlements or communities (bat = daughter-city is a standard idiom for dependent settlements) — are told to put on sackcloth. The exile of Ammonite priests and their officials (kohanim and sarim) mirrors the pattern of Judah's own exile: national institutions — priesthood and royal administration — go into exile together. Milcom's exile (his cultic objects and priests removed) means the national deity has been defeated and taken as plunder, the ancient Near Eastern sign of conquest.
49.4: The taunt against 'the faithless daughter' (habat hashoveivah): Ammon is characterized by a flowing valley (imeqeich zav, perhaps the Jabbok River or the lush Jordan valley agriculture) and by trust in treasures. The accusation — 'who will come against me?' — is the hubris of geographic security. Ammon's position east of Jordan, sheltered by terrain and by relative distance from major invasion routes, bred confidence. The rhetorical answer is: YHWH. National overconfidence rooted in natural advantages rather than covenant relationship is a recurring OAN theme.
49.5: Terror brought from all surrounding neighbors: Ammon was surrounded by Moab to the south, Aram to the north, Israel to the west — multiple potential vectors of judgment. The phrase Lord GOD of hosts (Adonai YHWH Tzvaot) combines all three divine names: the covenant name, the divine universal sovereignty, and the cosmic army. Niddach ish lefanav — each one will be driven out straight ahead, with no one to gather the fugitives. The absence of a gatherer/shepherd is the sign of covenant breakdown: the flock scattered with no one to collect it.
49.6: The restoration promise for Ammon: ve-acharei-chen ashiv et-shevut benei Ammon (afterward I will restore the fortunes of the Ammonites). The pattern — judgment oracle → qualified restoration promise — appears repeatedly in the OAN section (also 49:39 for Elam; 48:47 for Moab). This does not minimize the judgment but places it within a larger framework: YHWH's purpose is not permanent erasure of nations but correction and, where possible, restoration. The Ammonites historically survived and are attested through the Persian period.
49.7: The Oracle Against Edom opens with a challenge to Teman's famous wisdom: ha-ein od chokhmah beTeman (is there no longer wisdom in Teman?). Teman was an important Edomite city in the north of Edom (the name means 'south' in Hebrew — from the perspective of Israel). The Edomites were renowned for wisdom tradition: Job's friend Eliphaz is from Teman (Job 2:11); Obadiah 8 also challenges Edom's sages. The wisdom motif suggests Edom had an intellectual tradition, perhaps connected to its position on trade routes where information from Egypt, Arabia, and Mesopotamia converged. That the wise counselors could not foresee their own destruction is the irony: wisdom that excludes YHWH cannot anticipate YHWH's actions.
49.8: Dedan was an Arabian trading city (modern al-Ula in Saudi Arabia) in communication with Edom along caravan routes. The instruction to 'flee into the depths' (amqu lashevet, withdraw to dwell deep) is survival advice — hide from the coming disaster. Eid Esav (the disaster/calamity of Esau) — the time of his punishment has come. This is the Edom = Esau identification that runs through Obadiah and Genesis: Esau is the ancestor of Edom (Gen 36:1, 8), and the Jacob-Esau rivalry becomes the Israel-Edom rivalry. The 'time of his visitation' (eit piqadti oto) is the covenantal reckoning: YHWH has scheduled this moment.
49.9: The comparison of incomplete gleaning versus total stripping: grape-gatherers leave some gleanings (olelot — the commandment of Lev 19:10 requires leaving gleanings for the poor); thieves steal only enough for their needs. But Edom's devastation will be total — nothing left. The gleaning-law reference is ironic: Edom will receive no such mercy; not even the minimum left for the poor. The comparison implies that the instrument of judgment will be more thorough than any human thief or harvester.
49.10: YHWH himself strips Esau bare (ani chalafti et-Esav, I myself have stripped Esau). The language of stripping/uncovering (chalaf) echoes the imagery of removing a covering: Edom's hiding places (mistaro) are exposed. His children, brothers, and neighbors are all destroyed — no network of survival. The verse ends: ve-ein hu (and he is no more). Edom's total erasure is announced. This was historically fulfilled: the Edomites were displaced by Arab pressure in the fifth-fourth centuries BCE, moved into the Negev (becoming Idumeans), and the Nabataean Arabs occupied Petra. The Idumeans were forcibly converted to Judaism by John Hyrcanus in 125 BCE and absorbed into the Jewish people. Edom as a nation ceased to exist.
49.11: An unexpected mercy within the judgment oracle: azov yetomecha ani achayyeh uvalmenoticha alay yivtachu (leave your orphans; I will keep them alive; your widows may trust in me). Even within the comprehensive judgment on Edom, YHWH declares himself protector of the most vulnerable — orphans and widows, the social categories without protective male relatives. The verse creates a moral distinction: YHWH destroys Edom's power structures but protects the powerless. This anticipates the NT pattern of divine judgment that distinguishes between the powerful who relied on self and the vulnerable who relied on God.
49.12: The cup of judgment logic: asher ein mishpatam lishtot hakkos shotoh yishtu (those who did not deserve to drink the cup have had to drink it). 'Those who did not deserve to drink' refers to Judah — YHWH's own people who drank the cup of his wrath in the exile. If Judah received judgment despite the covenant, how much more will Edom — without covenant protection — receive judgment? The argument from greater to lesser (qal va-chomer): if the guilty party received judgment, the unpunished party certainly will not escape. Edom will definitely drink.
49.13: The divine oath (ki vi nishba'ti, for I have sworn by myself) intensifies the certainty: YHWH swears by himself because there is nothing greater by which to swear (cf. Heb 6:13: 'when God made his promise to Abraham ... he swore by himself'). The fate of Bozrah — the major city of Edom (modern Buseirah in Jordan) — is pronounced: desolation, reproach, ruin, curse. Four destruction terms underscore the comprehensiveness. Archaeological surveys of Buseirah confirm major occupation in the Iron Age that ended abruptly, consistent with Jeremiah's oracle.
49.14: The divine messenger sent among the nations (tzir bashulach bagoyim): a herald deployed to gather the nations for war against Edom. This is the divine war council in action — YHWH orders the campaign. The intelligence network of the ancient Near East (trade caravans, merchants) served as a communication system: news of military campaigns spread rapidly. The oracle reflects this: a divine decree goes out that galvanizes coalition forces.
49.15: Hineh qaton netaticha bagoyim baz ba-adam (I will make you small among the nations, despised among mankind). Edom's pride (zadon libbecha, the arrogance of your heart, v. 16) is met with the reversal: the nation that thought itself superior becomes the most despised. The elevation of Edom's physical position (v. 16 — heights and eagle-nests) is the occasion of the fall. Obadiah 3-4 elaborates the same image: 'though you soar like the eagle and make your nest among the stars, I will bring you down.'
49.16: Tiflatecha hissi'ah otach zadon libbecha shoken bechagvei hasela' tofes merom giva: 'Your fearsome reputation has deceived you, the arrogance of your heart — O you who dwell in the clefts of the rock and hold the height of the hill.' The 'clefts of the rock' (chagvei hasela) is a strong geographical allusion to Petra (Arabic: 'rock' = Nabataean Raqmu), Edom's famous rock-city. The cliff-dwellings and mountain fortresses of Edom's Transjordanian landscape — including Umm el-Biyara, Sela, and later Petra — gave the Edomites confidence of impregnability. 'Though you make your nest as high as the eagle, I will bring you down from there' — the pride of geographic security is named and reversed.
49.17: Edom will become a horror (shamma): passersby will be appalled (yishom, hiss) at all its calamities. The public visibility of Edom's desolation — nations observing and reacting — is the covenant-curse sign of maximal disgrace. The same language appears for Judah/Jerusalem in 18:16; 19:8; 25:9, 11, 18. By applying it to Edom, Jeremiah places the pagan nation under the same covenant-curse framework: YHWH's judgment on the nations uses the same register as his judgment on his own people.
49.18: Ke-mahpechat Sedom va-Amorah vishneiha: the Sodom-Gomorrah comparison for Edom. The destroyed cities of the plain (Gen 19) became the biblical shorthand for total divine judgment — no survivors, no settlement, permanent desolation. The comparison invokes both the severity and the moral dimension: Sodom was judged for specific sins (Ezek 16:49-50 adds: pride, excess food, failure to help the poor, and abomination). Applying the Sodom comparison to Edom implies moral equivalence: Edom's sins of pride and violence against its brother Jacob (cf. Obadiah's full catalog) rise to the Sodom level.
49.19: The lion-from-Jordan image: ke-aryeh ya-aleh migaon haYarden (like a lion coming up from the thickets of the Jordan). The Jordan's floodplain (gaon haYarden) was dense vegetation where lions sheltered — the lion ascending to higher pasture is an image of irresistible, sudden attack. YHWH compares himself to the lion: he will suddenly rouse Edom and appoint whom he chooses over it. The image of divine appointment — mi bachur elaeiha (who is chosen for her?) — suggests YHWH specifically selects the instrument of Edom's judgment, as he selected Babylon for Judah. No one can summon him to account: the rhetorical question 'who is like me? and who will challenge me? and what shepherd can stand before me?' asserts absolute divine sovereignty in the execution of judgment.
49.20: The divine plan (etzat YHWH) against Edom and the divine purposes (machshevotav) against Teman: both terms are the language of divine deliberation and strategy. Edom is not a secondary casualty of geopolitical events — it is a specific object of divine intent. The young (tz'irei hatzon, the lambs of the flock) will be dragged away; their pasture will be appalled at them — the land itself reacting to the destruction, an image of the creation responding to covenant violation (cf. the land vomiting out its inhabitants, Lev 18:28).
49.21: Miqqol nifalam ra-ashshah ha-aretz tzaaaqah baYam-Suf nishma qolah: the noise of Edom's fall causes the earth to tremble, and the cry is heard at the Red Sea (Yam Suf). The Red Sea reference is geographically specific: Edom's territory extended to the Gulf of Aqaba (the eastern arm of the Red Sea; cf. 1 Kgs 9:26 — Solomon's fleet at Ezion-geber). The trembling earth and far-reaching cry convey the enormity of the fall. A great empire's collapse reverberates cosmically.
49.22: The eagle-swooping image: hineh ke-nesher ya-aleh vidaa (like an eagle ascending and spreading its wings over Bozrah). The Babylonian army swooping over Bozrah like an eagle. The eagle (nesher) was a standard ANE symbol of military power — the Babylonian army used eagle-wing imagery on their battle standards; the Assyrians similarly used winged-bull and eagle iconography. The hearts of Edom's warriors becoming like the heart of a woman in labor (yom tzarah) is the ancient idiom for panic-induced helplessness: the most fearsome soldiers lose all capacity for courage.
49.23: The Oracle Against Damascus opens with the alarm of Hamath and Arpad (major cities of Syria to the north of Damascus). Bad news (shemu-ah ra-ah) has traveled and dismantled their composure. Hamath (modern Hama on the Orontes River) and Arpad (Tell Rif'at, north of Aleppo) were major Syrian cities that had already fallen to Assyria (Hamath in 720 BCE under Sargon II; Arpad in 740 BCE under Tiglath-pileser III). Their mention here suggests the oracle looks at a broader Syrian collapse that includes Damascus. The 'anguish like the sea that cannot be quiet' (yam de-agah lo tishqot) uses the sea's restlessness as an image of uncontainable anxiety.
49.24: Damascus grows feeble (raphah Damascus), turns to flee, panic seizes her. The 'city of renown' (ir tehillati, city of my joy/praise — YHWH speaking) — Damascus is described with a term of divine affection before its destruction. This unexpected note of divine lament adds pathos: even the condemned city was once the object of divine pleasure. The birth-pangs image (chil keyoleda) appears again — universal ANE metaphor for military helplessness.
49.25: Eich lo-azuvah ir tehillah qiriyat masoseh: 'How is the renowned city not abandoned, the city of my delight!' A difficult verse: is this YHWH lamenting the impending abandonment, or a rhetorical exclamation at its unexpected fall? Damascus was one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world (continuously settled since at least 8000 BCE; mentioned in Egyptian records from the fifteenth century BCE). Its antiquity and prosperity made it seem permanent. The oracle cuts against that assumption: no city is so ancient it cannot be abandoned.
49.26: The young men falling in the streets, warriors struck down: the same formula used for Jerusalem's fall (Lam 2:21) is applied to Damascus. The YHWH-of-hosts formula closes the Damascus oracle. Damascus historically survived but was repeatedly contested — the Babylonians campaigned in Syria in 604-600 BCE; Nebuchadnezzar controlled the region. Damascus did not experience the same total desolation as Edom, but it fell under Babylonian hegemony.
49.27: The burning of Damascus's walls and the palaces of Ben-hadad: Ben-hadad (Bar-Hadad = son of Hadad, the storm-god) was a dynastic name for the kings of Aram-Damascus — there were at least three Ben-hadads in the ninth-eighth centuries BCE (1 Kgs 15:18; 20:1; 2 Kgs 8:7-15). The palaces bear the dynastic name, identifying them as the seat of Aramean royal power. The burning of palaces is the standard mark of conquest in the ancient Near East — the defeat of the royal house and its divine patron.
49.28: The Oracle Against Kedar and the kingdoms of Hazor: Nebuchadnezzar is explicitly named as the instrument of judgment. Kedar was a major confederation of Arab tribes from the northern Arabian peninsula, mentioned in Assyrian records as early as the ninth century BCE (the Qidri in the Annals of Tiglath-pileser III). Genesis 25:13 identifies Kedar as a son of Ishmael. Hazor here is not the Galilean Hazor of Joshua 11 but a term for settled Arab communities in the Syro-Arabian desert. The command qum (arise!) addresses the Babylonian army — YHWH mobilizing his instrument.
49.29: Their tents and flocks — pastoral property (ohaleihem vetzoneihem) — and their tent-curtains, vessels, and camels will be taken. The specific listing of Kedar's moveable wealth (tents, flocks, camels — the three pillars of nomadic economy) reflects accurate knowledge of their lifestyle. Camels were the primary capital asset of desert peoples; their loss meant economic ruin. The war cry over them — magor misaviv (terror on every side) — is the signature phrase of Jeremiah (6:25; 20:3, 10; 46:5; 49:29; see also 20:3 where Jeremiah renames Pashhur 'Magor-Missabib').
49.30: The Babylonian Chronicles record Nebuchadnezzar's campaign against Arab tribes in 599/598 BCE (the 'Hagarites' campaign in the Chronicles). The command to flee deep into the wilderness is ironic: nomadic peoples who lived in the wilderness as their normal habitat are now told to go even deeper into it for safety. The plan (chashav aleihem etzah) is YHWH's, executed by Nebuchadnezzar.
49.31: Qumu alu el-goy shanaen yoshev lavetach: 'Rise and advance against a nation living in ease that dwells in security.' The description of the Arab tribes — no gates, no bars, dwelling alone — reflects the nomadic tribal lifestyle: no walls (mobility is their defense), scattered habitation. They had 'no gates or bars' (delatayim ubriach) — in contrast to walled cities, their security was openness and mobility. This very quality makes them vulnerable to a concentrated military campaign they cannot wall out.
49.32: Their camels — their capital — become plunder; their cattle a spoil. Those who 'clip the corners of their hair' (qotztzei pe'ah): this is a specific cultural practice of Arab tribes mentioned in Herodotus (3.8) as distinctive Arabian grooming — they clipped the hair at the temples to honor their god Orotalt. The same practice is mentioned in Jer 9:26 and 25:23 as characterizing peripheral peoples. YHWH will scatter them to all the winds — the four-directional scattering formula (cf. 49:36 for Elam) indicates total dispersal with no remnant gathered together.
49.33: Hazor becoming a haunt of jackals (me'on tannim), a desolation forever (shemamah le-olam): the desolation-animal formula (jackals replacing human habitation) appears throughout the OAN section and in the Babylon oracle (50:39; 51:37). Jackals were the scavengers of the desert and ruined places — their presence marks the complete withdrawal of human civilization. The contrast between Kedar's current nomadic ease (v. 31) and the future jackal-haunted waste is the oracle's shock.
49.34: The Oracle Against Elam: dated to the beginning of Zedekiah's reign (ca. 598/597 BCE — immediately after Jehoiachin's exile). Elam was the ancient kingdom centered on Susa (modern Shush in Khuzestan Province, Iran). Its history extended back to the third millennium BCE. Elam was periodically a major power — the Elamites sacked Babylon in 1158 BCE. By Jeremiah's period, Elam had been devastated by Ashurbanipal of Assyria (ca. 647 BCE) but was recovering. Elamite archers were famous throughout the ancient world: Isa 22:6 mentions Elam's quiver; the Persian imperial army later relied heavily on Elamite bowmen.
49.35: Hinneni shover et-qeshet Elam reshit gevuratam: 'I am going to break the bow of Elam, the foremost source of their strength.' The bow was Elam's signature weapon and the basis of their military reputation. Breaking it is not merely military disarmament but the destruction of national identity — a people known by their bow lose their reason for being a warrior-nation. The phrase reshit gevuratam (the foremost source of their strength) is almost 'the thing they were best known for.' YHWH identifies and attacks the specific foundation of each nation's pride: Edom's wisdom (v. 7), Ammon's wealth (v. 4), Edom's geographic security (v. 16), Elam's bow.
49.36: The four winds from the four corners of heaven (arba ruchot mishteih shamayim): a cosmic dispersal. YHWH commands the winds themselves to scatter Elam. This language intensifies the dispersal beyond any human military action: the forces of creation are mobilized. The four-corners-of-heaven formula occurs in Ezek 37:9 (the valley of dry bones vision) and Zech 2:6 (the four winds scattering Israel). The dispersal to every nation, every people — there will be no land where Elamite exiles are not found.
49.37: Terror before enemies, disaster brought — charon api (my fierce anger), the burning wrath of YHWH sent against them. The sword after them — judgment that pursues rather than waiting. The sequence: terror (psychological), disaster (social/economic), sword (military) — all three modes of covenant curse (Lev 26; Deut 28). The emphasis is that YHWH himself brings the disaster: not the Babylonian army acting independently but YHWH's own wrath sent against them.
49.38: Vesimti kis-i be-Elam: 'I will place my throne in Elam.' The divine throne in Elam is the most striking image in this oracle: YHWH establishes his rule in the enemy territory, and from there destroys their king and officials. The throne of YHWH set up in enemy territory symbolizes universal sovereignty — no place is beyond his dominion (cf. Amos 9:2-4: 'though they go into captivity before their enemies, there I will command the sword to kill them'). The king (melekh) and his officials are specifically targeted — the leadership structure, not just the population.
49.39: The restoration promise for Elam in the latter days (be-acharit hayamim): the same formula as 48:47 (Moab) and 49:6 (Ammon). All three enemy nations in this cluster receive restoration promises. This pattern throughout the OAN reflects the theological principle that YHWH's judgment is corrective and not merely destructive: the 'latter days' restoration encompasses even traditional enemies of Israel. Historically, Elam was absorbed into the Persian empire — and Susa became a royal capital of Persia (Esther 1:2). Elamites were present at Pentecost (Acts 2:9): the eschatological restoration of Elam is there beginning to be realized.

50.1: The Great Oracle Against Babylon occupies two full chapters (50-51) — the longest single oracle in the book and arguably the most theologically dense. The superscription: hadavar asher diber YHWH el-Bavel el-eretz Kasdim beyad Yirmiyahu hanavi. The oracle is addressed to Babylon as a whole and to the land of the Chaldeans specifically — the Chaldeans being the ruling Babylonian dynasty (Neo-Babylonian dynasty founded by Nabopolassar in 626 BCE). The prophetic medium is specified: beyad Yirmiyahu (through/by the hand of Jeremiah) — the hand-formula for prophetic transmission emphasizes that this is mediated divine speech.
50.2: The proclamation formula to the nations: hagidu vagoyim vehashmi-u (announce and proclaim). Raise the signal banner, proclaim without hiding. What follows is the core announcement: Babylon is taken, Bel is put to shame, Marduk is dismayed, her images are put to shame, her idols are dismayed. Bel (title meaning 'lord') was the title of Marduk, the supreme god of Babylon — the Babylonian national deity who was believed to have given Nebuchadnezzar his victories. Chofer and chatat (shamed and dismayed) — the terms for divine defeat. When Babylon falls, Marduk's power is refuted. The proclamation to the nations inverts the Babylonian victory announcements that went out when Babylon conquered others.
50.3: A nation from the north (goy mitsafon) will come against Babylon and make her land a desolation. The 'foe from the north' that Jeremiah repeatedly applied to Babylon itself (1:14; 4:6; 6:22) is now turned against Babylon. The instrument of judgment becomes the object of judgment. Historically, this was fulfilled by Cyrus the Great of Persia (Achaemenid dynasty) who conquered Babylon in 539 BCE — the Persians came from the north and east. The formula 'man nor beast will dwell there' (ein yoshev bahem) echoes the desolation pattern applied to Judah in 4:25; now Babylon receives the same.
50.4: Bayamim hahem uveet hahi neum YHWH yavo-u venei Yisrael hemmah uvnei Yehudah yachdav: 'In those days and at that time, declares the LORD, the people of Israel and the people of Judah will come together.' The joint return of northern and southern kingdoms is a recurring restoration promise (3:18; 31:6, 31-34; 33:7). They will weep as they come (haloch vivecheh yelekhu) — penitential tears accompanying the return; and seek YHWH their God. The covenant-seeking formula: the return from exile is not merely geographic but theological — it is the people turning to their God (cf. Deut 30:1-6 where the return from exile is contingent on return to YHWH).
50.5: Tziyon yish-alu derekh hennah peneihem yilekhu: They will ask the way to Zion with their faces turned toward it. This is the reversal of exile: exile is face-turned-away from Zion (the covenantal geography of presence); return is face-turned-toward Zion. The covenant renewal they seek is described as berit olam (eternal/everlasting covenant) — the phrase that characterizes the new covenant of 31:31-34 and the Davidic covenant of 33:20-21. Lo tishakhach (not to be forgotten) — this covenant is permanent; no future apostasy will annul it. The return from Babylon is the beginning of the fulfillment of the new covenant promise.
50.6: Tzon ovedet hayu ammi: 'My people have been like lost sheep.' The shepherd-and-sheep covenant image runs through Jeremiah: the false shepherds of ch. 23 who led the flock astray; the promise of a good shepherd in 23:4-5. Here, the sheep 'wandered from mountain to hill' — the high-place worship that characterized Israel's apostasy; they 'forgot their resting place' (mavtzehem) — the covenantal resting place of YHWH's presence. Their enemies devoured them without guilt, 'because they sinned against the LORD, the true habitation of righteousness, the LORD, the hope of their fathers' (v. 7). The guilt-logic: the nations attacked Israel without their own guilt because Israel's sin invited the attack.
50.7: The enemy's self-exculpation: lo ne-esham (we are not guilty) — because Israel sinned against YHWH. This is the logic of covenant curse: when YHWH's people are punished, the nations who execute the judgment can claim they acted under divine sanction. But this logic has limits — the instruments of judgment can themselves be judged for excess or pride (cf. Isa 10:5-15 where Assyria is punished for its arrogance in executing YHWH's judgment). YHWH, the neveh tzedeq (habitation of righteousness) and mikveh avoteihem (hope of their fathers) — two divine epithets: the place where justice dwells and the object of ancestral hope.
50.8: The exodus command: nud mitorev Bavel (flee from the midst of Babylon). Isaiah 48:20 gives the same command: 'Come out of Babylon, flee from the Chaldeans.' Rev 18:4 repeats it for the eschatological Babylon: 'Come out of her, my people.' The exodus pattern: YHWH's people are to leave the place of captivity and return to the covenant land — just as the Exodus from Egypt structured the first redemption. The he-goat (ka-ayelim lifnei tzon) image: the lead animal at the front of the flock. The exiles are to take initiative and lead the way out.
50.9: An assembly of great nations from the north stirred up against Babylon: the Medo-Persian alliance that Cyrus led was indeed a coalition of peoples. Gedudim (companies/troops) arranged against Babylon. The arrows of the coalition will not miss: lo tashov reyqam (they will not return empty) — a warrior's arrows hitting their mark. The cosmic precision of divine judgment: the arrows of YHWH's appointed instrument are guided to their target.
50.10: Chaldea as plunder: vehayetah Kasdim leshallal kol sholaleiha yisba-u (Chaldea will become plunder; all who plunder her will be satisfied). The Babylonians who plundered the nations will themselves be plundered — the retributive mirror of covenant justice. The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BCE) describes Cyrus restoring temple treasures to their places: the plunder that Babylon took from the nations was redistributed when Babylon fell. Ezra 1:7-11 records Cyrus returning the temple vessels Nebuchadnezzar had taken from Jerusalem.
50.11: The indictment of Babylon's joy: they 'rejoiced and exulted' (samachtem ve-aloztem) as plunderers of YHWH's inheritance. The heifer-on-grass (ka-eglah doresheh) image depicts unrestrained, carefree joy — the animal sporting in a meadow, free of burden. This unrestrained celebration at Judah's destruction inverts what Babylon should have felt: any serious engagement with what they had done — destroying YHWH's city and temple, deporting his people — should have produced sobriety, not exuberance. The neighing (tzahaltun ka-abirim, neighed like stallions) adds the image of full-throated, uncontained pride.
50.12: Boshah imchem meod chafrah yoladtechem: 'Your mother will be utterly ashamed; the one who bore you will be disgraced.' Babylon's 'mother' — the Chaldean dynasty (Nabopolassar, the founder) or the city-goddess — will be put to shame. The end of the verse: acharit goyim midbar tziyah va-aravah (she will become the last of the nations — a wilderness, a dry land, a desert). Three progressively barren terms: wilderness, dry, desert. Babylon's lush Mesopotamian territory, irrigated by the Euphrates and Tigris, will become like the desert wastelands. Archaeological and geographic surveys confirm that after Alexander's death and the shift of population to Seleucia, the ancient city of Babylon progressively abandoned and was eventually completely deserted.
50.13: Because of YHWH's wrath (miqetzef YHWH), she will not be inhabited — shemamah kula (completely desolate). The hissing passersby (yishreq) echo the covenant curse formula: Babylon receives the same response from passersby that Jerusalem received (18:16; 19:8). The irony is complete: the nation that gloated over Jerusalem's fall becomes itself the object of travelers' horrified hissing.
50.14: The battle deployment against Babylon: erekhu al-Bavel misaviv (deploy yourselves against Babylon from every side). All who draw the bow — the Persian armies were famous archers (Herodotus describes the Persian-Median archers in detail). The command: 'do not spare your arrows' (al tachmosu al chetz). The reason: ki le-YHWH chata-ah (for she has sinned against the LORD). Babylon's sin is the basis of the military campaign: this is not merely geopolitical conquest but covenant-consequential judgment.
50.15: Shout against Babylon from every side — she has surrendered (nattanah yadah, she has given her hand). The surrendered hand is the ANE gesture of submission: a defeated king extended his hand to the victor to receive a cord or chain. Her foundations have fallen (naflu ashuteha): not merely the walls but the deep foundations of Babylonian civilization. Ki niqmat YHWH hi: this is the vengeance of the LORD — the retributive justice that returns on Babylon what it did to others. 'As she has done, do to her' is the lex talionis of covenant justice applied to nations.
50.16: Cut off the sower and the reaper: the agricultural cycle of Babylon — planting and harvest — is disrupted. The foreign workers flee: mipnei cherev hayonah ish el-ammo yifnu (before the sword of the oppressor, everyone will return to their own people). The foreign labor force — migrants and subjugated peoples working Babylonian fields — disperses at the first sign of military collapse. This was historically accurate: Babylon's agricultural economy depended on subject peoples; their departure at the Persian conquest accelerated the city's economic decline.
50.17: Seh pezurah Yisrael arayot hiddichu: 'Israel is a scattered sheep; lions have driven it away.' The scattered-sheep image (50:6) returns. Two sequential predators are named: first the king of Assyria devoured it, last Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon has gnawed his bones. The two-stage destruction of Israel: the Assyrian conquest of the north (722 BCE) and the Babylonian conquest of the south (586 BCE). Both are identified as lions — powerful predators — but both will be punished in turn (v. 18). The chronological sequence (first Assyria, then Babylon) structures Judah's history within the oracle.
50.18: Laken koh amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael: therefore YHWH will punish the king of Babylon and his land just as he punished the king of Assyria (ka-asher paqadti al melech Ashur). Assyria's fate is the precedent for Babylon's. Assyria was destroyed by the Babylonian-Median alliance in 614-612 BCE (fall of Nineveh) and 610-609 BCE (fall of Harran) — within Jeremiah's lifetime. The same God who brought down Assyria will bring down Babylon. The historical precedent validates the prophetic claim.
50.19: The restoration of Israel to its pasture: vehashiboti et-Yisrael el-navehu (I will bring Israel back to his pasture). The geographic specificity — Carmel, Bashan, Ephraim, Gilead — represents all of Israel's most productive territories. Carmel (fertile coastal range), Bashan (rich tableland east of the Sea of Galilee, famous for cattle — Amos 4:1), Ephraim (the central highlands), Gilead (Transjordanian highlands). Tishba nafsho (his appetite/soul will be satisfied) — the soul-satisfaction formula of Ps 107:9 ('he satisfies the thirsty soul and fills the hungry soul with good things'). Return from exile means complete restoration of the covenant life.
50.20: Bayamim hahem uveet hahi neum YHWH yevuqqash et-avon Yisrael veeynenu: 'In those days and at that time, declares the LORD, Israel's iniquity will be searched for, but there will be none, and the sins of Judah will not be found; for I will forgive those I spare.' This is the most comprehensive forgiveness formula in Jeremiah — total iniquity-erasure. The connection to 31:34 ('I will forgive their iniquity and remember their sin no more') is explicit: the new covenant promise of complete forgiveness, applied to the restoration from Babylon as its initial fulfillment. The search for iniquity yields nothing — it has been expunged. NT application: 1 John 1:9 ('he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness') and Heb 10:17-18 ('their sins and their lawless acts I will remember no more').
50.21: Merathaim and Pekod: two geographic locations with Hebrew wordplay. Merathaim (double rebellion/bitterness) is a wordplay on Marratu, the Babylonian name for the marshy area at the confluence of the Tigris and Euphrates (the Persian Gulf region). Pekod (visitation/punishment) is a wordplay on Puqudu, an Aramean tribe in southern Babylonia. The puns make the oracle's meaning visible in the geography: YHWH has built judgment into the very names of Babylon's territories. Cherem (complete destruction/ban): the same term used for holy war in Joshua — the Canaanites were devoted to cherem destruction; now Babylon receives the same designation.
50.22: Qol milchamah ba-aretz veshever gadol: the sound of battle and great destruction. The two terms — sound of battle (qol milchamah) and great destruction (shever gadol) — are the two dimensions of the fall: the audible chaos of combat and the visible wreckage left behind. The compressed brevity of the verse (five Hebrew words) is itself expressive: the disaster arrives with blunt, sudden force.
50.23: Eikh niqqatz veyishaver pattish kol-ha-aretz: 'How the hammer of the whole earth has been cut to pieces and broken!' Babylon as the hammer of the whole earth (pattish kol-ha-aretz) — YHWH's instrument for striking the nations (cf. 51:20-23 where Babylon is 'my war club' and 'weapon of war'). The hammer is now broken: the instrument that was used to beat others is itself beaten. The exclamation eikh (how!) is the lament particle of Lamentations 1:1 ('How lonely sits the city'). Even Babylon's fall elicits something like lament at the magnitude of what has happened.
50.24: Yaqoshti lach vegam nillakhat Bavel: 'I laid a snare for you and you were caught, O Babylon, before you knew it.' The divine hunter who sets snares (cf. 5:26 where the wicked of Israel set snares for men; now YHWH sets the snare for Babylon). The surprise of capture — lo yadata (before you knew it) — reflects the historical suddenness of Babylon's fall: according to Herodotus and the Nabonidus Chronicle, Cyrus entered Babylon in 539 BCE while Belshazzar held a feast (Dan 5). The reason: ki el-YHWH higgarat (because you strove against the LORD). Striving against YHWH is the fundamental category of Babylonian sin — not merely political overreach but ontological hubris.
50.25: YHWH has opened his armory (pithach YHWH et-otzaro) and brought out the weapons of his anger (kele za-amo). The divine armory is the arsenal of cosmic warfare — the divine warrior's weapons. The work of the Lord GOD of hosts is in the land of the Chaldeans — the phrase melechet Adonai YHWH Tzvaot (the work of the Lord GOD of hosts) frames Babylon's destruction as a divine project, not a human military achievement. Cyrus is the instrument; YHWH is the agent.
50.26: The siege commands: attack from every end (miqetz ba-u lah), open her granaries (pitchu mizbanoieha), pile her up like heaps (soluha ka-aremim), completely destroy her, let nothing remain. The granary-opening image: Babylon's immense grain stores (Herodotus describes Babylon's twenty-year food supply) are opened for plunder. The heap-image (aremim): grain heaps, but also ruin-mounds — tel. Both meanings converge: the grain stores become ruins.
50.27: Slaughter all her bulls — her military leadership, the most powerful men. The bovine metaphor for warriors (cf. Ps 22:12: 'strong bulls of Bashan surround me') is standard ANE imagery. The killing ground (yedu latebach, go down to the slaughter) echoes the sacrifice-slaughter image. Oi lahem ki va yomam eit piqudatam: Woe to them, for their day has come — the time of their reckoning. Eit piqudat is the appointed-time-of-visitation formula: the date for covenant reckoning was set in YHWH's calendar before Babylon ever conquered Judah.
50.28: Those who flee from Babylon declare YHWH's vengeance in Zion: mesha-eim uferiteimim me-eretz Bavel lehagid beTziyon et-niqmat YHWH. The fugitives' proclamation of YHWH's vengeance in Zion serves an apologetic purpose: the destruction of Babylon is declared to be specifically about the temple. Niqmat heichalo (vengeance for his temple): the burning of the temple in 586 BCE is the specific grievance; Babylon's fall is YHWH vindicating his own house. The theme of temple-vengeance grounds the geopolitical event in covenant theology.
50.29: Archers summoned against Babylon: encamp all around, let none escape, give her according to her work (shalem la kema-aseiha), act to her according to all she has done (kekol asher-asatah asu lah). The proportionality principle: Babylon receives exactly what she gave. The specific provocation: ki el-YHWH zadah (for she has acted arrogantly against the LORD), against the Holy One of Israel. The title qadosh Yisrael (Holy One of Israel) is characteristic of Isaiah (used 26 times in Isa 1-66) but appears here in Jeremiah — the rarity marks the intensity of the theological claim: Babylon has offended YHWH's holiness itself.
50.30: Young men fallen in the streets, warriors silenced: the standard formula of city-fall applied to Babylon (cf. Lam 2:21; Jer 49:26; 50:30). The repetition across the oracles against multiple nations establishes a pattern: YHWH's judgment formula is consistently applied regardless of which nation receives it. The universality of the judgment pattern — no city exempt — is itself the theological point.
50.31: Hinneni eleicha zadon: 'Behold, I am against you, O most arrogant.' Zadon (arrogance/pride) is personified as Babylon itself — the empire is its arrogance. The divine adversative (hinneni eleicha) appears throughout Jeremiah as the covenant lawsuit formula for divine opposition. The time has come: ki va yomcha eit piqadticha (your day has come, the time I will punish you). YHWH appointed the time of Babylon's punishment before it happened — the oracle was given decades before Cyrus.
50.32: The most arrogant will stumble and fall (ve-kashel ve-nafal hazadon) — no one to raise him up. The sequence of stumbling and falling is the same vocabulary used for Israel's fall in Amos 5:2 ('fallen, no more to rise, is the virgin Israel'). The same fate now comes to the empire that caused Israel to fall. Fire set to Babylon's cities consuming all around: the burning of cities is the standard conquest-conclusion in the ancient Near East (the Lachish reliefs of Sennacherib show Assyrian fires consuming Judean cities).
50.33: The shared oppression: koh amar YHWH Tzvaot ashukot benei Yisrael uvnei Yehudah yachdav (the people of Israel are oppressed, and the people of Judah with them). All their captors hold them fast (kol shoveichem hecheziku vam) and refuse to release them (me-anu shalacham). The legal-rescue framing: YHWH as the kinsman-redeemer (go-el) who pleads the cause of the held-captive. The captors' refusal to release triggers the goel's action: when human captors will not release, the divine redeemer acts directly.
50.34: Go-alam chazaq YHWH Tzvaot shemo riv yariv et-rivam: 'Their Redeemer is mighty; the LORD of hosts is his name. He will certainly plead their cause.' The go-el (kinsman-redeemer) title for YHWH: in Israelite law, the go-el was the nearest male relative who had the right and obligation to redeem a family member from slavery, avenge his blood, or redeem mortgaged family land. YHWH is the ultimate go-el (cf. Isa 43:14; 44:6, 24; 47:4; 48:17 where 'go-el' is a major Isaiah title for YHWH). The strongest kinsman who owes redemption-loyalty will act — Babylon has enslaved what belongs to him.
50.35: The sword litany begins: cherev al-Kasdim (a sword against the Chaldeans). The sequence of sword-against in vv. 35-38 is a poem — seven recipients of the sword: inhabitants of Babylon (v. 35), officials and wise men (v. 35), false prophets (v. 36), warriors (v. 36), horses and chariots (v. 37), foreign troops (v. 37), treasures (v. 37), waters (v. 38). The comprehensiveness maps every component of Babylonian power. The prophets and wise men (chakhameha) — Babylon's divination establishment (the baru priests, astrologers, omen-readers) were famous throughout the ancient world; Daniel 1:20 notes the Babylonian wise men's status at court. Their sword-judgment means Babylon's intelligence apparatus will fail.
50.36: A sword against her false prophets (badim, diviners/liars) — they will become fools (venoallu). The Hebrew badim refers specifically to false prophets or diviners; the root suggests those who speak empty or false words. Babylon's religious-political prophecy establishment — which consistently predicted Babylonian invincibility — will be exposed as foolish when the empire falls. A sword against her warriors, so they become dismayed: the military elite who formed Babylon's professional army (the gabbe soldiers) will lose heart.
50.37: A sword against her horses and chariots: the chariot corps was Babylon's mechanized warfare. A sword against the foreign troops (ha-erev): the mixed multitude of mercenaries and subject-peoples in Babylon's army. They will become like women (vehaytem lanashim) — the idiom for complete loss of martial courage (cf. Isa 19:16; Nah 3:13). A sword against her treasures: the immense wealth of Babylon — plundered from nations, hoarded in temples and palaces — becomes subject to plunder itself.
50.38: A drought upon her waters: chorev al-meiha veyvoshu (drought upon her waters and they will dry up). This is wordplay: cherev (sword) in vv. 35-37 shifts to chorev (drought/heat) in v. 38 — the two words differ only in vocalization. The Euphrates and its canals were the lifeblood of Babylon; Cyrus's strategy according to Herodotus involved diverting the Euphrates to allow his troops to enter through the dry riverbed. The drying of the waters is both literal (the military tactic) and theological (YHWH withdrawing the life-support of Babylon). Ki eretz pesilim hi (for it is a land of idols) — the worship of images (pesilim) is the spiritual root of Babylon's judgment.
50.39: Wild desert creatures and jackals will inhabit Babylon; ostriches will live there. The three desolation-animals (demons of the wilderness, jackals, ostriches) appear in the similar desolation oracles of Isa 13:21-22 and 34:13-15 — standard ancient Near Eastern imagery for the complete withdrawal of human civilization and its replacement by the creatures of uninhabited waste. Lo-teshev la-netzach (she shall never again be inhabited) — the permanent desolation formula. Archaeological evidence: after Alexander the Great's death, plans to rebuild Babylon were abandoned; the city was progressively deserted as population moved to Seleucia on the Tigris. By the first century CE, Babylon was a ruin inhabited by Parthian soldiers.
50.40: The Sodom-Gomorrah comparison applied to Babylon: the same comparison made for Edom (49:18) now describes Babylon. Both Edom and Babylon receive the ultimate judgment-type — complete, permanent, uninhabitable. The repetition reinforces that Jeremiah applies the same theological framework to all the nations: YHWH's judgment on pagan powers uses the Sodom-Gomorrah template as its benchmark. Lo yeshev sham ish (no man will dwell there): the formula of absolute desolation that applies to both individuals and communities.
50.41: A people coming from the north — a great nation, many kings roused from the ends of the earth. The Medo-Persian coalition: Cyrus united the Medes (who had destroyed Nineveh in 612 BCE), the Persians, and allied peoples from across the Iranian plateau. Yaqitzu (roused): the awakening-of-kings image suggests divine mobilization — YHWH rousing the nations as his instruments, as he roused Nebuchadnezzar against Judah (1:15; 5:15-17).
50.42: They hold bow and spear; cruel, showing no mercy. Their roaring like the sea (yish-agu kayam): the coalition army's voice fills the land like the surf — overwhelming, constant, irresistible. On horses, arrayed like a man for battle against Babylon. The Persian-Median horse cavalry was formidable; the royal Persian Immortals (10,000 elite troops) were the most professional army of their era. The same phrase that Jeremiah used to describe the Babylonian threat against Judah (6:23: 'they are cruel and show no mercy; their voice roars like the sea') is now applied to the threat against Babylon.
50.43: The king of Babylon hears the report and his hands go limp: the same reaction that Zedekiah and the kings of Judah had when they heard of Babylon's advance. The reversal is exact and deliberate: the empire that caused Judah's kings to tremble will itself tremble. Chil kayoleda (anguish like a woman in labor): the universal ANE image for paralytic fear. Nebuchadnezzar — or his successors — the great conqueror, reduced to childbirth-pain helplessness.
50.44: The lion-from-Jordan image that was used for YHWH against Edom (49:19) now appears against Babylon. The repetition of the image frames the OAN section as a unified whole: YHWH uses the same hunting strategy against all the nations. The rhetorical question — who is appointed over Babylon? — has a clear answer: YHWH himself. He will appoint his chosen one over her. Historically, YHWH's chosen one for this task was Cyrus — explicitly named in Isa 44:28 and 45:1 as YHWH's anointed and his shepherd.
50.45: Lachen shim-u etzat YHWH asher ya-atz el-Bavel umachshevotav asher chashav el-eretz Kasdim: hear the strategy of YHWH against Babylon and his purposes against the Chaldeans. The young of the flock (tz-irei hatzon) will be dragged away — the same phrase used for Edom (49:20). The pasture-horror: asham aleihem naveihem (their pasture will be appalled at them). YHWH's plan is fixed and announced ahead of time: prophecy is the advance announcement of history from the divine council.
50.46: At the sound of Babylon's capture the earth will quake (tera-ash ha-aretz); the cry will be heard among the nations (yishama bagoyim za-aqatah). The cosmic scale of Babylon's fall: the earth itself shakes. The Babylonian empire was the dominant power for seventy years — its fall in 539 BCE was a seismic geopolitical event whose reverberations were felt throughout the ancient Near East. The cry heard among the nations is the inverse of v. 2's proclamation: the announcement goes out to the nations that the empire which had dominated them is ended. Rev 18:9-10, 18-19 uses these images for the eschatological fall of Babylon: kings of the earth weeping, crying 'woe, woe for the great city' when they see her smoke rising.

51.1: The oracle against Babylon opens with the cryptic place-name Leb-qamai — a cipher (atbash code, reversing the Hebrew alphabet) for Kasdim (Chaldea/Babylon). The same cryptic substitution is used for "Sheshach" = Babel (25:26; 51:41). The use of coded language may reflect the political danger of openly declaring Babylon's fall while Babylonian power was at its peak, or it may be a literary device that signals the insider knowledge of the prophetic tradition. YHWH stirs up a destroying wind (ruaḥ mashḥit) against Babylon — the wind of cosmic destruction that recalls the creation narrative of Genesis 1.
51.2: The winnowing wind — sent to winnow Babylon and empty its land — extends the agricultural metaphor of separation. Winnowing used wind to separate grain from chaff; the destroyers sent against Babylon will perform the cosmic separation of her people from the land. The emptying of her land on the day of disaster echoes the language of the Exodus plagues, when Egypt was emptied. Babylon is being subjected to an Exodus-like judgment, consistent with the book's repeated use of Exodus typology for the fall of the great oppressor.
51.3: The military instruction — let the archer bend his bow against her — addresses the attacking forces directly. The irony of the command is that the archer is invited to do what seems impossible: attack the most powerful military state of the ancient world. The phrase do not spare her young men, devote her entire army to destruction uses the ḥerem vocabulary (devote to destruction) that the Torah applied to Canaanite cities (Deut 7:2; 20:17). Babylon is to be treated as the object of total sacred destruction — the same fate Israel was commanded to execute on Canaan.
51.4: The result — the slain will fall in the land of the Chaldeans, those thrust through in her streets — describes the defeat of Babylon's army in her own homeland and cities. The Babylonian army, which had devastated the streets of Jerusalem and left bodies throughout Judean cities, will now face the same fate in their own streets. The reversal of fortune is precise: what Babylon did to Israel will be done to Babylon. The covenant principle of measure-for-measure operates across the OAN.
51.5: The declaration that Israel and Judah have not been forsaken by their God interrupts the judgment oracle against Babylon with a covenant affirmation. Despite the exile, YHWH has not dissolved the covenant relationship. The land of the Chaldeans is full of guilt before the Holy One of Israel — the divine title Qedosh Yisraʾel (Holy One of Israel), characteristic of Isaiah, appears here to identify YHWH's holiness as the basis for both Israel's preservation and Babylon's judgment. YHWH's holiness cannot permanently tolerate the oppressor of his people.
51.6: The call to flee from Babylon, everyone save yourselves — do not perish in her punishment — is addressed to the exile community in Babylon. The three warnings to flee (here, v. 45, and v. 50) frame the oracle as genuine advance warning: the community has time to escape before the judgment falls.
